This is the *tidy data* form (Wickham) required by `ggplot2`. ### Step 10: Tidy Reshape (Long ŌåÆ Wide using `pivot_wider`) ```r df_wide <- df_long %>% pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = value) head(df_wide, 3) ``` **Logic:** The reverse operation restores the original wide format. `pivot_wider` is used when feeding a model that expects one-row-per-observation input. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Imputation Strategy Selection | Scenario | Recommended Imputation | Justification | |---|---|---| | < 5% missing, MCAR, numeric | Mean / Median | Negligible bias, simple | | 5ŌĆō15% missing, numeric | KNN imputation or MICE | Preserves variance | | 5ŌĆō15% missing, categorical | Mode imputation | Preserves category distribution | | > 15% missing, single column | Drop the column | Imputation unreliable | | > 30% missing overall | Drop rows or collect more data | Risk of bias too high | | Time series | Forward / backward fill | Respects temporal order | | MNAR (Missing Not At Random) | Model-based or domain expert | Simple imputation is biased | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Outlier Treatment | Method | Formula / Logic | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **Tukey's Fences (IQR)** | $x \notin [Q_1 - 1.5 IQR, Q_3 + 1.5 IQR]$ | Non-parametric, robust | Misses multivariate outliers | | **Z-Score** | $\vert z \vert > 3$ | Simple, parametric | Assumes normality | | **Winsorization** | Cap at 1st / 99th percentile | Keeps sample size | Distorts extremes | | **Isolation Forest** | ML-based | Detects multivariate | Computationally heavier | | **Removal** | Drop rows | Clean data | Loss of information | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Preprocessing Pipeline (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Data Source
CSV Excel JSON DB API] --> B[Import Layer
read_csv read_excel fromJSON] B --> C[Initial Inspection
head str summary dim] C --> D{Data Quality Check} D -->|Missing Detected| E[Handle Missing Values
na.omit mean median MICE] D -->|Duplicates Detected| F[Remove Duplicates
distinct duplicated] D -->|Type Issues| G[Convert Types
as.numeric as.factor as.Date] D -->|Outliers Detected| H[Outlier Treatment
IQR Zscore Winsorize] E --> I[Cleaning Complete] F --> I G --> I H --> I I --> J[Transformation Layer
mutate select filter arrange] J --> K{Rescaling Required?} K -->|Yes Bounded| L[Min-Max Normalization
X minus Xmin over Xmax minus Xmin] K -->|Yes Unbounded| M[Z-Score Standardization
X minus mu over sigma] K -->|No| N[Skip Rescaling] L --> O[Tidy Reshape
pivot_longer pivot_wider] M --> O N --> O O --> P[Analysis Ready Tibble] P --> Q[EDA Modeling Visualization] ``` ## 4.2 The Tidy Data Concept (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Wide Table
Country Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4] --> B[pivot_longer] B --> C[Tidy Long Table
Country Quarter Sales] C --> D[ggplot2 Visualization] C --> E[dplyr Grouping] C --> F[Statistical Modeling] G[Raw Long Table
Country Year Month Value] --> H[pivot_wider] H --> I[Tidy Wide Table
Country Year Jan Feb Mar] I --> J[Matrix Operations] I --> K[Correlation Analysis] ``` ## 4.3 Missing-Data Mechanism Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Data with Missing Values] --> B{Is the missingness random?} B -->|Yes MCAR| C{Quantity of missing} B -->|No MAR| D[Use Model Based Imputation
mice missForest] B -->|No MNAR| E[Domain Expert Required
or Pattern Mixture Models] C -->|Less than 5 percent| F[Simple Imputation
mean median mode] C -->|5 to 15 percent| G[KNN Imputation or MICE] C -->|More than 15 percent| H[Drop Column or Collect Data] F --> I[Validate via Cross Validation] G --> I D --> I H --> I ``` ## 4.4 Block Architecture ŌĆö R Preprocessing Toolchain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Base R
read.csv as.matrix] --> E[Data Object
tibble data.frame] B[readr Package
read_csv read_tsv] --> E C[readxl Package
read_excel read_sheet] --> E D[DBI and odbc
dbConnect dbGetQuery] --> E E --> F[dplyr
filter select mutate summarise] F --> G[tidyr
drop_na fill pivot_longer] G --> H[Clean Tidy Data] H --> I[ggplot2
Visualization] H --> J[stats and caret
Modeling] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** In the exam, if asked to "draw a data preprocessing pipeline," reproduce **Diagram 4.1** in Mermaid or simple boxes-and-arrows form. Examiners award marks for the *flow logic*, not artistic rendering. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] **Differentiate between `read.csv()` (base R) and `read_csv()` (readr package) with respect to speed, output class, and column type inference.** **Model Answer (Expected ~80 words):** | Aspect | `read.csv()` (base) | `read_csv()` (readr) | |---|---|---| | Speed | Slower (Ōēź 2├Ś for large files) | Faster (C++ backend) | | Output Class | `data.frame` | `tibble` | | Type Inference | Coerces strings to factors by default (in R < 4.0) | Smart type guessing; strings stay as character | | Progress Bar | None | Displays progress for large files | | Returns | Row names inferred from first column unless told not to | Never uses row names | **Key Point:** For modern KTU projects, prefer `read_csv()` because it is **non-destructive** (does not silently turn strings into factors) and is **2ŌĆō10├Ś faster**. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] **Explain any two methods of handling missing values in R with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** **Method 1 ŌĆö Deletion:** Use `na.omit(df)` to drop rows containing any `NA`. Suitable when missingness is < 5% and data is Missing Completely At Random (MCAR). ```r df_clean <- na.omit(df) ``` **Method 2 ŌĆö Mean Imputation:** Replace `NA` with the column mean. ```r df$age[is.na(df$age)] <- mean(df$age, na.rm = TRUE) ``` **Method 3 ŌĆö Median Imputation:** For skewed numeric data. ```r df$income[is.na(df$income)] <- median(df$income, na.rm = TRUE) ``` **Method 4 ŌĆö Mode Imputation:** For categorical data. ```r getmode <- function(v) { uniqv <- unique(v[!is.na(v)]) uniqv[which.max(tabulate(match(v, uniqv)))] } df$gender[is.na(df$gender)] <- getmode(df$gender) ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Computing `mean(df$age)` *without* `na.rm = TRUE` returns `NA`. Always use `na.rm = TRUE` when computing summary statistics on data containing missing values. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question carries internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. ### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the various stages involved in data preprocessing. List the R functions used at each stage. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following data frame in R: ```r emp <- data.frame( name = c("Anu", "Bala", "Anu", "David", "Eva"), salary = c(50000, NA, 50000, 72000, NA), dept = c("HR", "IT", "HR", "Finance", "IT") ) ``` Write R code to: 1. Detect and remove duplicate rows. 2. Replace the missing salaries with the mean salary of the respective department. 3. Add a new column `salary_norm` where each salary is min-max normalized to the range [0, 1]. **Model Solution for (a):** The data preprocessing pipeline consists of six stages: | Stage | Purpose | Key R Functions | |---|---|---| | 1. **Data Import** | Bring data into R | `read.csv()`, `read_csv()`, `read_excel()` | | 2. **Data Inspection** | Understand shape, types, summary | `head()`, `str()`, `summary()`, `glimpse()` | | 3. **Data Cleaning** | Handle NA, duplicates, outliers | `is.na()`, `na.omit()`, `duplicated()`, `distinct()` | | 4. **Data Transformation** | Mutate, filter, derive | `mutate()`, `filter()`, `select()`, `group_by()` | | 5. **Data Rescaling** | Normalize / standardize | `scale()`, `rescale()`, custom formulas | | 6. **Data Reshaping** | Tidy data layout | `pivot_longer()`, `pivot_wider()`, `gather()`, `spread()` | (Refer to **Diagram 4.1** for the visual flowchart.) **Model Solution for (b):** ```r library(dplyr) # ---- Step 1: Detect and remove duplicates ---- cat("Duplicates before:", sum(duplicated(emp)), "\n") emp <- emp %>% distinct() cat("Rows after dedup :", nrow(emp), "\n") # ---- Step 2: Department-wise mean imputation ---- emp <- emp %>% group_by(dept) %>% mutate(salary = ifelse(is.na(salary), mean(salary, na.rm = TRUE), salary)) %>% ungroup() # ---- Step 3: Min-Max normalization ---- sm <- min(emp$salary) sM <- max(emp$salary) emp <- emp %>% mutate(salary_norm = (salary - sm) / (sM - sm)) print(emp) ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Duplicates before: 1 Rows after dedup : 4 # A tibble: 4 x 4 name salary dept salary_norm 1 Anu 50000 HR 0 2 David 72000 Finance 1 3 Eva 50000 IT 0 4 Bala 50000 IT 0 ``` **Step-by-step logic:** 1. `distinct()` from `dplyr` removes exact duplicate rows (here, the second "Anu" row). 2. `group_by(dept)` ensures the mean is computed *per department*. For the "IT" department, mean of available salaries is `50000` (only Bala's record before Eva's NA is filled). 3. Min-max formula applied: $(x - 50000) / (72000 - 50000) = (x - 50000) / 22000$. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** > - Students often forget to call `ungroup()` after `group_by()`, causing all subsequent operations to remain grouped ŌĆö a 1-mark deduction. > - Computing the global mean instead of the **per-department** mean loses 2 marks. The question explicitly says *"of the respective department."* > - Forgetting `na.rm = TRUE` inside `mean()` returns `NA` and the entire column becomes `NA` ŌĆö total 7-mark loss on this sub-part. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between **Min-Max Normalization** and **Z-Score Standardization**. Derive both formulas and state one scenario where each is preferred. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the `iris` dataset in R. Write a complete R script to: 1. Compute the mean, median, and standard deviation of `Sepal.Length` for each `Species`. 2. Standardize `Sepal.Length` and `Petal.Length` using z-score. 3. Identify outliers in `Sepal.Width` using the IQR method and replace them with the median. **Model Solution for (a):** **Min-Max Normalization** rescales features to a fixed interval, typically $[0, 1]$: $$ X_{\text{norm}} = \frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}} $$ **Derivation (intuition):** Subtract the minimum to shift the lowest value to 0, then divide by the range $(X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ so the highest value becomes 1. **Properties:** - Output is **bounded** in $[0, 1]$. - Preserves the **shape** of the original distribution. - **Sensitive to outliers** (a single extreme value compresses all others). **Preferred scenario:** Image processing (pixel intensities Ōłł [0, 255] ŌåÆ [0, 1]) and **neural network inputs** that expect bounded activations. **Z-Score Standardization** rescales to mean 0 and standard deviation 1: $$ Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} $$ **Derivation (intuition):** Subtract the population mean $\mu$ to center the data, then divide by the standard deviation $\sigma$ to give it unit variance. **Properties:** - Output is **unbounded** (can be negative or > 1). - Assumes (or is robust to) approximate normality. - **Less sensitive to outliers** (uses mean & SD which are influenced, but the relative scaling remains meaningful). **Preferred scenario:** **PCA, LDA, SVM, Logistic Regression with regularization** ŌĆö algorithms that assume zero-centered, unit-variance features. **Comparison Table:** | Criterion | Min-Max | Z-Score | |---|---|---| | Output range | $[0, 1]$ (bounded) | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | | Outlier sensitivity | High | Moderate | | Preserves zeros | Yes | No | | Requires | Min, Max | Mean, SD | | Typical use | Neural networks, image data | PCA, SVM, regularized models | **Model Solution for (b):** ```r library(dplyr) data(iris) # ---- Step 1: Mean, Median, SD per Species ---- stats_summary <- iris %>% group_by(Species) %>% summarise( mean_SL = mean(Sepal.Length), median_SL = median(Sepal.Length), sd_SL = sd(Sepal.Length) ) print(stats_summary) # ---- Step 2: Z-score standardization ---- iris <- iris %>% mutate( Sepal.Length_z = (Sepal.Length - mean(Sepal.Length)) / sd(Sepal.Length), Petal.Length_z = (Petal.Length - mean(Petal.Length)) / sd(Petal.Length) ) # Verify (should be 0 and 1) cat("Mean of Sepal.Length_z:", mean(iris$Sepal.Length_z), "\n") cat("SD of Sepal.Length_z :", sd(iris$Sepal.Length_z), "\n") # ---- Step 3: IQR-based outlier detection & median replacement ---- Q1 <- quantile(iris$Sepal.Width, 0.25) Q3 <- quantile(iris$Sepal.Width, 0.75) IQR <- Q3 - Q1 lower <- Q1 - 1.5 * IQR upper <- Q3 + 1.5 * IQR cat("Outlier bounds:", lower, "to", upper, "\n") # Identify outlier rows out_mask <- iris$Sepal.Width < lower | iris$Sepal.Width > upper cat("Outliers found:", sum(out_mask), "\n") # Replace with median med_sw <- median(iris$Sepal.Width) iris$Sepal.Width[out_mask] <- med_sw ``` **Expected Numeric Results:** `stats_summary`: ``` Species mean_SL median_SL sd_SL 1 setosa 5.01 5 0.352 2 versicolor 5.94 5.9 0.516 3 virginica 6.59 6.5 0.636 ``` Outlier bounds for `Sepal.Width`: - $Q_1 = 2.8$, $Q_3 = 3.3$, $IQR = 0.5$ - Lower = $2.8 - 0.75 = 2.05$ - Upper = $3.3 + 0.75 = 4.05$ Iris contains 4 observations with `Sepal.Width = 2.0` (just below 2.05), which are replaced by the median = `3.0`. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** > - Forgetting `na.rm` in `mean()` / `sd()` for datasets with NAs will silently return `NA` and break the script. > - Not grouping by `Species` in Step 1 loses 2 marks ŌĆö the question explicitly says *"for each Species."* > - Replacing outliers with the **mean** instead of the **median** is a common error. Since outliers skew the mean, the median is the more robust choice. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conceptual error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU exam prep):** - Ō£ģ **Import functions** are file-format specific: `read_csv` (comma), `read_tsv` (tab), `read_delim` (custom), `read_excel` (xlsx), `fromJSON` (web APIs). - Ō£ģ **Always inspect first:** `head()`, `str()`, `summary()`, `dim()`, `colnames()` ŌĆö never clean blindly. - Ō£ģ **Missing values:** `is.na()` for detection; `na.omit()` for deletion; `mean(..., na.rm=TRUE)` for imputation. R uses `NA` (logical), not `NULL` or `NaN`. - Ō£ģ **Duplicates:** `duplicated()` returns a logical vector; `distinct()` from `dplyr` is the cleanest way to drop them. - Ō£ģ **Type conversion:** `as.numeric()`, `as.character()`, `as.factor()`, `as.Date()`. Use `mutate()` inside `dplyr` to apply column-wise. - Ō£ģ **String cleaning:** `stringr` package ŌĆö `str_trim()`, `str_to_lower()`, `str_replace()`, `str_detect()`. - Ō£ģ **Two scaling formulas to memorize:** * Min-Max: $X_{\text{norm}} = (X - X_{\min}) / (X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ * Z-Score: $Z = (X - \mu) / \sigma$ - Ō£ģ **Outlier detection (Tukey):** $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR, \; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$ is the valid range. - Ō£ģ **Tidy data principle:** One variable per column, one observation per row, one value per cell. - Ō£ģ **Reshape verbs (tidyr):** `pivot_longer()` for wideŌåÆlong; `pivot_wider()` for longŌåÆwide. (Older: `gather()` / `spread()`.) - Ō£ģ **Always include `na.rm = TRUE`** when computing summary statistics on data that may contain NAs. - Ō£ģ **Most common examiner traps:** (1) Using `mean()` without `na.rm`, (2) forgetting to `ungroup()` after `group_by()`, (3) confusing `NA` with `NaN` and `NULL`, (4) computing global mean instead of group-wise mean. - Ō£ģ **KDD Pipeline Position:** Preprocessing sits between **Data Selection** and **Data Mining** in the KDD process; never skip it. - Ō£ģ **Package load order:** For exam scripts, always start with `library(dplyr); library(tidyr); library(readr); library(stringr)` to ensure function availability.

topics.title: Data Import and Export - Reading and Writing Data from/to Text Files, Excel, and Other Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT746 - PROGRAMMING IN R
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Import and Export - Reading and Writing Data from/to Text Files, Excel, and Other Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Import and Export** in R refers to the systematic process of ingesting external data into the R working environment (import) and persisting in-memory R objects to external storage media or other software ecosystems (export). In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this encompasses the complete I/O (Input/Output) pipeline for heterogeneous data sources including delimited text files (`.csv`, `.tsv`, `.txt`), Microsoft Excel spreadsheets (`.xlsx`, `.xls`), proprietary statistical software formats (SPSS `.sav`, SAS `.sas7bdat`, Stata `.dta`, MATLAB `.mat`), and semi-structured formats (JSON, XML).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)**
> Data Import and Export operations in R constitute the foundational bridge between persistent storage and volatile memory. They rely on the **I/O paradigm** of base R and supplementary contributed packages indexed in the **CRAN (Comprehensive R Archive Network)** repository.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine R as a **highly organized restaurant kitchen**.

- The **chef (R engine)** can only cook with ingredients that are physically inside the kitchen (RAM/working memory).
- **Data Import** is like the **delivery boy bringing raw ingredients** (vegetables, meat, spices) from various suppliers (text files, Excel sheets, databases) into the kitchen. Each supplier uses different packaging ŌĆö some in cardboard boxes (CSV), some in glass jars (Excel), some in vacuum-sealed pouches (SPSS) ŌĆö so the delivery boy needs the right tools (functions like `read.csv()`, `read_excel()`, `read_sav()`) to unpack them properly.
- **Data Export** is the **reverse trip** ŌĆö packing freshly prepared dishes back into containers so customers (other software, analysts, archival systems) can take them home.

**Without import**, your R session is an empty kitchen ŌĆö no data means no analysis.
**Without export**, your hard-earned analysis vanishes the moment you close R.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operational Metrics in R I/O (Always in Bold)**
> - **Default Encoding**: **UTF-8** (Unicode Transformation Format, 8-bit) ŌĆö the modern global character standard.
> - **Standard Delimiter for CSV (Comma-Separated Values)**: **`,` (comma)** ŌĆö RFC 4180 compliant.
> - **Decimal Separator Convention**: **`.` (period)** in `read.csv()`; **`,` (comma)** in `read.csv2()` for European locales.
> - **Header Row Default**: **First row treated as column names** when `header = TRUE`.
> - **Missing Value Sentinel**: **`NA` (Not Available)** in R, mapped from blank cells, `"NA"` strings, or user-specified tokens via the `na.strings` argument.
> - **Line Ending Convention**: **`\n` (LF)** on Unix/macOS, **`\r\n` (CRLF)** on Windows ŌĆö handled transparently by R's I/O layer.

## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Directions

| Direction | R Terminology | Real-World Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Disk ŌåÆ Memory** | **Read / Import** | Opening a file |
| **Memory ŌåÆ Disk** | **Write / Export** | Saving a file |

> [!NOTE]
> In R documentation and CRAN package manuals, the verbs **read** and **write** are the canonical terms. Functions almost universally follow the `read.*()` and `write.*()` naming convention (e.g., `read.csv`, `write.xlsx`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The I/O Architecture in R

R's data import/export ecosystem operates in **three concentric layers**:

### Layer 1: Base R (Built-in, No Installation Required)
- Text file readers: `read.table()`, `read.csv()`, `read.csv2()`, `read.delim()`, `read.delim2()`, `scan()`, `readLines()`
- Text file writers: `write.table()`, `write.csv()`, `write.csv2()`, `writeLines()`, `cat()`
- R-native serialization: `save()`, `load()`, `saveRDS()`, `readRDS()`, `dput()`, `dget()`

### Layer 2: Tidyverse / Hadleyverse (Contributed, High-Level Abstractions)
- `readr` package: `read_csv()`, `read_tsv()`, `read_delim()`, `read_fwf()`, `read_log()` ŌåÆ returns **tibble** (modern data frame).
- `readxl` package: `read_excel()`, `read_xlsx()`, `read_xls()` ŌåÆ reads Excel without Java dependency.
- `writexl` package: `write_xlsx()` ŌåÆ writes Excel without Java dependency.
- `haven` package: `read_sav()` (SPSS), `read_dta()` (Stata), `read_sas()` (SAS), `read_xpt()` (XPORT).

### Layer 3: Specialized / Domain-Specific
- `xlsx` package: `read.xlsx()`, `write.xlsx()` (requires Java).
- `XLConnect` package: Full Excel automation via Apache POI.
- `jsonlite` package: `fromJSON()`, `toJSON()`.
- `xml2` / `XML` packages: `read_xml()`, `xmlParse()`.
- `foreign` package: Legacy readers for SPSS, Stata, Minitab, S-PLUS, Octave.
- `R.matlab` package: `readMat()`, `writeMat()`.
- `DBI` + `odbc`/`RMySQL`/`RPostgres`/`RSQLite`: Database connectivity.
- `data.table` package: `fread()` (ultra-fast text reader), `fwrite()`.
- `httr` / `curl` packages: Web APIs and HTTP-based I/O.

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö How R Reads a Text File (Step-by-Step)

When you invoke `read.table("data.txt", header = TRUE, sep = "\t")`, R executes this pipeline:

1. **Path Resolution**: The file path string is resolved against the current working directory (`getwd()`).
2. **Connection Opening**: A **file connection** (object of class `connection`) is opened in read mode (`"r"`).
3. **Header Detection**: If `header = TRUE`, the first line is consumed and split by the separator into column names.
4. **Type Inference**: R scans a configurable number of rows (`nrows` for `read.table`; full scan for `readr`) and assigns types: `integer`, `double`, `character`, `logical`, or `complex`.
5. **Data Streaming**: Each subsequent line is tokenized using the `sep` delimiter.
6. **Missing Value Mapping**: Tokens matching `na.strings` are converted to `NA`.
7. **Comment Stripping**: Lines beginning with the `comment.char` (default `"#"`) are ignored.
8. **Data Frame Construction**: A `data.frame` object is assembled in memory with the inferred column types.
9. **Connection Closure**: The file connection is automatically closed (or left open if `file()` was used manually).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Function Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table for KTU University Exam ŌĆö Memorize These**

| Function | Package | File Type | Direction | Key Arguments |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `read.table()` | base | Text (general) | Read | `file`, `header`, `sep`, `dec`, `na.strings` |
| `read.csv()` | base | CSV (comma) | Read | `header = TRUE`, `sep = ","`, `dec = "."` |
| `read.csv2()` | base | CSV (semicolon) | Read | `header = TRUE`, `sep = ";"`, `dec = ","` |
| `read.delim()` | base | TSV (tab) | Read | `header = TRUE`, `sep = "\t"` |
| `read.fwf()` | base | Fixed-width | Read | `widths` (vector of column widths) |
| `scan()` | base | Text (raw) | Read | `what`, `sep`, `nlines` |
| `readLines()` | base | Text (line-by-line) | Read | `n` (max lines), `encoding` |
| `write.table()` | base | Text (general) | Write | `x`, `file`, `sep`, `row.names`, `quote` |
| `write.csv()` | base | CSV (comma) | Write | `x`, `file`, `row.names = FALSE` |
| `read_csv()` | readr | CSV (comma) | Read | `file`, `col_types`, `na` |
| `read_tsv()` | readr | TSV (tab) | Read | `file`, `col_types` |
| `read_excel()` | readxl | Excel (.xls/.xlsx) | Read | `path`, `sheet`, `range`, `col_types` |
| `read.xlsx()` | xlsx | Excel (.xlsx) | Read | `file`, `sheetIndex`, `header` |
| `write.xlsx()` | xlsx | Excel (.xlsx) | Write | `x`, `file`, `sheetName` |
| `write_xlsx()` | writexl | Excel (.xlsx) | Write | `x`, `path` |
| `read_sav()` | haven | SPSS (.sav) | Read | `file`, `user_na` |
| `read_dta()` | haven | Stata (.dta) | Read | `file`, `encoding` |
| `read_sas()` | haven | SAS (.sas7bdat) | Read | `path`, `catalog` |
| `read_spss()` | foreign | SPSS legacy | Read | `file`, `to.data.frame` |
| `read.dta()` | foreign | Stata legacy | Read | `file` |
| `read.xport()` | foreign | SAS XPORT | Read | `file` |
| `readMat()` | R.matlab | MATLAB (.mat) | Read | `file` |
| `fromJSON()` | jsonlite | JSON | Read | `txt`, `simplifyVector` |
| `toJSON()` | jsonlite | JSON | Write | `x`, `pretty`, `auto_unbox` |
| `read_xml()` | xml2 | XML/HTML | Read | `x` |
| `fread()` | data.table | Text/CSV (fast) | Read | `file`, `sep`, `header` |
| `fwrite()` | data.table | Text/CSV (fast) | Write | `x`, `file`, `sep` |
| `saveRDS()` | base | R binary | Write | `object`, `file`, `compress` |
| `readRDS()` | base | R binary | Read | `file` |
| `save()` | base | R workspace | Write | `..., file` |
| `load()` | base | R workspace | Read | `file` |
| `download.file()` | utils | URL ŌåÆ disk | Download | `url`, `destfile`, `method` |

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real-World Engineering

| Domain | Practical Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Data Science / ML** | Load CSV training data, export predictions to Excel for stakeholders. |
| **Bioinformatics** | Read gene expression matrices from SAS/SPSS outputs. |
| **IoT / Sensor Networks** | Stream time-series logs via `scan()` or `readLines()`. |
| **Finance** | Connect to SQL databases (`DBI`), pull tick data, export reports to Excel. |
| **Academic Research** | Interoperate with Stata/SPSS collaborators via `haven`. |
| **Web Scraping Pipelines** | `download.file()` + `fromJSON()` for API-driven ETL (Extract-Transform-Load). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1: Reading and Writing CSV Files (Base R)

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 1: Create a working example data frame
# ============================================================
student_data <- data.frame(
  roll_no  = c(101, 102, 103, 104, 105),
  name     = c("Ananya", "Balu", "Chitra", "Deepak", "Esha"),
  marks    = c(85.5, 92.0, 78.5, 88.0, 95.5),
  passed   = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Display the data frame in the console
print(student_data)
```

**Console Output:**
```
  roll_no    name marks passed
1     101  Ananya  85.5   TRUE
2     102    Balu  92.0   TRUE
3     103  Chitra  78.5   TRUE
4     104  Deepak  88.0   TRUE
5     105    Esha  95.5   TRUE
```

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 2: WRITE to a CSV file on disk
# ============================================================
# row.names = FALSE  -> prevents R from writing the row numbers as a column
# quote = TRUE       -> wraps character strings in double quotes
write.csv(student_data,
          file      = "students_export.csv",
          row.names = FALSE,
          quote     = TRUE)
```

**Resulting file content (`students_export.csv`):**
```
"roll_no","name","marks","passed"
101,"Ananya",85.5,TRUE
102,"Balu",92,TRUE
103,"Chitra",78.5,TRUE
104,"Deepak",88,TRUE
105,"Esha",95.5,TRUE
```

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 3: READ the CSV file back into R
# ============================================================
imported_data <- read.csv(file      = "students_export.csv",
                          header    = TRUE,
                          stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Verify the round-trip integrity
str(imported_data)
```

**Console Output:**
```
'data.frame':    5 obs. of  4 variables:
 $ roll_no: int  101 102 103 104 105
 $ name   : chr  "Ananya" "Balu" "Chitra" "Deepak" "Esha"
 $ marks  : num  85.5 92 78.5 88 95.5
 $ passed : logi  TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Transition Explanation:** The `write.csv()` call serializes each column according to its R type (integer ŌåÆ bare number, numeric ŌåÆ decimal, character ŌåÆ quoted string, logical ŌåÆ `TRUE`/`FALSE`). On read-back, `read.csv()` performs **automatic type coercion** in reverse, recovering the original `data.frame` structure.

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2: Handling Delimited Text with `read.table()` and `scan()`

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 1: Write a custom-delimited text file
# ============================================================
# Use pipe (|) as delimiter, NA token as "missing"
write.table(student_data,
            file       = "students_pipe.txt",
            sep        = "|",
            row.names  = FALSE,
            na         = "missing",
            quote      = FALSE)
```

**Resulting file content (`students_pipe.txt`):**
```
roll_no|name|marks|passed
101|Ananya|85.5|TRUE
102|Balu|92|TRUE
103|Chitra|78.5|TRUE
104|Deepak|88|TRUE
105|Esha|95.5|TRUE
```

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 2: Read with read.table()
# ============================================================
df_pipe <- read.table(file       = "students_pipe.txt",
                      header     = TRUE,
                      sep        = "|",
                      na.strings = "missing",
                      stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

print(df_pipe)
```

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 3: Using scan() for low-level vector reading
# ============================================================
# scan() reads a file into a vector ŌĆö useful for raw numeric/text streams
numeric_vector <- scan(file  = "students_pipe.txt",
                       what  = "",
                       sep   = "\n",
                       skip  = 1)   # skip header row

print(numeric_vector)
```

**Console Output:**
```
[1] "101|Ananya|85.5|TRUE"  "102|Balu|92|TRUE"
[3] "103|Chitra|78.5|TRUE" "104|Deepak|88|TRUE"
[5] "105|Esha|95.5|TRUE"
```

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3: Excel Files via `readxl` and `writexl`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `readxl` and `writexl` packages are **Java-free** alternatives to the older `xlsx` package ŌĆö they install faster and have zero Java dependencies. **Preferred for KTU 2024 lab work.**

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 1: Install and load the required packages
# ============================================================
# install.packages("readxl")   # run once
# install.packages("writexl")  # run once
library(readxl)
library(writexl)

# ============================================================
# STEP 2: WRITE a data frame to an .xlsx file
# ============================================================
write_xlsx(student_data,
           path = "students_workbook.xlsx")

# ============================================================
# STEP 3: READ the .xlsx file back
# ============================================================
# By default, reads the first sheet
imported_excel <- read_excel(path  = "students_workbook.xlsx",
                             sheet = 1)   # or use the sheet name as a string

print(imported_excel)
str(imported_excel)
```

**Console Output:**
```
# A tibble: 5 x 4
  roll_no name    marks passed
    <dbl> <chr>   <dbl> <lgl>
1     101 Ananya   85.5 TRUE
2     102 Balu     92   TRUE
3     103 Chitra   78.5 TRUE
4     104 Deepak   88   TRUE
5     105 Esha     95.5 TRUE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction**: `read_excel()` returns a **tibble** (`tbl_df`), not a base `data.frame`. Both are 2-D tabular structures, but tibbles have enhanced printing and stricter subsetting behavior. You can convert with `as.data.frame()` if needed.

```r
# ============================================================
# STEP 4: Reading a SPECIFIC RANGE of cells (Excel-like addressing)
# ============================================================
# Syntax: "SheetName!A1:C5"
subset_data <- read_excel(path  = "students_workbook.xlsx",
                          range = "Sheet1!A1:B3")
print(subset_data)
```

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4: Interoperability with SPSS, Stata, SAS (via `haven`)

```r
# ============================================================
# SPSS (.sav) ŌĆö using haven
# ============================================================
# install.packages("haven")
library(haven)

# Read SPSS file
# spss_df <- read_sav("survey_results.sav")

# Write to SPSS format
# write_sav(survey_df, "output_for_spss.sav")

# ============================================================
# Stata (.dta) ŌĆö using haven
# ============================================================
# stata_df <- read_dta("panel_data.dta")
# write_dta(panel_df, "output_for_stata.dta")

# ============================================================
# SAS (.sas7bdat) ŌĆö using haven
# ============================================================
# sas_df <- read_sas("clinical_trial.sas7bdat")

# Display label attributes (preserved from original software)
# print(attr(spss_df$variable_name, "label"))
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `haven` over `foreign`?** The `foreign` package (base R companion) is older and **strips variable labels, value labels, and missing-value codes** during import. The `haven` package **preserves these metadata attributes** as special R attributes (`label`, `labels`, `class = "haven_labelled"`), making round-trip fidelity near-perfect.

## 3.5 Walkthrough 5: JSON and Web Data

```r
# ============================================================
# JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) via jsonlite
# ============================================================
# install.packages("jsonlite")
library(jsonlite)

# WRITE a data frame as JSON
json_text <- toJSON(student_data, pretty = TRUE, auto_unbox = TRUE)
cat(json_text, "\n")

# Output:
# {
#   "roll_no": [101, 102, 103, 104, 105],
#   "name": ["Ananya", "Balu", "Chitra", "Deepak", "Esha"],
#   "marks": [85.5, 92, 78.5, 88, 95.5],
#   "passed": [true, true, true, true, true]
# }

# WRITE JSON to file
writeLines(json_text, "students.json")

# READ JSON back
restored <- fromJSON("students.json", simplifyVector = TRUE)
print(restored)
str(restored)
```

## 3.6 Walkthrough 6: R-Native Serialization (`saveRDS` / `readRDS`)

```r
# ============================================================
# saveRDS / readRDS ŌĆö best for single R objects (preserves type exactly)
# ============================================================

# WRITE
saveRDS(student_data, file = "students.rds", compress = "gzip")

# READ
restored_rds <- readRDS(file = "students.rds")
identical(student_data, restored_rds)   # returns TRUE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: `saveRDS()` saves **one** object; `save()` saves **multiple** objects or the **entire workspace**. `save.image()` is shorthand for saving the workspace to `.RData`. The corresponding read for `saveRDS` is `readRDS`; for `save` it is `load`.

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Which Function Should I Use?

| Scenario | Recommended Function | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Small CSV under 1 GB, base R only | `read.csv()` | No extra packages needed. |
| Large CSV (multi-GB), need speed | `data.table::fread()` | **C-optimized**, 10-50├Ś faster. |
| Modern tidyverse pipeline | `readr::read_csv()` | Returns tibble, better diagnostics. |
| Excel file, want to avoid Java | `readxl::read_excel()` | Pure C++, lightweight. |
| Excel file, need formulas/charts | `XLConnect` or `openxlsx` | Full Excel API. |
| SPSS file with value labels | `haven::read_sav()` | Preserves `labelled` class. |
| Legacy SPSS/Stata (pre-2010) | `foreign::read.spss()` | Backward compatibility. |
| Database query | `DBI` + `odbc`/`RMySQL` | SQL-native access. |
| Web API response | `jsonlite::fromJSON()` | Standard REST/JSON parsing. |
| Save R object for later R session | `saveRDS()` / `readRDS()` | Type-stable, compressed. |
| Save entire workspace | `save.image()` / `load()` | Preserves all objects. |
| Stream large file line-by-line | `readLines()` / `writeLines()` | Memory-efficient. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö R Data Import/Export Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Data Sources] --> B{Data Format Detection}
    B -->|Delimited Text| C[Text File Handlers]
    B -->|Spreadsheet| D[Excel Handlers]
    B -->|Statistical SW| E[SAS SPSS Stata Handlers]
    B -->|Structured Web| F[JSON XML Handlers]
    B -->|Database| G[SQL Handlers]
    B -->|MATLAB Binary| H[MAT Handlers]

    C --> C1[read.table / read.csv]
    C --> C2[readr::read_csv]
    C --> C3[data.table::fread]
    C --> C4[scan / readLines]

    D --> D1[readxl::read_excel]
    D --> D2[xlsx::read.xlsx]
    D --> D3[writexl::write_xlsx]
    D --> D4[XLConnect]

    E --> E1[haven::read_sav]
    E --> E2[haven::read_dta]
    E --> E3[haven::read_sas]
    E --> E4[foreign::read.spss]

    F --> F1[jsonlite::fromJSON]
    F --> F2[xml2::read_xml]

    G --> G1[DBI::dbReadTable]
    G --> G2[odbc / RMySQL]

    H --> H1[R.matlab::readMat]

    C1 --> I[(R Working Memory)]
    C2 --> I
    C3 --> I
    C4 --> I
    D1 --> I
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    D4 --> I
    E1 --> I
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    E4 --> I
    F1 --> I
    F2 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    H1 --> I

    I --> J{Export Direction}
    J -->|Text| K[write.table / write.csv]
    J -->|Excel| L[write_xlsx / write.xlsx]
    J -->|Stats SW| M[write_sav / write_dta / write_sas]
    J -->|JSON| N[toJSON]
    J -->|R Native| O[saveRDS / save.image]

    K --> P[Disk Storage Output]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram ŌĆö Round-Trip Read/Write Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Script
    participant R as R Engine
    participant FS as File System
    participant DF as Data Frame in RAM

    U->>R: read.csv("data.csv", header=TRUE)
    R->>FS: Open file connection (mode "r")
    FS-->>R: Stream raw bytes
    R->>R: Parse header, infer column types
    R->>DF: Construct data.frame object
    R-->>U: Return populated data frame

    U->>R: write.csv(df, "output.csv", row.names=FALSE)
    R->>DF: Serialize columns by type
    R->>FS: Open file connection (mode "w")
    R->>FS: Write formatted text stream
    R-->>U: Return invisibly NULL
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree ŌĆö Choosing the Right Reader

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Data File Arrives] --> Q1{File Extension?}
    Q1 -->|.csv| R1[read.csv or read_csv]
    Q1 -->|.tsv or .txt| R2[read.delim or read_tsv]
    Q1 -->|.xlsx or .xls| R3[read_excel]
    Q1 -->|.sav| R4[read_sav from haven]
    Q1 -->|.dta| R5[read_dta from haven]
    Q1 -->|.sas7bdat| R6[read_sas from haven]
    Q1 -->|.json| R7[fromJSON from jsonlite]
    Q1 -->|.mat| R8[readMat from R.matlab]
    Q1 -->|.xml or .html| R9[read_xml from xml2]
    Q1 -->|.rds| R10[readRDS]
    Q1 -->|.RData| R11[load]
    Q1 -->|SQL DB| R12[DBI::dbReadTable]

    R1 --> Q2{Size?}
    Q2 -->|> 1 GB| R1Fast[fread from data.table]
    Q2 -->|< 1 GB| R1Std[read.csv fine]
```
