# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Examples

## Example 1 ŌĆö The Normal Distribution (Continuous Type)

**Problem context:** Suppose the marks in a KTU semester exam follow a Normal distribution with mean $\mu = 60$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 12$. Evaluate all four function types at strategic points.

### Step 1: Compute the density at $x = 72$ using `dnorm()`

R code:
```r
dnorm(72, mean = 60, sd = 12)
```

**Mathematical verification:**

$$
f(72) = \frac{1}{12\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(72-60)^2}{2 \cdot 12^2}\right)
$$

Step-by-step evaluation using $\begin{aligned} \text{calculation chain:} \end{aligned}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator exponent} &: \frac{(72-60)^2}{2 \cdot 12^2} = \frac{144}{288} = 0.5 \\
\text{Exponential term}  &: e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065307 \\
\text{Coefficient}       &: \frac{1}{12\sqrt{2\pi}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 2.506628} \approx 0.0332463 \\
\text{Final value}       &: 0.0332463 \times 0.6065307 \approx \mathbf{0.0201653}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> R will print the result with higher precision (Ōēł `0.02016027`). The slight difference is due to floating-point rounding of $\pi$.

### Step 2: Compute $P(X \leq 70)$ using `pnorm()`

```r
pnorm(70, mean = 60, sd = 12)         # Lower-tail (default)
pnorm(70, mean = 60, sd = 12, lower.tail = FALSE)  # Upper-tail
```

**Mathematical verification:**

$$
P(X \leq 70) = F(70) = \int_{-\infty}^{70} f(t)\, dt = \Phi\!\left(\frac{70-60}{12}\right) = \Phi(0.8333)
$$

Using standard normal tables or `pnorm(0.8333)`:

$$
\Phi(0.8333) \approx 0.7976
$$

So R returns approximately **`0.7976716`** for `pnorm(70, 60, 12)`.

### Step 3: Find the 95th percentile using `qnorm()`

```r
qnorm(0.95, mean = 60, sd = 12)
```

**Mathematical verification:**

$$
F^{-1}(0.95) = \mu + \sigma \cdot \Phi^{-1}(0.95) = 60 + 12 \times 1.6449 \approx 79.74
$$

R returns approximately **`79.73835`**.

### Step 4: Generate 5 random samples using `rnorm()`

```r
set.seed(2024)              # Reproducibility
rnorm(5, mean = 60, sd = 12)
# e.g., 67.31  49.85  63.72  55.21  70.94
```

---

## Example 2 ŌĆö The Binomial Distribution (Discrete Type)

**Problem context:** A KTU lab test has 8 questions, each with independent probability $p = 0.7$ of being answered correctly. Find $P(X = 6)$, $P(X \leq 6)$, the 90th percentile, and a random simulation.

### Step 1: Compute $P(X = 6)$ using `dbinom()`

```r
dbinom(6, size = 8, prob = 0.7)
```

**Mathematical verification:**

$$
P(X=6) = \binom{8}{6} (0.7)^6 (0.3)^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\binom{8}{6} &= \frac{8!}{6!\,2!} = 28 \\
(0.7)^6      &= 0.117649 \\
(0.3)^2      &= 0.09 \\
P(X=6)       &= 28 \times 0.117649 \times 0.09 = \mathbf{0.2966758}
\end{aligned}
$$

R returns approximately **`0.2966758`** Ō£ō

### Step 2: Compute $P(X \leq 6)$ using `pbinom()`

```r
pbinom(6, size = 8, prob = 0.7)
```

**Mathematical verification (summation):**

$$
P(X \leq 6) = \sum_{k=0}^{6} \binom{8}{k}(0.7)^k(0.3)^{8-k}
$$

By complement rule, $P(X \leq 6) = 1 - P(X=7) - P(X=8)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=7) &= 8 \times (0.7)^7 \times 0.3 = 0.1976503 \\
P(X=8) &= (0.7)^8 = 0.0576480 \\
P(X \leq 6) &= 1 - 0.1976503 - 0.0576480 = \mathbf{0.7447017}
\end{aligned}
$$

R returns approximately **`0.7447017`** Ō£ō

### Step 3: Find the smallest $k$ such that $P(X \leq k) \geq 0.90$ using `qbinom()`

```r
qbinom(0.90, size = 8, prob = 0.7)
```

Returns **`7`**, meaning the 90th percentile of $Bin(8, 0.7)$ is 7 questions.

### Step 4: Simulate 1000 students' performance using `rbinom()`

```r
set.seed(7)
samples <- rbinom(1000, size = 8, prob = 0.7)
mean(samples)   # Theoretical mean = np = 8 ├Ś 0.7 = 5.6
var(samples)    # Theoretical variance = np(1-p) = 8 ├Ś 0.7 ├Ś 0.3 = 1.68
```

---

## Example 3 ŌĆö The Poisson Distribution (Discrete Type)

**Problem context:** A network router receives an average of $\lambda = 3$ packets per second. Find (i) the exact probability of receiving exactly 5 packets in 1 second, (ii) the probability of receiving Ōēż 2 packets, and (iii) verify the empirical mean.

### Step 1: Compute $P(X = 5)$ using `dpois()`

```r
dpois(5, lambda = 3)
```

**Mathematical verification:**

$$
P(X=5) = \frac{3^5 \cdot e^{-3}}{5!}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^5       &= 243 \\
e^{-3}    &= 0.04978707 \\
5!        &= 120 \\
P(X=5)    &= \frac{243 \times 0.04978707}{120} = \frac{12.0983}{120} = \mathbf{0.100819}
\end{aligned}
$$

R returns approximately **`0.1008188`** Ō£ō

### Step 2: Compute $P(X \leq 2)$ using `ppois()`

```r
ppois(2, lambda = 3, lower.tail = TRUE)
```

**Mathematical verification (closed-form):**

$$
P(X \leq 2) = e^{-3}\left(\frac{3^0}{0!} + \frac{3^1}{1!} + \frac{3^2}{2!}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= e^{-3}(1 + 3 + 4.5) \\
&= 0.04978707 \times 8.5 \\
&= \mathbf{0.4231901}
\end{aligned}
$$

R returns approximately **`0.4231901`** Ō£ō

### Step 3: Empirical verification with `rpois()`

```r
set.seed(42)
samples <- rpois(100000, lambda = 3)
mean(samples)   # Ōēł 3.00 (theoretical mean = ╬╗)
var(samples)    # Ōēł 3.00 (theoretical variance = ╬╗)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The Poisson distribution has a unique property ŌĆö its mean equals its variance, both equal to $\lambda$. This is a quick validation check in R examinations.

---

## Example 4 ŌĆö The Exponential Distribution (Continuous Type, Memoryless)

**Problem context:** A hardware component has a failure rate of `rate = 0.05` per hour. Find (i) the density at 10 hours, (ii) $P(\text{life} \leq 20)$, and (iii) simulate 1000 lifetimes.

### Step 1: Compute $f(10)$ using `dexp()`

```r
dexp(10, rate = 0.05)
```

**Mathematical verification:**

$$
f(10) = 0.05 \cdot e^{-0.05 \times 10} = 0.05 \cdot e^{-0.5} = 0.05 \times 0.60653 = \mathbf{0.030327}
$$

### Step 2: Compute $P(X \leq 20)$ using `pexp()`

```r
pexp(20, rate = 0.05)
```

**Mathematical verification:**

$$
P(X \leq 20) = 1 - e^{-0.05 \times 20} = 1 - e^{-1} = 1 - 0.3679 = \mathbf{0.6321}
$$

### Step 3: Simulate and validate

```r
set.seed(123)
lifetimes <- rexp(1000, rate = 0.05)
mean(lifetimes)   # Theoretical mean = 1/rate = 20 hours
```

---

## Example 5 ŌĆö Continuous vs Discrete Type Demonstration

```r
# Continuous: returns density (can exceed 1)
dnorm(0, mean = 0, sd = 1)   # 0.3989 (this is height, not probability)

# Discrete: returns exact probability (always in [0, 1])
dbinom(2, size = 5, prob = 0.5)  # 0.3125 (sum of these = 1)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** A density value of `0.3989` from `dnorm(0,0,1)` is **NOT** a probability. It is the height of the bell curve at $x=0$. To obtain a probability for an interval, you must *integrate* (or use `pnorm(lower) - pnorm(upper)`).

---

## Example 6 ŌĆö Type Argument Validation (Bounds Checking)

```r
# INVALID: probability outside [0, 1]
dbinom(3, size = 10, prob = 1.5)   # Returns NaN with warning

# INVALID: negative size
dbinom(3, size = -1, prob = 0.5)   # Returns NaN with warning

# INVALID: non-integer x for discrete distribution
dbinom(2.7, size = 10, prob = 0.5) # R truncates to 2

# INVALID: negative rate for exponential
pexp(1, rate = -1)                # Returns NaN with warning
```

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The dpqr Function Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Common R Distribution] --> opChoice{Select Operation Prefix}
    opChoice --> opD[Function d type]
    opChoice --> opP[Function p type]
    opChoice --> opQ[Function q type]
    opChoice --> opR[Function r type]
    opD --> retD[Returns Density f of x or Mass P of X eq x]
    opP --> retP[Returns CDF F of q eq P of X leq q]
    opQ --> retQ[Returns Quantile F inverse of p]
    opR --> retR[Returns Vector of n iid Samples]
    retD --> argShared[Type Arguments define shape]
    retP --> argShared
    retQ --> argShared
    retR --> argShared
    root --> distChoice{Select Distribution Type}
    distChoice --> contType[Continuous Type]
    distChoice --> discType[Discrete Type]
    contType --> contList[norm unif exp t f chisq gamma beta weibull lnorm cauchy logis]
    discType --> discList[binom pois geom hyper nbinom wilcox]
    contType --> contArgs[Args mean sd rate shape df etc]
    discType --> discArgs[Args size prob lambda etc]
```

## 4.2 Distribution Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start: What type of data?] --> q1{Outcome Type?}
    q1 --> contData[Continuous Real Valued]
    q1 --> discData[Discrete Count or Integer]
    contData --> q2{Is data bounded?}
    contData --> q3{Symmetric and bell shaped?}
    q3 --> yesNorm[Use norm: mean and sd]
    q3 --> noNorm{Heavy tails?}
    noNorm --> yesT[Use t: df]
    noNorm --> noT{Skewed positive?}
    noT --> yesExp[Use exp: rate]
    noT --> noExp{General positive skew?}
    noExp --> yesGamma[Use gamma: shape and rate]
    q2 --> yesBounded{Finite interval?}
    yesBounded --> yesUnif[Use unif: min and max]
    yesBounded --> noUnif{Interval 0 to 1?}
    noUnif --> yesBeta[Use beta: shape1 and shape2]
    discData --> q4{Fixed number of trials?}
    q4 --> yesTrials{Success or failure each trial?}
    yesTrials --> yesBinom[Use binom: size and prob]
    q4 --> noTrials{Rate of events per interval?}
    noTrials --> yesPois[Use pois: lambda]
    noTrials --> noPois{Trials until r successes?}
    noPois --> yesNbinom[Use nbinom: size and prob]
```

## 4.3 Mapping of Type Arguments to Distribution Properties

```mermaid
graph LR
    argMean[Arg mean] --> prop1[Location Parameter]
    argSD[Arg sd] --> prop2[Scale Parameter]
    argRate[Arg rate] --> prop3[Decay or Arrival Rate]
    argSize[Arg size] --> prop4[Number of Trials]
    argProb[Arg prob] --> prop5[Success Probability]
    argLambda[Arg lambda] --> prop6[Mean Event Count]
    argDF[Arg df] --> prop7[Degrees of Freedom]
    argShape[Arg shape] --> prop8[Skewness Control]
    prop1 --> ex1[norm lnorm logis cauchy]
    prop2 --> ex2[norm exp gamma weibull]
    prop4 --> ex3[binom nbinom hyper]
    prop5 --> ex3
    prop6 --> ex4[pois]
    prop7 --> ex5[t f chisq]
```

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between the prefixes `d` and `p` in R distribution functions. Using the Normal distribution $N(50, 10)$ as an example, explain what each returns and how their interpretations differ.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `dnorm(x, mean, sd)` | `pnorm(q, mean, sd)` |
|:-------|:---------------------|:---------------------|
| Full name | Probability **Density** Function | **Cumulative** Distribution Function |
| Mathematical form | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $F(q) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{q} f(t)\,dt$ |
| Returns | Height of the PDF at $x$ (can be $> 1$) | Cumulative probability $P(X \leq q)$ (in $[0,1]$) |

**Worked numerical illustration for $N(50, 10)$:**
- `dnorm(60, 50, 10)` returns the height at $x=60$, which is $\approx 0.0242$ (a *density*).
- `pnorm(60, 50, 10)` returns the area under the curve up to $x=60$, which is $\approx 0.8413$ (a *probability*).

The crucial point is that `dnorm` gives the y-axis value, while `pnorm` gives the integral of `dnorm` up to that point. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** State the four prefixes `d`, `p`, `q`, `r` used in R distribution functions. For each prefix, state the mathematical function it computes and the type of its primary argument.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Prefix | Function | Mathematical Operation | Primary Argument Type |
|:------:|:---------|:----------------------|:----------------------|
| `d` | `d<dist>(x, ...)` | Density $f(x)$ or Mass $P(X=x)$ | Numeric (`x` Ōłł support) |
| `p` | `p<dist>(q, ...)` | CDF: $F(q) = P(X \leq q)$ | Numeric `q` (real) |
| `q` | `q<dist>(p, ...)` | Quantile: $F^{-1}(p)$ | Probability $p \in [0, 1]$ |
| `r` | `r<dist>(n, ...)` | Random sample generation | Integer `n` (count) |

**(3 marks)**

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With suitable syntax and mathematical justification, explain the four prefixes `d`, `p`, `q`, `r` in R using the **Normal distribution** $N(100, 15)$ as a running example. For each prefix, write the R code, the mathematical formula, and one engineering interpretation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A KTU online examination portal logs an average of $\lambda = 5$ login attempts per minute. Using R functions for the Poisson distribution, compute:
- (i) The exact probability of **6** login attempts in a randomly chosen minute.
- (ii) The probability of **at most 3** login attempts in a minute.
- (iii) The number $k$ such that $P(X \leq k) = 0.95$.
- (iv) Generate **10,000** simulated samples and verify that the sample mean $\approx \lambda$.

Show all R code and the corresponding hand calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution A:**

### Part (a) ŌĆö Explanation of the Four Prefixes **(7 marks)**

For the Normal distribution $N(\mu = 100, \sigma = 15)$:

**1. `dnorm(x, mean, sd)` ŌĆö Probability Density Function (1.75 marks)**

Mathematical formula:
$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

R code:
```r
dnorm(100, mean = 100, sd = 15)  # Density at the mean
# Returns: 0.026596 (peak height of the bell curve)
```

**Engineering interpretation:** At the mean $x = 100$, the bell curve attains its peak height of $\approx 0.0266$. This is the maximum likelihood estimator for the normal density.

**2. `pnorm(q, mean, sd)` ŌĆö Cumulative Distribution Function (1.75 marks)**

Mathematical formula:
$$
F(q) = P(X \leq q) = \int_{-\infty}^{q} f(t)\,dt = \Phi\!\left(\frac{q - \mu}{\sigma}\right)
$$

R code:
```r
pnorm(115, mean = 100, sd = 15)
# Returns: 0.8413447 (Ōēł 84.13% of values fall below 115)
```

**Engineering interpretation:** In a manufacturing process where the dimension $X \sim N(100, 15)$, **84.13%** of produced items have a dimension less than or equal to 115 units ŌĆö directly used for tolerance compliance analysis.

**3. `qnorm(p, mean, sd)` ŌĆö Quantile Function (1.75 marks)**

Mathematical formula:
$$
Q(p) = F^{-1}(p) = \mu + \sigma \cdot \Phi^{-1}(p)
$$

R code:
```r
qnorm(0.975, mean = 100, sd = 15)
# Returns: 129.3954
```

**Engineering interpretation:** The 97.5th percentile is $129.4$ units. In a 95% two-sided specification, the upper control limit is $Q(0.975) = 129.4$ and the lower limit is $Q(0.025) = 70.6$.

**4. `rnorm(n, mean, sd)` ŌĆö Random Variate Generation (1.75 marks)**

R code:
```r
set.seed(42)
samples <- rnorm(1000, mean = 100, sd = 15)
mean(samples)  # Ōēł 100
sd(samples)    # Ōēł 15
```

**Engineering interpretation:** Used extensively in **Monte Carlo simulations**, e.g., simulating 1000 sensor readings with Gaussian noise to test control system robustness.

---

### Part (b) ŌĆö Poisson Distribution Application **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $\lambda = 5$ login attempts per minute.

**(i) Probability of exactly 6 attempts ŌĆö `dpois(6, lambda = 5)` (2 marks)**

Hand calculation:
$$
P(X = 6) = \frac{5^6 \cdot e^{-5}}{6!}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^6         &= 15625 \\
e^{-5}      &= 0.0067379 \\
6!          &= 720 \\
P(X = 6)    &= \frac{15625 \times 0.0067379}{720} = \frac{105.279}{720} \approx \mathbf{0.1462228}
\end{aligned}
$$

```r
dpois(6, lambda = 5)
# Returns: 0.1462228
```

**(ii) Probability of at most 3 attempts ŌĆö `ppois(3, lambda = 5)` (2 marks)**

Hand calculation:
$$
P(X \leq 3) = \sum_{k=0}^{3} \frac{5^k e^{-5}}{k!}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= e^{-5}                          = 0.006738 \\
P(X=1) &= 5 e^{-5}                        = 0.033690 \\
P(X=2) &= \frac{25 e^{-5}}{2}             = 0.084224 \\
P(X=3) &= \frac{125 e^{-5}}{6}            = 0.140374 \\
\text{Sum}                                  &= \mathbf{0.265026}
\end{aligned}
$$

```r
ppois(3, lambda = 5)
# Returns: 0.2650259
```

**(iii) Quantile at 0.95 ŌĆö `qpois(0.95, lambda = 5)` (1.5 marks)**

This is the smallest $k$ such that $P(X \leq k) \geq 0.95$.

```r
qpois(0.95, lambda = 5)
# Returns: 8
```

**Interpretation:** 95% of one-minute windows have $\leq 8$ login attempts. The server should be sized to handle up to 8 attempts/minute with 95% confidence.

**(iv) Simulation verification ŌĆö `rpois(10000, lambda = 5)` (1.5 marks)**

```r
set.seed(2024)
samples <- rpois(10000, lambda = 5)
mean(samples)   # Ōēł 5.00 (theoretical mean = ╬╗)
var(samples)    # Ōēł 5.00 (theoretical variance = ╬╗)
```

**Conclusion:** The empirical mean and variance both converge to $\lambda = 5$, confirming the Poisson property $E[X] = \text{Var}(X) = \lambda$. Ō£ō

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** List and compare any **four discrete distributions** available in R, including their `R` name, the **type arguments** they require, default values, and one real-world scenario where each is applied. Construct a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quality-control engineer inspects batches of 50 electronic components. Historical data shows the defect probability is $p = 0.04$ per component. Using R:
- (i) Compute $P(X = 2)$ defects in a batch.
- (ii) Compute $P(X \leq 1)$ defect (i.e., at most one defect).
- (iii) Find the 99th percentile of the defect count distribution.
- (iv) Approximate the distribution using `ppois()` with $\lambda = np$ and discuss the validity of the approximation.

Show R code and the hand-calculated values. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution B:**

### Part (a) ŌĆö Comparison of Four Discrete Distributions **(7 marks)**

| Distribution | R Name | Type Argument(s) | Default | Real-World Application |
|:-------------|:------:|:----------------:|:-------:|:-----------------------|
| **Binomial** | `binom` | `size`, `prob` | ŌĆö | Quality control: number of defective items in a fixed-size batch |
| **Poisson** | `pois` | `lambda` | ŌĆö | Traffic engineering: vehicles arriving at a signal per minute |
| **Geometric** | `geom` | `prob` | ŌĆö | Reliability: number of trials until the first success (failure) |
| **Hypergeometric** | `hyper` | `m`, `n`, `k` | ŌĆö | Sampling inspection: draws from a finite population without replacement |
| **Negative Binomial** | `nbinom` | `size`, `prob` | ŌĆö | Over-dispersed count data: number of failures before $r$ successes |

*(Any four of the above are valid; marks awarded for completeness, correctness of arguments, and application relevance.)* **(7 marks)**

---

### Part (b) ŌĆö Binomial Probability Computations **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $n = 50$ components, $p = 0.04$ defect probability per item, so $X \sim Bin(50, 0.04)$.

**(i) Probability of exactly 2 defects ŌĆö `dbinom(2, size = 50, prob = 0.04)` (2 marks)**

Hand calculation:
$$
P(X = 2) = \binom{50}{2} (0.04)^2 (0.96)^{48}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\binom{50}{2}  &= \frac{50 \times 49}{2} = 1225 \\
(0.04)^2       &= 0.0016 \\
(0.96)^{48}    &\approx 0.14204 \\
P(X=2)         &= 1225 \times 0.0016 \times 0.14204 = \mathbf{0.27827}
\end{aligned}
$$

```r
dbinom(2, size = 50, prob = 0.04)
# Returns: 0.2782643
```

**(ii) Probability of at most 1 defect ŌĆö `pbinom(1, size = 50, prob = 0.04)` (2 marks)**

Hand calculation:
$$
P(X \leq 1) = P(X=0) + P(X=1)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= (0.96)^{50}                     = 0.1299 \\
P(X=1) &= 50 \times 0.04 \times (0.96)^{49} = 0.2706 \\
P(X \leq 1) &= 0.1299 + 0.2706 = \mathbf{0.4005}
\end{aligned}
$$

```r
pbinom(1, size = 50, prob = 0.04)
# Returns: 0.4004812
```

**(iii) 99th percentile ŌĆö `qbinom(0.99, size = 50, prob = 0.04)` (1.5 marks)**

```r
qbinom(0.99, size = 50, prob = 0.04)
# Returns: 5
```

**Interpretation:** In 99% of batches, the number of defects will be at most 5. This is the **upper control limit** for the SPC (Statistical Process Control) chart.

**(iv) Poisson approximation with $\lambda = np$ (1.5 marks)**

When $n$ is large and $p$ is small, $Bin(n, p) \approx Pois(\lambda = np)$.

```r
lambda_approx <- 50 * 0.04   # = 2
dpois(2, lambda = lambda_approx)
# Returns: 0.2706706

# Compare with exact binomial:
dbinom(2, size = 50, prob = 0.04)
# Returns: 0.2782643
```

**Validity discussion:** The Poisson approximation gives $0.2707$ versus the exact binomial $0.2783$ ŌĆö an error of about 2.7%. This is acceptable when $n \geq 20$ and $p \leq 0.05$ (rule of thumb). Here $n = 50$, $p = 0.04$, so the rule-of-thumb conditions are satisfied. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Marks-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing `d` and `p` outputs:** A student who writes "the probability is 0.0266" for `dnorm(100, 100, 15)` will lose marks ŌĆö this is a *density*, not a probability. **(ŌłÆ2 marks typical penalty)**
> 2. **Wrong argument order:** For `qbinom(p, size, prob)` students sometimes write `qbinom(size, prob, p)` ŌĆö incorrect. The quantile `p` is **always first** for `q` functions.
> 3. **Forgetting `set.seed()`:** A code-only answer for `rnorm`/`rbinom` without `set.seed()` may be penalised for non-reproducibility in lab examinations.
> 4. **Mixing `rate` and `scale`:** The exponential distribution uses `rate`, but the Weibull and Gamma distributions may use `scale = 1/rate`. Students often mix these up. **(ŌłÆ1 mark penalty)**
> 5. **Integer vs Real:** Passing `prob = 1.5` or `size = -2` will silently produce `NaN` ŌĆö the model answer must show argument validation.

---

## ­¤ōī TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

### One-Line Summary
> R's common distributions follow the universal **dpqr** naming convention; the **type arguments** (e.g., `mean`, `sd`, `size`, `prob`, `lambda`, `rate`, `df`) define the shape of the probability curve, and their validity bounds must be respected.

### Critical Must-Remember Points

- Ō£ģ **Universal pattern:** `d<dist>`, `p<dist>`, `q<dist>`, `r<dist>` ŌĆö applies to all base-R distributions.
- Ō£ģ **Two distribution TYPES:**
  - **Continuous** ŌĆö `d` returns density (can be > 1); `x` Ōłł real line.
  - **Discrete** ŌĆö `d` returns probability (Ōēż 1); `x` Ōłł integers.
- Ō£ģ **Common continuous distributions & their arguments:**
  - `norm` ŌåÆ `mean`, `sd`
  - `unif` ŌåÆ `min`, `max`
  - `exp` ŌåÆ `rate`
  - `t` ŌåÆ `df`
  - `f` ŌåÆ `df1`, `df2`
  - `chisq` ŌåÆ `df`
  - `gamma` ŌåÆ `shape`, `rate`
  - `beta` ŌåÆ `shape1`, `shape2`
  - `weibull` ŌåÆ `shape`, `scale`
  - `lnorm` ŌåÆ `meanlog`, `sdlog`
- Ō£ģ **Common discrete distributions & their arguments:**
  - `binom` ŌåÆ `size`, `prob`
  - `pois` ŌåÆ `lambda`
  - `geom` ŌåÆ `prob`
  - `hyper` ŌåÆ `m`, `n`, `k`
  - `nbinom` ŌåÆ `size`, `prob`
- Ō£ģ **Argument validation rules:**
  - `prob`, `p` Ōłł [0, 1]
  - `size`, `n`, `df`, `lambda`, `rate` > 0
  - `sd`, `scale`, `sdlog` > 0
- Ō£ģ **Two-sided tail control:** The optional `lower.tail` argument toggles between $P(X \leq q)$ and $P(X > q)$.
- Ō£ģ **Reproducibility mantra:** Always use `set.seed()` before any `r<dist>()` call in examinations.
- Ō£ģ **Property shortcut:** For Poisson, $E[X] = \text{Var}(X) = \lambda$. A quick simulation-check identity.
- Ō£ģ **Inverse relationship:** `q<dist>(p<dist>(q)) Ōēł q` ŌĆö the quantile and CDF functions are mathematical inverses.
- Ō£ģ **Mean of distribution sample Ōēł theoretical mean** as $n \to \infty$ (Law of Large Numbers) ŌĆö a frequent question theme.
- Ō£ģ **Central Limit Theorem hook:** Sample means of any distribution (with finite variance) tend to `norm` as $n$ grows ŌĆö a direct application of `rnorm`, `rbinom`, `runif` etc.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probability Distributions - Normal Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT746 - PROGRAMMING IN R
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Probability Distributions - Normal Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Normal Distribution** (also known as the **Gaussian Distribution** or **LaplaceŌĆōGauss Distribution**) is a continuous probability distribution that is symmetric about its mean, depicting that data near the mean are more frequent in occurrence than data far from the mean. In graph form, the normal distribution appears as a **"bell curve"**.

A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a normal distribution with parameters $\mu$ (mean) and $\sigma^2$ (variance) if its **Probability Density Function (PDF)** is given by:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{\,-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}, \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

where:
- $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **mean** (location parameter)
- $\sigma > 0$ is the **standard deviation** (scale parameter)
- $\pi \approx \mathbf{3.14159}$ and $e \approx \mathbf{2.71828}$ are mathematical constants

We denote this as $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The variance of a normal distribution is denoted as $\sigma^2$, **NOT** $\sigma$. The second parameter in the notation $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is the **variance**, not the standard deviation. This is a common student mistake in board exams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Height of Students"**
> Imagine measuring the heights of **10,000 B.Tech students** in Kerala. Plot the heights on the x-axis and the number of students at each height on the y-axis. You will notice:
> - Most students cluster around the **average height** (say, 168 cm).
> - Very few students are extremely short (140 cm) or extremely tall (200 cm).
> - The shape of the graph is **symmetric and bell-shaped**.
>
> This is a **Normal Distribution** in action! It captures the natural variation present in almost every measurable quantity: blood pressure, exam scores, manufacturing tolerances, measurement errors, IQ scores, etc.

### 1.3 Why is the Normal Distribution "Special"?

The Normal Distribution holds a unique place in statistics, often called the **"bell curve of nature"** because of the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Central Limit Theorem (Informal Statement):** The sum (or average) of a large number of independent and identically distributed random variables, regardless of their original distribution, tends to follow a **Normal Distribution** as the sample size becomes large ($n \to \infty$).

This is why the Normal Distribution appears in:
- **Engineering:** Quality control, signal processing noise, reliability analysis.
- **Data Science:** Machine learning (Gaussian Naive Bayes, Gaussian kernels).
- **Finance:** Stock return modeling, risk assessment.
- **Biology:** Population traits, growth modeling.

### 1.4 The Two "Faces" of the Normal Distribution

| Type | Notation | Mean | Variance | Use Case |
|------|----------|------|----------|----------|
| **General Normal** | $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | Real-world raw data |
| **Standard Normal** | $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ | $0$ | $1$ | Tabulated probabilities (Z-tables) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Standard Normal Distribution** is a special case where $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$. Every normal distribution can be **transformed** (standardized) into the standard normal using the **Z-transformation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of the Normal Distribution (The "7 Golden Rules")

For $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:

1. **Symmetry:** The distribution is perfectly symmetric about the mean $\mu$. That is, $f(\mu - a) = f(\mu + a)$ for any $a > 0$.
2. **Unimodality:** It has exactly **one peak** (mode) located at $x = \mu$.
3. **Mean = Median = Mode:** All three measures of central tendency coincide at $\mu$.
4. **Total Probability = 1:** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$.
5. **Asymptotic Tails:** The curve approaches the x-axis as $x \to \pm\infty$ but **never touches** it.
6. **Inflection Points:** The curve changes concavity at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$.
7. **Skewness = 0, Excess Kurtosis = 0:** It is **mesokurtic** (neither too peaked nor too flat).

### 2.2 The Probability Density Function (PDF) ŌĆö Component-wise Breakdown

$$f(x) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}}_{\text{Normalization Constant}} \cdot \underbrace{e^{\,-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}}_{\text{Exponential Decay Term}}$$

- **Normalization Constant** ensures the total area under the curve equals 1.
- **Exponential Term** ensures $f(x) > 0$ for all $x$ and creates the bell shape.

### 2.3 The Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

The CDF of a normal random variable is the integral of the PDF:

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{\,-\frac{(t-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}\, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This integral has **no closed-form elementary solution**. It must be evaluated numerically. In R, we use `pnorm(x, mean, sd)`.

### 2.4 The Standard Normal Distribution & Z-Score

The **Z-score** (also called the **standard score**) measures how many standard deviations a data point $x$ is from the mean $\mu$:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

For the Standard Normal $Z \sim N(0, 1)$, the PDF simplifies to:

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{\,-z^2/2}, \quad -\infty < z < \infty$$

### 2.5 The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** This is one of the most frequently asked questions in KTU exams. Memorize it!

For any normal distribution $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:

| Interval | Probability | Percentage |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| $\mu - \sigma \leq X \leq \mu + \sigma$ | $\approx 0.6827$ | **68.27 %** |
| $\mu - 2\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 2\sigma$ | $\approx 0.9545$ | **95.45 %** |
| $\mu - 3\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 3\sigma$ | $\approx 0.9973$ | **99.73 %** |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Normal PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | For $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ |
| 2 | Standard Normal PDF | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-z^2/2}$ | For $Z \sim N(0,1)$ |
| 3 | Mean | $E(X) = \mu$ | Also median and mode |
| 4 | Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$ | SD = $\sigma$ |
| 5 | Z-transformation | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Converts $X$ to $Z$ |
| 6 | Reverse Z-transformation | $X = \mu + Z\sigma$ | Converts $Z$ to $X$ |
| 7 | Linear Transformation | $aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b,\; a^2\sigma^2)$ | $a \neq 0$ |
| 8 | Sum of Independent Normals | $X_1 + X_2 \sim N(\mu_1 + \mu_2,\; \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)$ | Additivity property |
| 9 | 68% Rule | $P(\mu - \sigma < X < \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827$ | Empirical rule |
| 10 | 95% Rule | $P(\mu - 2\sigma < X < \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545$ | Empirical rule |
| 11 | 99.7% Rule | $P(\mu - 3\sigma < X < \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$ | Empirical rule |
| 12 | R: PDF value | `dnorm(x, mean, sd)` | Density at $x$ |
| 13 | R: CDF value | `pnorm(q, mean, sd)` | $P(X \leq q)$ |
| 14 | R: Quantile | `qnorm(p, mean, sd)` | Inverse of `pnorm` |
| 15 | R: Random sample | `rnorm(n, mean, sd)` | Generate $n$ samples |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Quality Control (Manufacturing)** | Tolerance limits for shaft diameters, bolt sizes; Six-Sigma quality |
| **Signal Processing** | Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) modeling |
| **Reliability Engineering** | Failure time analysis of components |
| **Finance & Risk** | Value-at-Risk (VaR) calculations for portfolios |
| **Biostatistics** | Modeling heights, blood pressure, drug responses |
| **Machine Learning** | Gaussian Naive Bayes, Radial Basis Function kernels |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$ is the Normalization Constant

We want to find $C$ such that the integral of the bell-shaped function equals 1:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} C \cdot e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}\, dx = 1$$

**Step 1:** Use the substitution $u = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma \sqrt{2}}$, so $du = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\, dx$, i.e., $dx = \sigma\sqrt{2}\, du$.

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} C \cdot e^{-u^2} \cdot \sigma\sqrt{2}\, du = 1$$

**Step 2:** The famous **Gaussian Integral**:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\, du = \sqrt{\pi}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and solve for $C$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C \cdot \sigma\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} &= 1 \\
C \cdot \sigma\sqrt{2\pi} &= 1 \\
C &= \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the normalization constant in the PDF.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked-Out Probability Examples

---

#### **Example 1 (Standard Normal Probability)**

Let $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. Find $P(Z \leq 1.5)$.

**Step 1:** Identify the problem type: **CDF of Standard Normal** at $z = 1.5$.

**Step 2:** Look up the standard normal table (or use R: `pnorm(1.5)`):

$$P(Z \leq 1.5) = \Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation: There is a **93.32 %** chance that a standard normal random variable is less than or equal to 1.5.

---

#### **Example 2 (Two-Sided Probability)**

Let $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. Find $P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1)$.

**Step 1:** Use the property of symmetry:

$$P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1)$$

**Step 2:** Use the symmetry property $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$:

$$\Phi(-1) = 1 - \Phi(1) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$$

**Step 3:** Compute the difference:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) &= 0.8413 - 0.1587 \\
&= 0.6826 \\
&\approx 0.6827
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** This confirms the **Empirical 68 % Rule**.

---

#### **Example 3 (General Normal Probability ŌĆö Heights)**

The heights of students in a college follow $X \sim N(168, 16)$ (i.e., $\mu = 168$ cm, $\sigma^2 = 16$, $\sigma = 4$ cm). Find the probability that a randomly selected student has a height between 164 cm and 172 cm.

**Step 1:** Convert the raw values to Z-scores:

$$Z_1 = \frac{164 - 168}{4} = \frac{-4}{4} = -1$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{172 - 168}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = +1$$

**Step 2:** The problem reduces to:

$$P(164 \leq X \leq 172) = P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1)$$

**Step 3:** From Example 2 (or Empirical Rule):

$$P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) \approx 0.6827$$

**Step 4:** **Final Answer: There is a 68.27 % chance that a randomly selected student has a height between 164 cm and 172 cm.**

---

#### **Example 4 (Inverse Normal ŌĆö Finding Quantiles)**

A factory produces bolts with lengths following $X \sim N(10, 0.25)$. Find the length $L$ such that **95 %** of bolts are shorter than $L$.

**Step 1:** We need the 95th percentile, so $P(X \leq L) = 0.95$.

**Step 2:** The corresponding Z-score is:

$$z_{0.95} = 1.645 \quad \text{(from standard normal table)}$$

**Step 3:** Reverse-transform using $X = \mu + Z\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \mu + z_{0.95} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 10 + (1.645)(\sqrt{0.25}) \\
&= 10 + (1.645)(0.5) \\
&= 10 + 0.8225 \\
&= 10.8225 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** **Final Answer: 95 % of the bolts produced have length $\leq$ 10.8225 cm.**

---

#### **Example 5 (Sum of Two Independent Normals ŌĆö R Implementation)**

If $X_1 \sim N(50, 4)$ and $X_2 \sim N(60, 9)$ are independent, find $P(X_1 + X_2 > 120)$.

**Step 1:** By the additivity property:

$$X_1 + X_2 \sim N(50 + 60,\; 4 + 9) = N(110, 13)$$

**Step 2:** Standardize:

$$Z = \frac{120 - 110}{\sqrt{13}} = \frac{10}{3.6056} \approx 2.774$$

**Step 3:** Compute the upper tail:

$$P(X_1 + X_2 > 120) = P(Z > 2.774) = 1 - \Phi(2.774)$$

**Step 4:** From the Z-table: $\Phi(2.77) \approx 0.9972$.

$$P(X_1 + X_2 > 120) = 1 - 0.9972 = 0.0028$$

**Step 5:** **Final Answer: There is only a 0.28 % chance that the sum exceeds 120.**

---

#### **R Programming Demonstration (PECAT746 Specific)**

```r
# Example 3 in R
mu <- 168
sigma <- 4
p1 <- pnorm(172, mean = mu, sd = sigma)   # P(X <= 172)
p2 <- pnorm(164, mean = mu, sd = sigma)   # P(X <= 164)
prob <- p1 - p2
print(prob)   # 0.6826895

# Example 4 in R (Inverse)
L <- qnorm(0.95, mean = 10, sd = 0.5)
print(L)   # 10.82249

# Example 5 in R (Sum of Normals)
prob_sum <- 1 - pnorm(120, mean = 110, sd = sqrt(13))
print(prob_sum)   # 0.002804
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture: From General Normal to Standard Normal

The following block-level functional architecture illustrates the **standardization pipeline** used to compute probabilities for any normal distribution using the standard normal Z-tables.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Random Variable X follows N mu sigma squared] --> B[Step 1 Identify Parameters mu and sigma]
    B --> C[Step 2 Define Event of Interest e.g. a to b]
    C --> D[Step 3 Apply Z Transformation]
    D --> D1[Z1 equals a minus mu over sigma]
    D --> D2[Z2 equals b minus mu over sigma]
    D1 --> E[Step 4 Standard Normal Space Z to N 0 1]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Step 5 Consult Z Tables or pnorm in R]
    F --> G[Step 6 Compute Phi Z2 minus Phi Z1]
    G --> H[Final Probability P a to b]
    H --> I[Step 7 Interpret Result in Context]
```

### 4.2 Architecture: The Empirical 68-95-99.7 Rule (Visual Map)

The following diagram maps the three "sigma bands" of the empirical rule to their corresponding probabilities and engineering interpretations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    N[Normal Distribution Bell Curve] --> B1[Band 1 mu minus sigma to mu plus sigma]
    N --> B2[Band 2 mu minus 2sigma to mu plus 2sigma]
    N --> B3[Band 3 mu minus 3sigma to mu plus 3sigma]
    B1 --> P1[Probability 0.6827 or 68.27 percent]
    B2 --> P2[Probability 0.9545 or 95.45 percent]
    B3 --> P3[Probability 0.9973 or 99.73 percent]
    P1 --> U1[Engineering Use Routine Quality Control]
    P2 --> U2[Engineering Use Standard Tolerance Limits]
    P3 --> U3[Engineering Use Outlier Detection Six Sigma]
```

### 4.3 Architecture: R Function Ecosystem for Normal Distribution

The following block diagram shows the four primary R functions used to interact with the normal distribution and their relationships.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[R Programming Language] --> F1[dnorm x mean sd]
    R --> F2[pnorm q mean sd]
    R --> F3[qnorm p mean sd]
    R --> F4[rnorm n mean sd]
    F1 --> D1[Returns PDF value f of x]
    F2 --> D2[Returns CDF value P X to q]
    F3 --> D3[Returns Quantile x such that P X to x to p]
    F4 --> D4[Returns n random samples from N mu sigma squared]
    D2 --> L1[pnorm and qnorm are Mathematical Inverses]
    D3 --> L1
    F1 --> L2[dnorm integrates to pnorm]
    F2 --> L2
```

### 4.4 ASCII Sketch: The Bell Curve with Key Inflection Points

```
                       Normal Distribution PDF
                f(x)
                 |
        .-------Ō¢ł-------.
      .'         Ō¢ł         '.
    .'           Ō¢ł           '.
   /             Ō¢ł             \
  /  ╬╝-3Žā  ╬╝-2Žā  Ō¢ł  ╬╝+2Žā  ╬╝+3Žā  \
 /-------|-------|Ō¢ł|-------|-------\
                ╬╝ (mean = median = mode)
                                
  |ŌåÉ 0.15% ŌåÆ|ŌåÉŌöĆŌöĆ 95.45% ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ|ŌåÉ 0.15% ŌåÆ|
  |ŌåÉŌöĆŌöĆ 99.73% (Total Coverage) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ|
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
Define the Normal Distribution. Write its probability density function and state any three of its properties.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Normal Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution that is symmetric and bell-shaped, characterized by two parameters: the mean $\mu$ and the variance $\sigma^2$. It is denoted as $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Probability Density Function (1 Mark):**

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{\,-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}, \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

**Three Properties (1.5 Marks ŌĆö 0.5 each):**

1. The curve is **symmetric** about the mean $\mu$, i.e., the mean, median, and mode are all equal to $\mu$.
2. The **total area** under the curve is equal to 1.
3. The curve is **asymptotic** to the x-axis ŌĆö it approaches but never touches the x-axis.

**Smoothness and unimodality** (0.5 Mark):

4. It has exactly one peak (unimodal) and the points of inflection occur at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]**
What is the Standard Normal Distribution? How do you transform a general normal variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ into a standard normal variable?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Standard Normal Distribution** is a special case of the normal distribution with mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 1$. It is denoted as $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. (1 Mark)

The PDF of the standard normal is:

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-z^2/2}$$

**Z-Transformation (2 Marks):**

Any general normal variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ can be converted to a standard normal variable $Z$ using the formula:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

This transformation **shifts** the distribution to have mean 0 (by subtracting $\mu$) and **scales** it to have standard deviation 1 (by dividing by $\sigma$). The resulting $Z$ can then be used with standard normal tables to compute probabilities.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

**(a)** The marks of students in a class follow a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 65$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 8$.

  **(i)** Find the probability that a randomly selected student scored **more than 75 marks**. (3 Marks)
  **(ii)** Find the probability that a student scored **between 55 and 75 marks**. (4 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)** of the normal distribution with a neat diagram. What percentage of data lies within $2\sigma$ of the mean? (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a)(i) ŌĆö $P(X > 75)$ (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert $X = 75$ to a Z-score using $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$:

$$Z = \frac{75 - 65}{8} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25$$

**Step 2:** From the standard normal table:

$$\Phi(1.25) = P(Z \leq 1.25) = 0.8944$$

**Step 3:** Compute the required upper-tail probability:

$$P(X > 75) = P(Z > 1.25) = 1 - \Phi(1.25) = 1 - 0.8944 = 0.1056$$

**Final Answer:** $P(X > 75) \approx \mathbf{0.1056}$ (i.e., about **10.56 %**).

---

**(a)(ii) ŌĆö $P(55 \leq X \leq 75)$ (4 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert both bounds to Z-scores:

$$Z_1 = \frac{55 - 65}{8} = \frac{-10}{8} = -1.25$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{75 - 65}{8} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25$$

**Step 2:** Express the probability:

$$P(55 \leq X \leq 75) = P(-1.25 \leq Z \leq 1.25) = \Phi(1.25) - \Phi(-1.25)$$

**Step 3:** Use the symmetry property $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$:

$$\Phi(-1.25) = 1 - \Phi(1.25) = 1 - 0.8944 = 0.1056$$

**Step 4:** Compute the difference:

$$P(-1.25 \leq Z \leq 1.25) = 0.8944 - 0.1056 = 0.7888$$

**Final Answer:** $P(55 \leq X \leq 75) \approx \mathbf{0.7888}$ (i.e., about **78.88 %**).

---

**(b) ŌĆö Empirical Rule (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**

The **Empirical Rule** (also called the **68-95-99.7 Rule**) states that for any normal distribution, approximately:
- **68.27 %** of the data lies within **1 standard deviation** of the mean.
- **95.45 %** of the data lies within **2 standard deviations** of the mean.
- **99.73 %** of the data lies within **3 standard deviations** of the mean.

**Mathematical Statement (2 Marks):**

For $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:

$$P(\mu - \sigma \leq X \leq \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827$$

$$P(\mu - 2\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545$$

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$$

**Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
         f(x)
          Ō¢ł
        Ō¢ł Ō¢ł Ō¢ł
      Ō¢ł   Ō¢ł   Ō¢ł
    Ō¢ł     Ō¢ł     Ō¢ł
  Ō¢ł       Ō¢ł       Ō¢ł
  |---|---|---|---|---|---|
  ╬╝-3Žā ╬╝-2Žā ╬╝-Žā  ╬╝ ╬╝+Žā ╬╝+2Žā ╬╝+3Žā
  |ŌåÉ 0.15 ŌåÆ|ŌåÉ 13.6 ŌåÆ|ŌåÉ 34.1 ŌåÆ|ŌåÉ 34.1 ŌåÆ|ŌåÉ 13.6 ŌåÆ|ŌåÉ 0.15 ŌåÆ|
  |ŌåÉŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ 99.73 % of data within ┬▒3Žā ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ|
```

**Percentage within $2\sigma$ (1 Mark):**

**Approximately 95.45 %** of the data lies within $2\sigma$ of the mean.

**Engineering Application (1 Mark):**

This rule is the foundation of **Six-Sigma quality control** in manufacturing. By setting process limits at $\pm 3\sigma$, only **0.27 %** of products fall outside specifications, ensuring near-perfect quality.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2022 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Write the R code to:
  **(i)** Generate 1000 random samples from $N(50, 5)$ and plot the histogram. (3 Marks)
  **(ii)** Compute $P(X \leq 55)$ where $X \sim N(50, 5)$. (2 Marks)
  **(iii)** Find the value $x$ such that $P(X \leq x) = 0.90$. (2 Marks)

**(b)** The lifetime (in hours) of a certain electronic component follows $X \sim N(500, 10000)$. Find:
  **(i)** The probability that a component lasts **more than 600 hours**. (3 Marks)
  **(ii)** The value $L$ such that **90 %** of components last at least $L$ hours. (4 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a)(i) ŌĆö R Code: Generate and Plot (3 Marks):**

```r
# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(42)

# Generate 1000 random samples from N(50, 5)
# Note: In R, rnorm uses standard deviation (not variance)
samples <- rnorm(n = 1000, mean = 50, sd = 5)

# Plot the histogram
hist(samples,
     breaks = 30,
     col = "skyblue",
     border = "black",
     main = "Histogram of 1000 Samples from N(50, 5)",
     xlab = "Value",
     ylab = "Frequency")

# Overlay the theoretical density curve
curve(dnorm(x, mean = 50, sd = 5),
      col = "red",
      lwd = 2,
      add = TRUE)
legend("topright", legend = "Theoretical N(50,5)", col = "red", lwd = 2)
```

---

**(a)(ii) ŌĆö R Code: CDF (2 Marks):**

```r
# P(X <= 55) where X ~ N(50, 5)
prob <- pnorm(55, mean = 50, sd = 5)
print(prob)   # Output: 0.8413447
```

**Interpretation:** There is approximately an **84.13 %** probability that $X \leq 55$.

---

**(a)(iii) ŌĆö R Code: Quantile (2 Marks):**

```r
# Find x such that P(X <= x) = 0.90
x <- qnorm(0.90, mean = 50, sd = 5)
print(x)   # Output: 56.40776
```

**Interpretation:** The 90th percentile of the distribution $N(50, 5)$ is approximately **56.41**.

---

**(b)(i) ŌĆö $P(X > 600)$ (3 Marks):**

Given: $X \sim N(500, 10000)$, so $\mu = 500$ and $\sigma = \sqrt{10000} = 100$.

**Step 1:** Convert to Z-score:

$$Z = \frac{600 - 500}{100} = \frac{100}{100} = 1.00$$

**Step 2:** Look up $\Phi(1.00)$ in the Z-table:

$$\Phi(1.00) = 0.8413$$

**Step 3:** Compute upper-tail probability:

$$P(X > 600) = 1 - \Phi(1.00) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$$

**Final Answer:** $P(X > 600) \approx \mathbf{0.1587}$ (i.e., about **15.87 %**).

**R Verification:**

```r
1 - pnorm(600, mean = 500, sd = 100)   # Output: 0.1586553
```

---

**(b)(ii) ŌĆö Finding $L$ such that $P(X \geq L) = 0.90$ (4 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Rewrite the condition:

$$P(X \geq L) = 0.90 \implies P(X < L) = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$

**Step 2:** Find the Z-score for the 10th percentile:

$$z_{0.10} = -1.2816 \quad \text{(from Z-table, or `qnorm(0.10)`)}$$

**Step 3:** Reverse-transform to find $L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \mu + z_{0.10} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 500 + (-1.2816)(100) \\
&= 500 - 128.16 \\
&= 371.84 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** **Final Answer: 90 % of components last at least approximately 371.84 hours.**

**R Verification:**

```r
L <- qnorm(0.10, mean = 500, sd = 100)
print(L)   # Output: 371.8485
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Variance and Standard Deviation:** In $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the second parameter is the **variance**, not the standard deviation. If the question says $\sigma^2 = 9$, then $\sigma = 3$, not 9. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Z-table Reading Direction:** When asked for $P(X > a)$, many students compute $P(X < a)$ instead. Always remember: "greater than" ŌåÆ **upper tail** ŌåÆ subtract from 1. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Symmetry Property:** Use $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ rather than looking up negative values directly in the table. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 4. **R function misuse:** `rnorm(n, mean, sd)` and `dnorm(x, mean, sd)` use **sd** (standard deviation), not variance. **(Lose 2 marks in R coding questions)**
>
> 5. **Not showing Z-conversion steps:** Always show the calculation $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ as a separate line. Examiners award partial credit for this. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 6. **No units in the final answer:** If the question asks about "hours" or "cm", include the unit in your final numerical answer. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Normal Distributions**

- [x] **Normal Distribution PDF:** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$
- [x] **Notation:** $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ ŌĆö second parameter is **variance**, not SD.
- [x] **Key Constants:** $\pi \approx 3.14159$, $e \approx 2.71828$, $1/\sqrt{2\pi} \approx 0.3989$.
- [x] **Mean = Median = Mode** = $\mu$. The distribution is **symmetric** about $\mu$.
- [x] **Total area under curve** = 1 (normalization).
- [x] **Inflection points** at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$.
- [x] **Z-transformation:** $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ converts $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ to $N(0, 1)$.
- [x] **Symmetry property:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.
- [x] **Empirical Rule:** 68.27 % within $\pm 1\sigma$, 95.45 % within $\pm 2\sigma$, 99.73 % within $\pm 3\sigma$.
- [x] **Linear combination:** $aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b, a^2\sigma^2)$ for $a \neq 0$.
- [x] **Sum of independent normals:** $X_1 + X_2 \sim N(\mu_1 + \mu_2, \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)$.
- [x] **R functions:** `dnorm` (PDF), `pnorm` (CDF), `qnorm` (quantile), `rnorm` (random samples). All use **mean** and **sd** as arguments.
- [x] **CLT connection:** Sum/average of many i.i.d. random variables $\to$ Normal distribution.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** Quality control, signal noise, reliability, risk modeling, machine learning.
- [x] **Common KTU pitfall:** For upper-tail probabilities, compute $1 - \Phi(z)$, not $\Phi(z)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R Graphics - Overview, Customizing Charts, Graphical Parameters, and Basic Graphics Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT746 - PROGRAMMING IN R
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** R Graphics - Overview, Customizing Charts, Graphical Parameters, and Basic Graphics Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is R Graphics?

**R Graphics** is the in-built subsystem of the R programming language used to create publication-quality static, dynamic, and interactive visualisations ranging from simple scatter plots to complex multi-panel dashboards. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PECAT746 ŌĆō Programming in R*, R Graphics encompasses **two parallel engines** ŌĆö the *traditional (base) graphics engine* built on S/R's original Bell Labs design, and the *grid graphics engine* introduced by Paul Murrell (2002), which forms the backbone of the famous `ggplot2` and `lattice` packages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved)**
> "R Graphics refers to the collection of functions, parameters, and devices in R used to construct, annotate, customize, render, and export statistical plots and charts, governed by the `graphics`, `grDevices`, and `grid` packages."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Digital Architect's Drafting Table"

Imagine you are an **architect** sketching a building plan on a giant **digital drafting table** that has:
- A **paper roll** (the *graphics device* ŌĆö your screen, a PDF file, or a PNG image)
- A **set of stencils** (the *high-level functions* like `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`)
- A **palette of coloured pens** (the *graphical parameters* like `col`, `lty`, `pch`)
- A **ruler and compass** (the *coordinate system* and `axes` parameters)
- A **stencil guide book** (the *layout engine* ŌĆö `par()`, `layout()`, `mfrow`)

Every time you ask R to "draw" something, you are essentially:
1. **Unrolling a fresh sheet of paper** (`dev.new()` or auto-device).
2. **Choosing a stencil** (a *plot function*).
3. **Adjusting the rulers** (graphical *parameters*).
4. **Stamping the drawing** on paper (rendering the *output device*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why R Graphics is special for KTU engineers**: Unlike Excel's click-and-drag approach, R Graphics is **fully scriptable**, which means your entire visualisation pipeline becomes **reproducible** ŌĆö a critical skill for B.Tech project reports and research publications.

## 1.3 The Two Graphics Engines ŌĆö A 30-Second Comparison

| Aspect | Traditional (Base) Graphics | Grid Graphics |
|---|---|---|
| **Package Name** | `graphics` (auto-loaded) | `grid` (must be loaded) |
| **Philosophy** | *Painter's model* ŌĆö draw directly on canvas | *Scene-graph model* ŌĆö build a tree of graphical objects, then render |
| **High-level user packages** | Built-in functions | `lattice`, `ggplot2` |
| **Output** | Immediate / direct | Compositional / layered |
| **KTU Syllabus Weight** | **High (Module 3 core)** | Conceptual awareness only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Module 3 examinations, you are **only required** to master **Traditional (Base) Graphics** along with its parameters. `ggplot2` is mentioned in the syllabus only as a *related concept* and will not be a 14-mark question.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorise

- **Default plot dimensions** ŌåÆ **7 ├Ś 7 inches** (at 72 ppi).
- **Default point character size** (`cex = 1`) ŌåÆ approximately **12 points**.
- **Default line width** (`lwd = 1`) ŌåÆ approximately **1 pixel**.
- **Number of built-in plotting characters (`pch`)** ŌåÆ **25 symbols** (codes 0ŌĆō25).
- **Number of line types (`lty`)** ŌåÆ **6 predefined types** (1ŌĆō6).
- **Standard colour palette** ("R colours") ŌåÆ **657 named hues** accessible via `colors()`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of R Graphics ŌĆö How a Plot is Born

Understanding *how* R produces a plot is critical for debugging and for the 14-mark questions. The rendering pipeline is:

1. **Device Driver Activation** ŌĆö R opens a *graphical device* (screen ŌåÆ `RStudioGD`/`quartz`/`windows()`; file ŌåÆ `pdf()`, `png()`, `jpeg()`, `tiff()`, `svg()`).
2. **Coordinate System Establishment** ŌĆö A default 0-to-1 user coordinate system is mapped onto the device's physical coordinates.
3. **High-Level Function Call** ŌĆö A function like `plot(x, y)` draws axes, titles, and the data points.
4. **Low-Level Function Overlays** ŌĆö Functions like `points()`, `lines()`, `text()`, `legend()`, `abline()` are *added on top* of an existing plot.
5. **Device Finalisation** ŌĆö `dev.off()` closes the device and flushes the buffer (mandatory for file devices).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: A common 3-mark question asks *"What is the difference between a high-level and a low-level plotting function in R?"* ŌĆö answer using the pipeline above.

### 2.1.1 High-Level vs. Low-Level Functions

| High-Level (Standalone) | Low-Level (Overlay) |
|---|---|
| Always start a **new plot** | Always operate on an **existing plot** |
| `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`, `boxplot()`, `pie()`, `mosaicplot()`, `pairs()`, `coplot()` | `points()`, `lines()`, `text()`, `mtext()`, `legend()`, `abline()`, `segments()`, `arrows()`, `rect()`, `polygon()`, `title()` |
| **Erase previous plot** by default (unless `add = TRUE`) | **Preserve** the existing canvas |

## 2.2 Graphical Devices ŌĆö Where Your Plot Lives

```r
getOption("device")      # Returns the default device name
dev.list()               # Lists all active devices with their numbers
dev.cur()                # Returns the number of the currently active device
dev.set(which = k)       # Makes device k the current one
dev.off(k)               # Closes device k
dev.copy(device = pdf, file = "copy.pdf")   # Clones current plot
```

## 2.3 The `par()` Function ŌĆö The Master Control Panel

The function `par()` is the **single most important** KTU topic in this module. It sets or queries **72 graphical parameters** that control *every* aspect of plotting. It has two modes:

| Mode | Syntax | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Setter (Permanent)** | `par(col = "red", lwd = 2)` | Changes parameter values **for the rest of the session** |
| **Getter (Snapshot)** | `oldpar <- par(col = "red", lwd = 2)` | Saves current state, then changes ŌĆö used to **safely revert** later via `par(oldpar)` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Forgetting to restore `par()` after a change will **corrupt every subsequent plot** in your session. Always wrap customisation in `oldpar <- par(...)` ... `par(oldpar)`.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Graphical Parameters)

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact. **Do not use `|` inside any table cell.**

| Parameter | Allowed Values | Effect on Plot | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pch` | Integer 0ŌĆō25 (or any single character) | Shape of the plotting symbol | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `cex` | Numeric scalar (default \vert 1.0\vert) | Character/text expansion factor | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `lty` | Integer 1ŌĆō6 or a string of 2/4/6 hex digits | Line type (solid, dashed, dotted, ŌĆ”) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `lwd` | Numeric scalar (default \vert 1\vert) | Line width in multiples of default | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `col` | Colour name, hex string `"#RRGGBB"`, or integer (palette index) | Colour of plotting symbol, line, or text | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `col.axis` / `col.lab` / `col.main` / `col.sub` | Same as `col` | Colour of axis numbers, axis labels, main title, subtitle | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `bg` | Colour string | Background colour of the plot region | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `mfrow` | Vector `c(nrow, ncol)` | Splits the device into an `nrow` ├Ś `ncol` panel grid, **filled row-wise** | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `mfcol` | Vector `c(nrow, ncol)` | Same as `mfrow` but **filled column-wise** | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `mar` | Numeric vector `c(bottom, left, top, right)` in **lines of text** | Plot margins; default is `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `mai` | Same as `mar` but in **inches** | Plot margins in inches | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `oma` / `omi` | Numeric vector | Outer margins (for super-titles) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `las` | Integer 0/1/2/3 | Orientation of axis labels (parallel/horizontal/perpendicular/vertical) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `bty` | One of `"o"`, `"l"`, `"7"`, `"c"`, `"u"`, `"]"` or `"n"` | Box type around the plot | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `tcl` | Numeric (default \vert -0.5\vert) | Length of axis tick marks in fractions of line height | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `xaxt` / `yaxt` | `"n"` to suppress, `"s"` to show | Whether to draw the X / Y axis | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `xlog` / `ylog` | Logical (`TRUE`/`FALSE`) | Use a logarithmic X / Y axis | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `xlim` / `ylim` | Numeric vector `c(min, max)` | Manual limits of the X / Y axis | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `main` / `sub` | Character string | Main title / subtitle | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `xlab` / `ylab` | Character string | X-axis / Y-axis label | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| `family` | Font family name (e.g., `"serif"`, `"sans"`, `"mono"`) | Font family for all text | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Customisable R Graphics

| Engineering Discipline | Typical Use of R Graphics |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Stress-strain curves, load-deflection plots, contour maps |
| **Electrical & Electronics** | Signal waveforms (`plot(ts_data)`), Bode plots, histograms of noise |
| **Mechanical** | Fatigue-life Weibull plots, vibration spectrums |
| **Computer Science (incl. AI/ML)** | ROC curves, confusion-matrix heatmaps, learning curves |
| **Biotechnology** | Growth curves, dose-response curves |
| **Statistical Quality Control** | X-bar/R control charts, Pareto diagrams |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> In this section, "derivations" are formalised **code-walks**: each R statement is dissected with its argument semantics, expected return values, and side-effects on the graphics device. Every R code block is **fully runnable**.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Anatomy of a `plot()` Call

The general form of the most fundamental R plotting function is:

```r
plot(x, y, type, main, sub, xlab, ylab, xlim, ylim,
     col, pch, cex, lty, lwd, bty, las, axes, log, ...)
```

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. **`x`, `y`** ŌåÆ numeric vectors defining the Cartesian coordinates. If `y` is omitted, `x` is plotted against its **index** `1:length(x)`.
2. **`type`** ŌåÆ character specifier controlling *what* is drawn. The full enumeration is:

   | `type` Value | Meaning |
   |---|---|
   | `"p"` | Points only (default) |
   | `"l"` | Lines only |
   | `"b"` | Both points and lines |
   | `"o"` | Both, lines *over* points |
   | `"h"` | Histogram-like vertical lines |
   | `"s"` | Step plot (right-continuous) |
   | `"S"` | Step plot (left-continuous) |
   | `"n"` | No plotting ŌĆö sets up axes only |

3. **`xlim`, `ylim`** ŌåÆ numeric vector `c(min, max)` that fixes the axis extents. Critical for **comparing two plots** side-by-side.
4. **`col`, `pch`, `cex`** ŌåÆ aesthetics of the data marks.
5. **`main`, `sub`, `xlab`, ` ylab`** ŌåÆ text annotations.
6. **`...`** ŌåÆ accepts any of the 72 `par()` parameters (and many more function-specific ones).

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**

```r
x <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = 100)
y <- sin(x)

plot(x, y,
     type  = "l",
     col   = "steelblue",
     lwd   = 3,
     lty   = 1,
     main  = expression(paste("Sine Wave from ", -pi, " to ", pi)),
     xlab  = "Angle (radians)",
     ylab  = expression(sin(x)),
     xlim  = c(-pi, pi),
     ylim  = c(-1.1, 1.1),
     bty   = "l",
     las   = 1)
abline(h = 0, v = 0, lty = 3, col = "grey50")   # low-level overlay
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is KTU-grade**: Notice the use of `expression()` inside `main` and `ylab` ŌĆö this is how you embed **mathematical symbols** ($\pi$, $\sin(x)$, subscripts) in plot annotations. This is a 7-mark favourite in KTU Part B.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Building a Customised Histogram

The `hist()` function has the signature:

```r
hist(x, breaks, freq, probability, include.lowest, right, density,
     angle, col, border, main, xlab, ylab, xlim, ylim, axes, labels, ...)
```

**Logical Decomposition:**

1. **`breaks`** ŌåÆ controls the bin edges. It can be:
   - A single integer (suggested number of bins).
   - A numeric vector of **explicit cut-points**.
   - A character string: `"Sturges"`, `"Scott"`, `"FD"` (FreedmanŌĆōDiaconis).
2. **`freq = TRUE`** (default) ŌåÆ y-axis shows *counts*; `freq = FALSE` ŌåÆ y-axis shows *densities* (so that total area \vert 1.0\vert).
3. **`col`, `border`, `density`, `angle`** ŌåÆ fill colour, border colour, shading density (lines per inch), and shading angle.
4. **`labels = TRUE`** ŌåÆ prints the count above each bar.

**Worked Example:**

```r
set.seed(42)
data <- rnorm(1000, mean = 50, sd = 10)

hist(data,
     breaks   = 30,
     col      = "skyblue",
     border   = "white",
     main     = "Normal Distribution (mean = 50, sd = 10)",
     xlab     = "Observed Value",
     ylab     = "Frequency",
     labels   = FALSE,
     las      = 1)
box(bty = "o")                                # draws a closed box around plot
```

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö `barplot()` for Categorical Data

```r
barplot(height, beside, horiz, col, border, legend.text, args.legend,
        main, xlab, ylab, names.arg, density, angle, width, space, ...)
```

**Logical Steps:**

1. **`height`** ŌåÆ either a *vector* (simple barplot) or a *matrix* (grouped/stacked barplot).
2. **`beside = FALSE`** (default) ŌåÆ **stacked** bars; `beside = TRUE` ŌåÆ **grouped** (side-by-side) bars.
3. **`horiz = TRUE`** ŌåÆ rotates the bars to be horizontal (great for ranked data).
4. **`names.arg`** ŌåÆ character vector giving labels for each bar.
5. **`legend.text`** ŌåÆ automatic legend (use with matrix `height`).

**Worked Example ŌĆö Grouped Barplot:**

```r
sales <- matrix(c(23, 30, 18,
                  25, 28, 22,
                  19, 33, 25), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
colnames(sales) <- c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3")
rownames(sales) <- c("2022", "2023", "2024")

barplot(sales,
        beside   = TRUE,
        col      = c("tomato", "steelblue", "seagreen"),
        main     = "Quarterly Sales (in Lakhs INR)",
        xlab     = "Quarter",
        ylab     = "Revenue",
        legend.text = rownames(sales),
        args.legend = list(x = "topleft", bty = "n"))
```

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö `boxplot()` for Five-Number Summaries

The boxplot encodes the **five-number summary** $\vert Q_0, Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4 \vert$ visually.

```r
boxplot(x, ..., range, width, notch, varwidth, horizontal, col,
        border, main, xlab, ylab, names, at, axes, outline)
```

| Argument | Effect |
|---|---|
| `notch = TRUE` | Draws a "notch" around the median ŌĆö if notches of two boxes don't overlap, the medians differ at ~95 % confidence |
| `varwidth = TRUE` | Box widths are proportional to $\vert \sqrt{n} \vert$ |
| `range = 1.5` (default) | Whiskers extend to $\vert Q_1 - 1.5 \times IQR \vert$ and $\vert Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR \vert$ (Tukey fences) |
| `horizontal = TRUE` | Rotates the plot |

**Worked Example:**

```r
boxplot(mpg ~ cyl, data = mtcars,
        col    = c("lightyellow", "lightblue", "lightgreen"),
        main   = "MPG by Number of Cylinders",
        xlab   = "Cylinders",
        ylab   = "Miles per Gallon",
        notch  = TRUE,
        varwidth = TRUE)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: The boxplot above uses R's *formula interface* `mpg ~ cyl`. This conditional syntax (`y ~ group`) is used in many R plot functions (`boxplot`, `plot`, `coplot`, `xyplot`). Memorise it.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö `pie()` for Proportional Data

```r
pie(x, labels, edges, radius, clockwise, init.angle, density,
    angle, col, border, lty, main, ...)
```

| Argument | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|
| `labels` | **Auto-uses** row names of `x` if not supplied ŌĆö beware of `NULL` labels |
| `clockwise` | Default is `FALSE` ŌĆö slices start at 12 o'clock and proceed *counter-clockwise* |
| `init.angle` | Initial angle (in degrees) of the **first** slice |
| `col` | If shorter than `length(x)`, R **recycles** the palette |

**Worked Example:**

```r
slices <- c(40, 25, 20, 10, 5)
labels <- paste0(c("C", "Java", "Python", "R", "Other"), " (", slices, "%)")
pie(slices,
    labels = labels,
    col    = rainbow(5),
    main   = "Programming Language Usage (KTU Survey 2024)",
    radius = 0.9)
```

## 3.6 Derivation 6 ŌĆö Multi-Panel Layouts using `par(mfrow)` and `layout()`

### 3.6.1 Using `par(mfrow)`

```r
oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))   # 2x2 panel grid
plot(1:10, main = "Plot 1")
hist(rnorm(100), main = "Plot 2")
boxplot(mtcars$mpg, main = "Plot 3")
pie(c(30, 70), labels = c("A", "B"), main = "Plot 4")
par(oldpar)                                            # ALWAYS restore
```

**Mathematical layout reasoning:**
- The device is divided into an $M \times N$ matrix, where $M = \texttt{mfrow[1]}$ and $N = \texttt{mfrow[2]}$.
- Total number of panels $\vert T \vert = M \times N$.
- Panels are filled **row-major order** for `mfrow` and **column-major order** for `mfcol`.

### 3.6.2 Using `layout()` (Non-Rectangular)

```r
m <- matrix(c(1, 1, 2,
              3, 4, 4), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
layout(m)
plot(1:10, main = "Big Plot (Top)")
hist(rnorm(100), main = "Side")
boxplot(mtcars$mpg, main = "Bottom-Left")
pie(c(30, 70), main = "Bottom-Right")
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Forgetting `par(mfrow)` to set the layout *before* the first plotting call. Once a plot has been opened, `mfrow` has no retroactive effect on that plot.

## 3.7 Derivation 7 ŌĆö Saving Plots with File Devices

The canonical pattern is **Open ŌåÆ Plot ŌåÆ Close**.

```r
png(filename = "histogram.png", width = 800, height = 600, res = 120)
hist(rnorm(1000), col = "steelblue", main = "Saved Histogram")
dev.off()                              # mandatory ŌĆö flushes to disk
```

For PDF (vector ŌĆö best for LaTeX reports):

```r
pdf(file = "report.pdf", width = 8, height = 6)
plot(1:10, type = "b", main = "Vector Output")
dev.off()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Tip**: If you open a `pdf()` device but forget `dev.off()`, the PDF file remains **locked / zero-bytes** until R is closed. Always close the device explicitly.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Function-Selection Decision Table

| Your Data | Best Base-R Function |
|---|---|
| Two numeric vectors (relationship) | `plot(x, y, type = "p")` |
| One numeric vector (distribution) | `hist()` |
| One categorical vs. one numeric (compare groups) | `boxplot(y ~ x)` |
| Frequencies of categories | `barplot()` |
| Proportions of a whole | `pie()` (Ōēż7 categories) |
| Multivariate (Ōēź3 numeric) | `pairs()` / `matplot()` |
| Time series | `plot.ts()` / `ts.plot()` |
| Functional 3D | `contour()`, `image()`, `persp()` |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö R Graphics Rendering Pipeline

The following block diagram traces **exactly** what happens between issuing `plot(...)` and seeing pixels on your screen.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Issues plot Call] --> B{Is a Device Open?}
    B -- No --> C[Auto-Open Default Device: RStudioGD or quartz or windows]
    B -- Yes --> D[Use Existing Active Device]
    C --> E[Initialise Graphics State via par]
    D --> E
    E --> F[High-Level Function Draws Axes, Titles, Data Marks]
    F --> G{User Invokes Low-Level Function?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Overlay points lines text legend abline on Existing Canvas]
    G -- No --> I[Plot Complete in Device Buffer]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Output Target?}
    J -- Screen --> K[Render to Monitor via Graphics Driver]
    J -- File --> L[Buffer Held in Memory]
    L --> M[User Calls dev.off]
    M --> N[Flush Buffer to pdf OR png OR jpeg OR tiff File]
    K --> O[End]
    N --> O
```

> **Reading guide for KTU students**: Trace each step verbally during your exam ŌĆö examiners reward answers that mention the **device-driver model** explicitly.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Function Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[R Plotting Functions] --> HL[High-Level Functions]
    Root --> LL[Low-Level Functions]
    Root --> Inter[Interactive Functions]
    HL --> HL1[plot]
    HL --> HL2[hist]
    HL --> HL3[barplot]
    HL --> HL4[boxplot]
    HL --> HL5[pie]
    HL --> HL6[pairs]
    HL --> HL7[coplot]
    LL --> LL1[points]
    LL --> LL2[lines]
    LL --> LL3[text and mtext]
    LL --> LL4[legend]
    LL --> LL5[abline]
    LL --> LL6[segments and arrows]
    LL --> LL7[rect and polygon]
    LL --> LL8[title]
    LL --> LL9[axis]
    Inter --> I1[locator]
    Inter --> I2[identify]
    Inter --> I3[grconvertX and grconvertY]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Multi-Panel Layout Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Need Multiple Panels?] --> Q2{Grid Shape Rectangular?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use par mfrow equals c nrows ncols]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use layout matrix for Non-Rectangular Mosaic]
    Q3 --> Q5{Restore par at End?}
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Save oldpar equals par then par oldpar after plotting]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[WARNING Side Effects Persist in Session]
    Q6 --> Done[Panels Rendered Correctly]
    Q7 --> Done
```

```
+------------------------------------------------------------+
| OMA: Outer Margin (outer=TRUE in mtext)                    |
|  +------------------------------------------------------+  |
|  | MAR: Top margin (main, sub titles live here)         |  |
|  |  +----------------------------------------------+    |  |
|  |  | MAR: Left margin (ylab)                      |    |  |
|  |  |  +-------- PLOT REGION (inner box) -------+  |    |  |
|  |  |  |  Axes, data marks, abline overlays     |  |    |  |
|  |  |  |  X-axis label (xlab) lives in MAR bot  |  |    |  |
|  |  |  +-----------------------------------------+  |    |  |
|  |  |  MAR: Bottom margin                          |    |  |
|  |  +----------------------------------------------+    |  |
|  +------------------------------------------------------+  |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule**: Part A carries 3 marks each and must be answered in **one to two crisp sentences** (max 80 words). Avoid padding.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between high-level and low-level plotting functions in R. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
High-level plotting functions in R are **stand-alone** functions that create a new, complete plot on the graphics device ŌĆö they automatically draw axes, titles, and data marks. *Examples:* `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`. In contrast, low-level plotting functions **operate on an existing plot** by adding elements such as extra points, lines, text, or legends without erasing the canvas. *Examples:* `points()`, `lines()`, `legend()`, `abline()`. *(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for definition, 1 for high-level example, 1 for low-level example)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the `par()` function in R. What happens if you forget to restore `par()` after a customisation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `par()` function is used to **set or query 72 global graphical parameters** (such as `col`, `pch`, `lwd`, `mfrow`, `mar`) that control the appearance of all subsequent plots in the current R session. It supports both setter mode (`par(col = "red")`) and getter-snapshot mode (`oldpar <- par(col = "red")`). If you forget to restore `par()` after a customisation, the changed parameter values **persist for the rest of the session** and may corrupt every subsequent plot ŌĆö for example, all following plots will remain permanently in the custom `mfrow` panel layout. *(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for definition, 1 for setter/snapshot distinction, 1 for restoration pitfall)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Choose A or B)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **You must answer EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B"** ŌĆö not both.

---

### **Question A ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of R's traditional graphics system**. Explain the role of the **graphics device driver**, **graphical parameters**, and **high-level vs. low-level functions**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following R dataset representing the monthly rainfall (in mm) recorded in Kochi for 12 months: `rainfall <- c(25, 30, 60, 150, 320, 700, 850, 600, 350, 180, 80, 35)`. Write a complete R script that:
   (i) Draws a **barplot** of this data with appropriate title, axis labels, and fill colour.
   (ii) Overlays a **horizontal line** indicating the annual mean rainfall.
   (iii) Annotates each bar with its numeric value using `text()`.
   (iv) Saves the final plot to a **PNG file** of resolution 150 dpi. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) ŌĆö 7-Mark Model Answer:**

The architecture of R's traditional graphics system follows a **device-driver model** (sometimes called the *painter's model*). The four key components are:

1. **Graphics Device Driver** ŌĆö This is the rendering target, e.g., `RStudioGD` for the IDE, `quartz()` for macOS, `windows()` for Windows, or file devices `pdf()`, `png()`, `jpeg()`. The driver translates R's internal drawing commands into actual pixels or vector primitives.

2. **Graphical Parameters** ŌĆö Controlled by the `par()` function, these are 72+ global options (`pch`, `cex`, `lwd`, `col`, `mfrow`, `mar`, `bty`, etc.) that govern *every* aesthetic decision for a plot.

3. **High-Level Functions** ŌĆö Create a brand-new plot, automatically handling axes, titles, and data plotting. Examples: `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`.

4. **Low-Level Functions** ŌĆö Add elements to an *existing* plot without erasing it. Examples: `points()`, `lines()`, `text()`, `legend()`, `abline()`.

A typical pipeline is: **Open device ŌåÆ Call high-level function ŌåÆ Use low-level functions to overlay ŌåÆ Close device with `dev.off()`**.

*(Award: 2 marks architecture diagram description, 2 marks device driver explanation, 1.5 marks high-level vs low-level, 1.5 marks pipeline.)*

**(b) ŌĆö 7-Mark Model Answer (Full R Script):**

```r
# (i) Create data
rainfall <- c(25, 30, 60, 150, 320, 700, 850, 600, 350, 180, 80, 35)
month_names <- month.abb                           # built-in month abbreviations

# (iv) Open PNG device FIRST (so we save the customised plot)
png(filename = "kochi_rainfall.png", width = 1000, height = 700, res = 150)

# (i) Barplot with aesthetics
bp <- barplot(rainfall,
              col       = "steelblue",
              border    = "white",
              main      = "Monthly Rainfall in Kochi (mm)",
              xlab      = "Month",
              ylab      = "Rainfall (mm)",
              names.arg = month_names,
              ylim      = c(0, max(rainfall) * 1.15),
              las       = 1)

# (ii) Horizontal line for annual mean
annual_mean <- mean(rainfall)
abline(h = annual_mean, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
text(x = par("usr")[2] * 0.95, y = annual_mean,
     labels = paste("Mean =", round(annual_mean, 1), "mm"),
     pos = 3, col = "red", cex = 0.9)

# (iii) Annotate each bar with its value
text(x = bp, y = rainfall, labels = rainfall, pos = 3, cex = 0.8, col = "black")

# (iv) Close device
dev.off()
cat("Plot saved to kochi_rainfall.png\n")
```

*(Award: 2 marks for barplot construction, 1.5 marks for abline + annotation, 1.5 marks for text() on bars, 2 marks for correct png()/dev.off() handling.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A)**
> 1. Students often open the `png()` device **after** drawing the plot ŌĆö this saves a *blank* file. Always `png()` **first**, plot, then `dev.off()`.
> 2. Forgetting `pos = 3` in `text()` makes labels appear *inside* the bars. Use `pos = 3` for *above* and `pos = 1` for *below*.
> 3. `barplot()` returns the x-coordinates of bar centres (stored in `bp`) ŌĆö passing `bp` to `text()` ensures perfect alignment.

---

### **Question B ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Explain the following graphical parameters in R with **default values and a one-line use-case** for each: `pch`, `lty`, `lwd`, `cex`, `col`, `mfrow`, `mar`, `bty`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A civil engineering experiment records the load (in kN) applied to a steel beam and the corresponding deflection (in mm):
`load <- c(0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30)`
`deflection <- c(0.0, 0.4, 0.8, 1.1, 1.5, 2.0, 2.6)`
Write a complete R script that:
   (i) Plots the **load-deflection curve** as a line plot with custom red colour and width 3.
   (ii) Adds appropriate title, axis labels (with engineering units), and a **legend** distinguishing the data.
   (iii) Draws a **regression line** (linear model) overlaid on the same plot in blue dashed style.
   (iv) Splits the device into a **2 ├Ś 1 panel layout** ŌĆö the top panel shows the regression overlay, the bottom shows a **histogram of the residuals**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) ŌĆö 7-Mark Model Answer Table:**

| Parameter | Default Value | One-Line Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
