| `pch` | 19 (filled circle) | `pch = 17` ŌåÆ filled triangle marker |
| `lty` | 1 (solid line) | `lty = 2` ŌåÆ dashed line for trend overlay |
| `lwd` | 1 (1 pixel) | `lwd = 3` ŌåÆ bold line for primary data |
| `cex` | 1.0 | `cex = 1.5` ŌåÆ enlarge all text by 50 % |
| `col` | `"black"` | `col = "steelblue"` ŌåÆ apply steel-blue fill |
| `mfrow` | `c(1, 1)` | `par(mfrow = c(2, 1))` ŌåÆ 2-row, 1-column panel |
| `mar` | `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)` | `par(mar = c(2, 2, 1, 1))` ŌåÆ shrink margins |
| `bty` | `"o"` (closed box) | `bty = "n"` ŌåÆ remove the bounding rectangle |

*(Award: 7 marks ŌĆö 0.75 per parameter, deduct 0.5 if default or use-case is missing.)*

**(b) ŌĆö 7-Mark Model Answer (Full R Script):**

```r
# (1) Data
load       <- c(0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30)
deflection <- c(0.0, 0.4, 0.8, 1.1, 1.5, 2.0, 2.6)

# (4) Fit linear model ONCE (needed for both regression line and residuals)
model    <- lm(deflection ~ load)
residuals <- resid(model)

# (4) Open 2x1 panel layout
oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

# --- TOP PANEL: Load-deflection curve with regression overlay ---
# (1) Line plot
plot(load, deflection,
     type = "l",
     col  = "red",
     lwd  = 3,
     main = "Load vs. Deflection of Steel Beam",
     xlab = "Load (kN)",
     ylab = "Deflection (mm)",
     ylim = c(0, max(deflection) * 1.1),
     xlim = c(0, max(load)),
     las  = 1)

# (2) Title and labels already set above; add legend
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Experimental Data", "Linear Regression"),
       col    = c("red", "blue"),
       lty    = c(1, 2),
       lwd    = c(3, 2),
       bty    = "n")

# (3) Overlay regression line in blue dashed
abline(model, col = "blue", lty = 2, lwd = 2)

# --- BOTTOM PANEL: Histogram of residuals ---
hist(residuals,
     col      = "lightgreen",
     border   = "white",
     main     = "Histogram of Regression Residuals",
     xlab     = "Residual (mm)",
     ylab     = "Frequency",
     las      = 1)
abline(v = 0, col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 2)

# Restore par
par(oldpar)
```

*(Award breakdown: 1.5 marks for line plot with custom aesthetics, 1.5 marks for legend & labels, 1.5 marks for regression overlay via abline, 2.5 marks for 2├Ś1 layout + residual histogram.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B)**
> 1. Fitting `lm()` **inside** each panel (a common copy-paste mistake) wastes marks ŌĆö fit it once and reuse.
> 2. `abline(model)` works only because the model object is of class `lm`. Using `abline(a, b)` for $\vert y = a + bx \vert$ is the other valid form ŌĆö but for the linear model use `abline(model)`.
> 3. The bottom panel's histogram must show **residuals**, not the original `deflection` ŌĆö many students confuse the two.
> 4. Forgetting to add `las = 1` makes Y-axis numbers horizontal ŌĆö a minor but real deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ŌŁÉ R has **two** graphics engines: *traditional* (`graphics` package) and *grid* (`grid` package). Module 3 = traditional only.
- ŌŁÉ **High-level** functions (`plot`, `hist`, `barplot`, `boxplot`, `pie`) start a **new** plot. **Low-level** functions (`points`, `lines`, `text`, `legend`, `abline`) **overlay** an existing plot.
- ŌŁÉ `par()` is the **master parameter controller** with 72 options. Always save/restore with `oldpar <- par(...)` ... `par(oldpar)`.
- ŌŁÉ **`pch`** (0ŌĆō25) controls point shape; `cex` is the expansion factor (default \vert 1\vert); `lty` (1ŌĆō6) is line type; `lwd` is line width; `col` is colour.
- ŌŁÉ **`mfrow = c(nr, nc)`** splits the device into a row-major grid; `mfcol` is column-major.
- ŌŁÉ **File-device workflow**: `pdf()` / `png()` / `jpeg()` / `tiff()` / `svg()` ŌåÆ plot ŌåÆ **mandatory** `dev.off()`.
- ŌŁÉ The **formula interface** `y ~ group` is used in `boxplot()`, `coplot()`, `xyplot()` to condition one variable on another.
- ŌŁÉ Default `mar` is `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)` lines; `oma` is for outer (super-title) margins.
- ŌŁÉ `expression()` is used to embed **Greek letters and math** in titles/labels: `expression(sigma), expression(beta[1])`.
- ŌŁÉ `bty` controls the box type; `las` controls axis-label orientation; `xaxt = "n"` suppresses the X-axis.
- ŌŁÉ `hist()` can plot *counts* (`freq = TRUE`) or *densities* (`freq = FALSE`); changing this changes the y-axis label automatically.
- ŌŁÉ `barplot()` accepts a *matrix* for grouped/stacked plots; use `beside = TRUE` for grouped, default is stacked.
- ŌŁÉ `pie()` is best for **Ōēż 7 categories**; otherwise prefer a `barplot`.
- ŌŁÉ **Residuals** of a linear model are obtained via `resid(model)` ŌĆö never confuse with `deflection` (the response).
- ŌŁÉ Two devices open simultaneously? Use `dev.list()`, `dev.cur()`, `dev.set(k)` to navigate.
- ŌŁÉ For **vector output** (publication quality), use `pdf()` or `svg()`. For **raster output** (web, slides), use `png()` or `jpeg()`.
- ŌŁÉ The Bell Labs **painter's model** means later drawing commands **paint over** earlier ones ŌĆö order matters in low-level overlays.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lattice Graphics - Lattice Functions and Customizing Lattice Graphics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT746 - PROGRAMMING IN R > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Lattice Graphics - Lattice Functions and Customizing Lattice Graphics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Lattice Graphics** is a powerful, high-level data visualization system implemented in R through the `lattice` package (originally developed by Deepayan Sarkar, building on the concepts of Cleveland's Trellis Graphics from Bell Labs). It provides a comprehensive framework for creating **conditioned plots** (multi-panel displays where a single plot is split into separate panels based on the levels of one or more categorical variables, called *shingles* or *factors*). In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECAT746 ŌĆō Programming in R**, Lattice Graphics is positioned as the second major visualization system after base R `graphics`, specifically engineered to handle the **"small multiples"** problem ŌĆö i.e., visualizing relationships across many subgroups simultaneously. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Box:** *Lattice* is an R package that uses a *formula-based interface* (e.g., `y ~ x | g`) to produce conditioned, multi-panel Trellis displays. The package is automatically loaded via `library(lattice)` and forms the foundation of the *grid* graphics model in R. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview ŌĆö The "Real World" Analogy Imagine you are a school principal analyzing the **marks distribution of 500 students across 5 sections (A, B, C, D, E)** in 3 subjects (Maths, Physics, Chemistry). If you plot everything in a single chart, you get a confusing mess. **The Base R Approach (like plotting 15 lines on one graph):** This is like trying to read 15 different conversations happening in one room ŌĆö impossible to isolate. **The Lattice Approach:** Think of it as a **"photo album with labeled sections"**. Instead of one giant photograph, you get a neatly arranged grid: - One panel per section (rows) - One panel per subject (columns) - Each panel is small, focused, and labeled - The axes are consistent across panels for easy comparison > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Lattice is to graphics what a **"pivot table"** is to data. Just as a pivot table automatically breaks down data by category, Lattice automatically breaks down plots by factor levels. The `|` symbol in the formula is your "split-by" operator. ## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (Mandatory KTU Terminology) | Term | Meaning | Example | |------|---------|---------| | **Trellis** | The conceptual framework of small-multiple conditioned plots | Cleveland's original 1993 design | | **Conditioning Variable** | The factor (or shingle) used to split panels | `group = c("A","B","C")` | | **Shingle** | A generalized factor allowing overlapping intervals | `equal.count(x, 4)` | | **Panel** | A single sub-plot within the lattice display | One mini-scatterplot | | **Strip** | The label bar above each panel showing its conditioning level | "Subject = Maths" | | **High-level Function** | A function that creates a complete plot | `xyplot()`, `bwplot()` | | **Low-level Function** | A function that adds elements to an existing plot | `llines()`, `lpoints()` | | **Panel Function** | The function that draws within each panel | `panel.xyplot()` | ## 1.4 Why Lattice? (KTU Exam Favourite Question) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Pillars of Lattice over Base Graphics:** > 1. **Conditioning is first-class** ŌĆö multi-panel layouts are native, not hacked. > 2. **Formula interface** ŌĆö the syntax `y ~ x | g` is self-documenting. > 3. **Grid-based engine** ŌĆö fine-grained graphical control via the `grid` package. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Lattice Formula Interface ŌĆö The Heart of the System Every high-level lattice function uses a **formula interface** in the form: $$\text{formula} = y \sim x \mid g_1 \, g_2$$ Where: - $y$ = response variable (plotted on Y-axis, optional for some functions) - $x$ = primary predictor (X-axis) - $g_1, g_2$ = **conditioning variables** (optional; create rows ├Ś columns of panels) - The `~` operator reads as "**is modelled by**" - The `|` operator reads as "**conditioned on**" ### Worked Example of Formula Anatomy ```r xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Petal.Width | Species, data = iris) ``` Decoded: *"Plot Sepal.Length vs Petal.Width, conditioned on Species"* - Y-axis: `Sepal.Length` - X-axis: `Petal.Width` - Conditioning: `Species` ŌåÆ 3 panels (setosa, versicolor, virginica) ## 2.2 The Complete Lattice Function Family (KTU High-Yield Table) | Lattice Function | Base R Equivalent | Primary Use Case | Formula Form | |------------------|-------------------|------------------|--------------| | `xyplot()` | `plot()` | **Scatter plots** | $y \sim x \mid g$ | | `bwplot()` | `boxplot()` | **Box-and-whisker plots** | $x \sim g$ or $y \sim x \mid g$ | | `histogram()` | `hist()` | **Histograms** | `~ x \mid g` | | `densityplot()` | `density()` + `plot()` | **Kernel density plots** | `~ x \mid g` | | `barchart()` | `barplot()` | **Bar charts** | $y \sim x$ | | `dotplot()` | `dotchart()` | **Cleveland dot plots** | $y \sim x$ | | `stripplot()` | `stripchart()` | **1-D scatter (jittered)** | `~ x \mid g` | | `qqmath()` | Custom | **Q-Q plot of theoretical distribution** | `~ x \mid g$ | | `qq()` | Custom | **Q-Q plot of two samples** | `y ~ x$ | | `splom()` | `pairs()` | **Scatter plot matrix** | Data frame as input | | `parallel()` | `parallel()` | **Parallel coordinates plot** | Data frame as input | | `cloud()` | Custom | **3-D scatter plot** | $z \sim x * y$ | | `wireframe()` | Custom | **3-D surface plot** | $z \sim x * y$ | ## 2.3 Customizing Lattice Graphics ŌĆö The Customization Matrix Customization in Lattice happens at **five distinct levels**, all examinable in KTU: ### Level 1: **Arguments to High-Level Functions** ```r xyplot(y ~ x | g, data = d, main = "Title", # Plot title xlab = "X Label", # X-axis label ylab = "Y Label", # Y-axis label xlim = c(0, 10), # X-axis limits ylim = c(0, 100), # Y-axis limits scales = list(x = "free"), # Free/fixed/equal axes layout = c(2, 3), # Columns=2, Rows=3 between = list(x = 0.5)) # Space between panels ``` ### Level 2: **Custom Panel Functions** This is the **most powerful** customization ŌĆö you control what happens *inside* each panel. ```r xyplot(y ~ x | g, data = d, panel = function(x, y, ...) { panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1) # Background grid panel.xyplot(x, y, ...) # Default scatter panel.lmline(x, y, col = "red") # Fitted regression line panel.rug(x, y) # Marginal rug marks }) ``` ### Level 3: **`trellis.par.set()` ŌĆö Theme Modification** Controls the **graphical parameters** (colors, line types, fonts, symbols) globally for the session. ```r trellis.par.set(theme = canonical.theme(color = FALSE)) # B/W theme trellis.par.set(list(plot.symbol = list(pch = 16, cex = 0.8))) show.settings() # Display current settings ``` ### Level 4: **`par.settings` Argument** Passes settings to a **single plot** without globally changing them. ```r xyplot(y ~ x | g, data = d, par.settings = list(superpose.symbol = list(pch = 16:18))) ``` ### Level 5: **`update()` and `print()` Methods** - `update(object, ...)` ŌĆö modify a stored lattice object. - `print(object, position = c(0,0,1,1), more = TRUE)` ŌĆö control multi-page output. ## 2.4 The Conditioning Shingle A **shingle** is a *continuous* generalization of a factor, used when conditioning on a numeric variable. ```r myshingle <- equal.count(iris$Sepal.Length, number = 4, overlap = 0.2) xyplot(Sepal.Width ~ Petal.Length | Sepal.Length_shingle, data = iris) ``` The `equal.count()` function creates shingles with a specified number of intervals and overlap. ## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Notes | |---------|------------------|-------| | Lattice formula | $y \sim x \mid g_1 + g_2$ | Y modelled by X, conditioned on G1 and G2 | | Custom panel signature | `panel = function(x, y, ..., subscripts, groups) { ... }` | The 5-argument form | | Layout specification | `layout = c(ncol, nrow)` | Always `(columns, rows)` | | Axis control | `scales = list(x = list(log = 10, relation = "free"))` | "same", "free", "sliced" | | Theme reset | `trellis.par.set(theme = col.whitebg())` | White background | | Legend groups | `auto.key = list(space = "right", text = c("A","B"))` | Auto-legend | | Strip control | `strip = strip.custom(bg = "lightblue", factor.labels = ...)` | Custom strip | | Shingle creation | `shingle(x, intervals = list(c(1,3), c(2,5)))` | Manual intervals | | Shingle via equal.count | `equal.count(x, number = 4, overlap = 0.25)` | Auto intervals | | Saving output | `print(plot_obj, position = c(0,0,1,1))` | `print()` is mandatory | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Lattice-style plots are extensively used in **quality engineering** (multi-process control charts), **biostatistics** (drug response across dose groups), and **machine learning** (per-class performance visualization) ŌĆö the small-multiples principle scales to thousands of panels via `useOuterStrips()`. ## 2.6 Groups and Superposition (Critical KTU Topic) When the data has a *grouping variable within each panel* (e.g., male vs female within each section): ```r xyplot(marks ~ study_hours | subject, data = d, groups = gender, auto.key = TRUE, panel = panel.superpose) # default; draws each group separately ``` For full control over per-group aesthetics: ```r panel = function(x, y, ..., groups, subscripts) { panel.superpose(x, y, ..., groups = groups, subscripts = subscripts, panel.groups = function(x, y, col, ...) { panel.xyplot(x, y, pch = 16, col = col, ...) panel.loess(x, y, col = col, lwd = 2) }) } ``` # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Logic & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Deriving a Custom Panel Function (Step-by-Step Walkthrough) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU Board Examiner's Mantra:** "Show the panel function signature explicitly; do not skip the `subscripts` argument when using grouped data." **Problem:** Create a lattice plot of `mpg` vs `wt` from the `mtcars` dataset, conditioned by `cyl`, that displays: 1. A grey background grid 2. Filled red points 3. A blue linear regression line 4. The correlation coefficient as text in the top-left corner ### Step 1 ŌĆö Load the Package and Inspect the Formula ```r library(lattice) # The intended formula: mpg ~ wt | cyl # This gives 3 panels: 4-cyl, 6-cyl, 8-cyl ``` **Logic:** The formula `y ~ x | g` tells lattice to map `g` to panels, `x` to horizontal axis, `y` to vertical axis. ### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Per-Panel Correlation in a Custom Panel ```r panel.cor <- function(x, y, ...) { # Step 2a: Draw the background grid panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1, lty = 3, col = "grey80") # Step 2b: Draw the default scatter points panel.xyplot(x, y, pch = 16, col = "red", cex = 0.8, ...) # Step 2c: Add the regression line (lm fit) panel.lmline(x, y, col = "blue", lwd = 2) # Step 2d: Compute and display correlation r <- cor(x, y, use = "complete.obs") r_text <- sprintf("r = %.3f", r) panel.text(0.05, 0.95, r_text, adj = c(0, 1), font = 2, # Bold cex = 1.0) } ``` **Logic Walkthrough ŌĆö Argument by Argument:** | Argument | Purpose | Why It Matters | |----------|---------|----------------| | `x, y` | Numeric vectors for current panel's data | Lattice passes panel data automatically | | `...` | Forward compatibility | Pass through to sub-functions | | `panel.grid(h,v)` | `-1` means "place at all tick marks" | Standard for grid look | | `panel.xyplot()` | The default scatter | Re-using built-in is robust | | `panel.lmline()` | Linear model fit line | Smoothing/regression helper | | `panel.text(x_rel, y_rel, ...)` | Coordinates in [0,1] are *NDC* (normalized device coordinates) | Crucial KTU point: lattice text uses NDC! | | `adj = c(0,1)` | Horizontal=0 (left), Vertical=1 (top) | Anchor text in top-left corner | ### Step 3 ŌĆö Combine into the Final Plot ```r xyplot(mpg ~ wt | factor(cyl), data = mtcars, panel = panel.cor, main = "Fuel Efficiency vs Weight by Cylinder Count", xlab = "Weight (1000 lbs)", ylab = "Miles per Gallon", layout = c(3, 1), # 3 columns, 1 row par.strip.text = list(cex = 1.2, font = 2), scales = list(alternating = 1)) ``` **Logic of Layout:** `layout = c(ncol, nrow)`. With 3 unique cylinder values and `c(3,1)`, we get a single row of 3 side-by-side panels. ### Step 4 ŌĆö Render with `print()` for Full Control ```r # Mandatory for scripts (not needed at prompt) print(trellis.last.object()) ``` ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Built-in Panel Functions Reference | Panel Function | Visual Output | Typical KTU Use | |----------------|---------------|-----------------| | `panel.xyplot(x,y)` | Scatter points | Default for `xyplot` | | `panel.points(x,y)` | Points (low-level) | Adding extra points | | `panel.lines(x,y)` | Connected lines | Adding a path | | `panel.segments(x1,y1,x2,y2)` | Line segments | Arrows, error bars | | `panel.text(x,y,labels)` | Text labels | Annotations | | `panel.abline(a,b)` | Straight line $y = a + bx$ | Reference lines | | `panel.lmline(x,y)` | OLS regression line | Trend overlay | | `panel.loess(x,y)` | LOESS smooth curve | Non-linear trend | | `panel.average(x,y,factor)` | Group means with CI | Grouped summaries | | `panel.smooth(x,y)` | LOESS with default span | Quick smooth | | `panel.bwplot(...)` | Box plot components | Inside `bwplot` | | `panel.violin(...)` | Violin plot | Distribution shape | | `panel.rug(x,y)` | Marginal rug ticks | Density indicator | | `panel.grid(h,v)` | Background grid lines | Visual reference | | `panel.superpose(...)` | Multi-group overlay | For `groups =` | | `panel.stripplot(...)` | Jittered 1-D points | For `stripplot` | | `panel.histogram(...)` | Histogram rectangles | For `histogram` | | `panel.densityplot(...)` | Kernel density | For `densityplot` | ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Creating a `bwplot` with Custom Strip Labels **Problem:** Create a side-by-side boxplot of `Sepal.Length` by `Species`, but rename the strip labels to uppercase and change the strip background to light blue. ```r # Step 1: Define the label-translation function label_map <- c(setosa = "SETOSA", versicolor = "VERSICOLOR", virginica = "VIRGINICA") # Step 2: Build the strip my_strip <- strip.custom( factor.labels = label_map, # Map factor levels to new labels bg = "lightblue", # Background color text.cex = 1.1, # Text size text.font = 2) # Bold text # Step 3: Assemble the plot bwplot(Sepal.Length ~ Species, data = iris, strip = my_strip, main = "Custom Strip Labels", xlab = "Iris Species", ylab = "Sepal Length (cm)", par.settings = list( box.umbrella = list(col = "darkred", lwd = 2), box.rectangle = list(fill = "lightyellow", col = "darkred") )) ``` **Derivation Logic:** The `strip.custom()` function is derived from the parameterization of Trellis strips in the underlying `grid` layout system. Internally, the strip is a `grid.rect` followed by a `grid.text`. The arguments are: - `factor.labels` ŌĆö overrides auto-generated labels (allows non-default names) - `bg` ŌĆö fill color of the strip rectangle - `text.cex` and `text.font` ŌĆö text appearance - `par.strip.text` ŌĆö alternative global control via list of graphical parameters ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Multi-Page Conditioning with `page = ` **Problem:** With many factor levels, we need a controlled multi-page output. ```r xyplot(mpg ~ wt | factor(cyl), data = mtcars, layout = c(2, 2), # 2x2 grid page = function(n) { cat("Showing panel group", n, "\n") TRUE # Return TRUE to continue, FALSE to stop }) ``` The `page` argument accepts a function of `n` (current page number). It is **invoked after each page** is drawn. Returning `FALSE` halts the device. This is critical for **batch report generation** in production. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Differences Between Lattice and Base Graphics | Feature | Base `graphics` | Lattice `lattice` | |---------|----------------|-------------------| | Underlying engine | Base R plotting | `grid` package | | Multi-panel facility | `par(mfrow)` (manual) | Formula-driven (automatic) | | Formula interface | None | $y \sim x \mid g$ | | Adding to existing plot | `points()`, `lines()` | `panel.points()`, `panel.lines()` | | Annotation | `text(x, y, ...)` | `panel.text(x, y, ...)` in NDC or data | | Axis control | `axis()` after plot | `scales = list(...)` argument | | Theme persistence | `par()` | `trellis.par.set()` | | Output object | None (side-effect) | A `trellis` class object | | Saving | `dev.copy` | `print(plot, position=...)` | | Memory of panels | No | Yes ŌĆö the object is fully re-playable | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture of the Lattice System (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[User Data Frame
data = argument] --> B[Lattice Formula
y ~ x | g1 g2] B --> C{Lattice Engine
trellis.focus} C --> D[Shingle / Factor Splitter] D --> E1[Panel 1
subset of data] D --> E2[Panel 2
subset of data] D --> E3[Panel 3
subset of data] D --> E4[Panel N
subset of data] E1 --> F[Custom Panel Function
panel = function] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G[Low-level Panel Helpers
panel.xyplot, panel.lmline] G --> H[Grid Graphics Engine
grid package] H --> I[Graphical Output Device
screen, PDF, PNG] J[trellis.par.set] -.-> F K[par.settings] -.-> F L[strip / strip.custom] -.-> F M[scales / layout / xlim] -.-> F ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow: Which Lattice Function to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([You have a dataset and want to plot]) --> Q1{What is the
primary variable type?} Q1 -->|Two numeric variables| XY[xyplot] Q1 -->|One numeric, one categorical| BW[bwplot] Q1 -->|One numeric, want histogram| HI[histogram] Q1 -->|One numeric, want smooth density| DE[densityplot] Q1 -->|Want 1-D jittered points| SP[stripplot] Q1 -->|Want Q-Q plot vs theory| QQ[qqmath] Q1 -->|Compare two samples' Q-Q| Q2[qq] Q1 -->|Many numeric variables| SPL[splom scatter matrix] Q1 -->|3 numeric variables| CL[cloud or wireframe] XY --> Sub{Grouping by
a factor?} BW --> Sub HI --> Sub DE --> Sub Sub -->|Yes, split panels| Pipe[Add | g to formula] Sub -->|Yes, overlay within panel| Grp[Add groups = argument] Pipe --> Custom{Customize
panel contents?} Grp --> Custom Custom -->|Yes| CPF[Write panel = function] Custom -->|No| Done([Default rendering]) CPF --> Done ``` ## 4.3 Anatomy of a Lattice Custom Panel Function ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Panel Function Called by Lattice Engine] --> B[Receive x, y, ..., subscripts, groups] B --> C[Step 1: Set Up Canvas
panel.grid] B --> D[Step 2: Plot Data
panel.xyplot or panel.points] B --> E[Step 3: Add Statistics
panel.lmline, panel.loess] B --> F[Step 4: Annotate
panel.text, panel.abline] B --> G[Step 5: Add Decorations
panel.rug, panel.segments] C --> H[Return to Lattice Engine] D --> H E --> H F --> H G --> H H --> I[Render to Grid Device] ``` ## 4.4 The Conditioning Variable Resolution Path ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Formula
y ~ x | g] --> B[Parse
terms object] B --> C{Right-hand side
of |} C -->|factor| D[Split by levels] C -->|numeric| E[equal.count
to shingle] C -->|shingle| F[Use intervals directly] D --> G[layout c ncol nrow] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Loop over
panel groups] H --> I[Each panel gets
x, y subset] I --> J[Panel function executes] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Style] **What is meant by "conditioning" in Lattice graphics? Explain with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Conditioning in Lattice refers to the technique of creating **multi-panel plots** where each panel corresponds to a level (or interval) of one or more categorical (or continuous, via shingles) variables. The conditioning variable is specified on the **right-hand side of the `|` operator** in the lattice formula. **Example:** ```r xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Petal.Length | Species, data = iris) ``` This produces three panels ŌĆö one each for the three species of iris ŌĆö all sharing common axes for easy visual comparison. (3 marks) ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024 Style] **Distinguish between `panel.xyplot()` and `panel.points()`. When would you use each?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - `panel.xyplot()` is a **high-level** panel function that draws the **default** scatter plot of the panel's data using the active graphical settings (from `par.settings` or `trellis.par.set`). It is the default panel function for `xyplot()`. (1.5 marks) - `panel.points()` is a **low-level** panel function analogous to base R's `points()`. It draws additional points on top of an existing panel and is used to **add** extra data to a customized panel. (1.5 marks) **Usage:** Use `panel.xyplot()` as the primary rendering call inside a custom panel; use `panel.points()` to overlay extra annotations or additional data subsets. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper Pattern]** > > **(a)** Explain the **Lattice formula interface** in detail. With a complete R code example, demonstrate how the formula `y ~ x | g` creates a conditioned multi-panel plot. Describe the role of the `data` argument. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With a suitable R program, show how to **customize a lattice plot** using: (i) a custom panel function that adds a regression line and correlation coefficient, and (ii) `trellis.par.set()` to modify plot symbols globally. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) The Lattice Formula Interface (7 marks):** The Lattice formula interface uses the syntax $y \sim x \mid g$ where: - $\sim$ reads as *"is modelled by"* ŌĆö analogous to regression formula - $\vert$ reads as *"conditioned on"* ŌĆö splits the plot into panels ```r library(lattice) # Formula: Ozone ~ Wind | Month # Y = Ozone (response), X = Wind (predictor), G = Month (conditioning) xyplot(Ozone ~ Wind | Month, data = airquality, layout = c(5, 1), # 5 months in one row main = "Ozone vs Wind Speed by Month", xlab = "Wind Speed (mph)", ylab = "Ozone (ppb)") ``` **Role of `data`:** The `data` argument is a data frame that hosts the variables referenced in the formula. Lattice looks up names in the formula within this data frame, so columns are referenced **without quotes or `$` extraction**. This makes the formula self-contained and reproducible. (7 marks) **(b) Custom Panel Function + Global Theme (7 marks):** ```r library(lattice) # (i) Custom panel function with regression and correlation panel.cor.reg <- function(x, y, ...) { panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1, col = "grey90", lty = 3) panel.xyplot(x, y, pch = 16, col = "steelblue", cex = 0.9, ...) panel.lmline(x, y, col = "darkred", lwd = 2) r <- cor(x, y, use = "complete.obs") panel.text(0.05, 0.95, sprintf("r = %.3f", r), adj = c(0, 1), font = 2, cex = 1) } plot_obj <- xyplot(mpg ~ wt | factor(cyl), data = mtcars, panel = panel.cor.reg, main = "MPG vs Weight (with correlation)") # (ii) Global theme modification trellis.par.set(list( plot.symbol = list(pch = 17, cex = 1.0, col = "darkgreen"), plot.line = list(lwd = 2, col = "purple"), strip.background = list(col = "lightyellow"), strip.text = list(font = 2, cex = 1.1) )) # Render print(plot_obj) ``` The custom panel adds a regression line and correlation text; `trellis.par.set()` modifies the global theme affecting all subsequent lattice plots. (7 marks) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper Pattern]** > > **(a)** List and briefly describe **any five high-level Lattice functions** with their respective formula forms. For each, give one engineering/statistical application. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Write an R program to create a `bwplot` (box-and-whisker plot) of `Sepal.Length` grouped by `Species` from the `iris` dataset, with: (i) custom strip labels in uppercase, (ii) light blue box fill, (iii) the plot titled "Iris Sepal Length Distribution", and (iv) a custom legend indicating the species names. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Five High-Level Lattice Functions (7 marks):** 1. **`xyplot(y ~ x | g)`** ŌĆö Scatter plot for two numeric variables. *Engineering application:* Visualizing stressŌĆōstrain relationships across different alloys. (1.4 marks) 2. **`bwplot(y ~ x | g)`** ŌĆö Box-and-whisker plot. *Engineering application:* Comparing batch quality control measurements across production lines. (1.4 marks) 3. **`histogram(~ x | g)`** ŌĆö Histogram with automatic frequency/density option. *Application:* Distribution of component lifetimes under different temperature conditions. (1.4 marks) 4. **`densityplot(~ x | g)`** ŌĆö Smoothed kernel density estimate. *Application:* Visualizing probability densities of process variation. (1.4 marks) 5. **`splom(~ data[,-5])`** ŌĆö Scatter plot matrix. *Application:* Multivariate correlation exploration in sensor data. (1.4 marks) **(b) Custom `bwplot` Implementation (7 marks):** ```r library(lattice) # (i) Custom strip labels (uppercase) upper_strip <- strip.custom( factor.labels = c(setosa = "SETOSA", versicolor = "VERSICOLOR", virginica = "VIRGINICA"), bg = "steelblue", text.col = "white", text.font = 2, text.cex = 1.1 ) # (ii-iv) The complete bwplot bwplot(Sepal.Length ~ Species, data = iris, main = "Iris Sepal Length Distribution", xlab = "Species", ylab = "Sepal Length (cm)", strip = upper_strip, par.settings = list( box.rectangle = list(fill = "lightblue", col = "darkblue", lwd = 1.5), box.umbrella = list(col = "darkblue", lwd = 1.5), plot.symbol = list(pch = 16, cex = 0.7, col = "darkblue") ), auto.key = list(title = "Species", text = c("Setosa", "Versicolor", "Virginica"), space = "top", columns = 3)) ``` **Explanation:** `strip.custom` provides the uppercase labels and steel-blue strip background; `par.settings` controls the box fill, border, and outlier symbol appearance; `auto.key` constructs an automatic legend with the species names at the top of the plot. (7 marks) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Lattice Questions (KTU 2024):** > > 1. **Forgetting `library(lattice)`** ŌĆö Causes "could not find function" error. **ŌłÆ1 mark** > 2. **Forgetting to call `print()` on the lattice object in scripts** ŌĆö Lattice objects must be explicitly rendered; the `print()` call is **mandatory** when running from a source file, but auto-prints at the console. **ŌłÆ1 mark** > 3. **Confusing `panel.text` coordinate system** ŌĆö `panel.text` in lattice uses *normalized parent coordinates* (NDC, range [0,1]) **OR** data coordinates depending on how it's called inside the panel. Students mix this up constantly. **ŌłÆ2 marks** > 4. **Using `points()` or `lines()` instead of `panel.points()` / `panel.lines()`** ŌĆö Base R low-level functions do not respect the lattice panel coordinate system correctly. **ŌłÆ2 marks** > 5. **Not passing `subscripts` argument** in grouped custom panels ŌĆö Causes incorrect group rendering. **ŌłÆ2 marks** > 6. **Wrong order in `layout`** ŌĆö Lattice expects `layout = c(ncol, nrow)`, not `(nrow, ncol)`. This is a common Lattice-vs-Base-graphics confusion. **ŌłÆ1 mark** > 7. **Forgetting `groups =` in `auto.key`** ŌĆö When using `auto.key = TRUE` with groups, you must include `groups = ` in the main call. **ŌłÆ1 mark** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ­¤ö╣ **Lattice** = high-level, formula-based, multi-panel Trellis graphics system in R (package: `lattice`). - ­¤ö╣ **Formula syntax** is $y \sim x \mid g$ ŌĆö read as "Y modelled by X, conditioned on G". - ­¤ö╣ **High-level functions**: `xyplot`, `bwplot`, `histogram`, `densityplot`, `barchart`, `dotplot`, `stripplot`, `qqmath`, `qq`, `splom`, `parallel`, `cloud`, `wireframe`. - ­¤ö╣ **Low-level / panel functions**: `panel.xyplot`, `panel.points`, `panel.lines`, `panel.abline`, `panel.lmline`, `panel.loess`, `panel.grid`, `panel.text`, `panel.rug`, `panel.superpose`. - ­¤ö╣ **Customization is 5-layered**: (1) high-level arguments, (2) custom panel functions, (3) `trellis.par.set()`, (4) `par.settings`, (5) `update()` / `print()`. - ­¤ö╣ **Shingles** = continuous generalization of factors, created via `shingle()` or `equal.count()`. - ­¤ö╣ **`layout = c(ncol, nrow)`** ŌĆö note the **column-first** order. - ­¤ö╣ **`strip.custom()`** for custom strip labels/colors. - ­¤ö╣ **`auto.key`** for automatic legends when using `groups =`. - ­¤ö╣ **Mandatory `print()`** for lattice objects in non-interactive scripts. - ­¤ö╣ **Lattice is built on `grid`** ŌĆö for fine-grained control beyond panel functions, drop down to `grid` calls. - ­¤ö╣ **The `subscripts` argument** is **essential** in custom panels when using groups. - ­¤ö╣ **panel.text coordinates** can be data or NDC (0ŌĆō1) ŌĆö choose carefully. - ­¤ö╣ **For KTU 14-mark answers**, always provide: (a) clear concept, (b) complete R code with `library()` call, (c) expected output description, (d) customization explanation.

topics.title: Ggplot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT746 - PROGRAMMING IN R
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ggplot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**ggplot2** is a powerful, declarative data visualization package for the R programming language, developed by **Hadley Wickham** as an implementation of Leland Wilkinson's **Grammar of Graphics**. It belongs to the `tidyverse` ecosystem and is the de-facto standard for publication-quality statistical graphics in R.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"ggplot2 is a plotting system for R, based on the grammar of graphics, which builds plots by layering independent components ŌĆö data, aesthetic mappings, geometric objects, statistical transformations, scales, coordinate systems, faceting, and themes."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **house** using prefabricated components:

- The **land/blueprint** is your dataset.
- The **walls, doors, windows** are geometric shapes (points, bars, lines).
- The **paint, textures, colors** are aesthetic mappings (aes).
- The **floors and rooms** are facets (sub-plots).
- The **interior design** is the theme.

You don't write a single imperative instruction like *"draw a pixel here"*. Instead, you **declare the components and stack them using `+`**. The grammar automatically composes them into a coherent visualization. This is the power of **declarative grammar-based graphics**.

## 1.3 Why ggplot2 in R?

| Feature | Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Layered Architecture** | Build complex plots incrementally |
| **Aesthetic Consistency** | Variables map uniformly to visual properties |
| **Statistical Integration** | Built-in `stat_` functions (smooth, summary) |
| **Faceting Engine** | Automatic small-multiples (Trellis plots) |
| **Theme System** | Full control over non-data ink |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Mnemonic ŌĆö "GLASS-FT":** The 7 layers of Grammar of Graphics in ggplot2 are: **G**eoms, **L**abels, **A**esthetics, **S**tats, **S**cales, **F**acets, **T**hemes.

## 1.4 Installation & Loading

```r
# Install once
install.packages("ggplot2")

# Load in every session
library(ggplot2)

# Modern approach via tidyverse
library(tidyverse)
```

> [!TIP]
> Always load `ggplot2` (or `tidyverse`) at the **top of your R script** before any plotting code, otherwise `ggplot()` will not be found.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Grammar of Graphics ŌĆö Layered Architecture

ggplot2 constructs a plot by **adding independent layers** using the `+` operator. Each layer is a self-contained specification of:

1. **Data** ŌĆö the tibble/data.frame holding the variables.
2. **Aesthetic Mappings (`aes()`)** ŌĆö how variables map to visual properties.
3. **Geometric Object (`geom_*`)** ŌĆö what shape to draw.
4. **Statistical Transformation (`stat_*`)** ŌĆö summary computation.
5. **Position Adjustment (`position_*`)** ŌĆö how overlapping elements are placed.
6. **Coordinate System (`coord_*`)** ŌĆö Cartesian, polar, map projections.
7. **Faceting (`facet_*`)** ŌĆö small multiples.
8. **Theme (`theme_*`)** ŌĆö non-data appearance.

## 2.2 The Master Template (Canonical Formula)

```r
ggplot(data = <DATA>, mapping = aes(<MAPPINGS>)) +
  <GEOM_FUNCTION>(mapping = aes(<MAPPINGS>), stat = <STAT>,
                  position = <POSITION>) +
  <COORDINATE_FUNCTION> +
  <FACET_FUNCTION> +
  <SCALE_FUNCTION> +
  <THEME_FUNCTION>
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Layer | Function Signature | Purpose / Default |
|---|---|---|
| **Initialize** | `ggplot(data, aes(...))` | Creates empty canvas; data is **mandatory** |
| **Aesthetics** | `aes(x, y, color, fill, shape, size, alpha, group, linetype)` | Maps variables ŌåÆ visual channels |
| **Geom Point** | `geom_point()` | Scatter plot ŌĆö default stat = `identity` |
| **Geom Line** | `geom_line()` | Time series / connected points |
| **Geom Bar** | `geom_bar()` | Counts rows per category (stat = `count`) |
| **Geom Col** | `geom_col()` | Bar with explicit y values (stat = `identity`) |
| **Geom Histogram** | `geom_histogram(bins = 30)` | Frequency distribution (stat = `bin`) |
| **Geom Boxplot** | `geom_boxplot()` | 5-number summary |
| **Geom Smooth** | `geom_smooth(method = "lm")` | Fits regression line + CI ribbon |
| **Scale Color** | `scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")` | Discrete color palettes |
| **Scale Continuous** | `scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0,100,10))` | Numeric axis control |
| **Facet Wrap** | `facet_wrap(~variable, nrow = 2)` | 1-D small multiples |
| **Facet Grid** | `facet_grid(rows ~ cols)` | 2-D grid of panels |
| **Theme** | `theme_minimal()`, `theme_bw()`, `theme_classic()` | Pre-built visual styles |
| **Labels** | `labs(title, x, y, color, fill)` | Annotations |
| **Save** | `ggsave("plot.png", width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)` | Export to file |

## 2.4 Aesthetic Mapping vs. Aesthetic Setting

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students confuse **mapping** (variable-dependent) with **setting** (constant value).
> - **Inside `aes()`** ŌåÆ variable drives the property: `aes(color = species)`
> - **Outside `aes()`** ŌåÆ fixed value: `geom_point(color = "blue")`

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **EDA (Exploratory Data Analysis)** in ML pipelines before model fitting.
- **Quality Control charts** in manufacturing (SPC, control limits via `geom_hline`).
- **Signal processing** ŌĆö `geom_line()` for time-series sensor data.
- **Statistical reporting** ŌĆö `geom_smooth()` for regression visualization.
- **Geospatial analysis** ŌĆö pairs with `sf` package for maps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Building a Scatter Plot Layer-by-Layer

We will derive a publication-quality scatter plot with regression line, faceting, and themed labels step by step. Assume the built-in `mpg` dataset (fuel economy data for 38 car models).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Base Canvas (Data + Aesthetic Mapping)

```r
library(ggplot2)

p1 <- ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))
```

**Logic:** We declare the **dataset** (`mpg`) and the **aesthetic mapping** (engine displacement on X, highway mileage on Y). The canvas is empty because no geom is specified yet.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Add a Geometric Layer (Points)

```r
p2 <- p1 + geom_point(mapping = aes(color = class))
```

**Logic:** We add a scatter geom. The `color = class` aesthetic maps the vehicle category to a discrete color channel. ggplot2 automatically picks a default hue palette and builds a legend.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Add a Statistical Layer (Smoothed Trend)

```r
p3 <- p2 + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "black")
```

**Logic:** `geom_smooth()` computes a linear model (`method = "lm"`) and draws the fitted line plus a **shaded 95 % confidence interval** (`se = TRUE`). The fixed color is set **outside** `aes()`.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Add Faceting (Small Multiples by Drive Type)

```r
p4 <- p3 + facet_wrap(~drv, nrow = 1)
```

**Logic:** `facet_wrap(~drv)` splits the plot into **3 panels** (4wd, fwd, rwd) ŌĆö one per drive-train type. The formula `~drv` is a tilde-notation specifying the faceting variable.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Add Scales (Custom Axes)

```r
p5 <- p4 +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, by = 1), name = "Engine Displacement (L)") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(10, 50, by = 5), name = "Highway MPG")
```

**Logic:** `scale_x_continuous()` and `scale_y_continuous()` provide explicit tick breaks and human-readable axis titles. This is critical for KTU examiner credit on axis labeling.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Add Labels and Theme

```r
p6 <- p5 +
  labs(
    title    = "Fuel Economy vs. Engine Size",
    subtitle = "Faceted by Drive Train",
    color    = "Vehicle Class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

**Logic:** `labs()` adds annotations; `theme_minimal()` strips away the gray background and gridlines for a clean, modern look. `base_size = 12` sets the default font size.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Render and Save

```r
print(p6)
ggsave("mpg_plot.png", plot = p6, width = 8, height = 4, dpi = 300)
```

**Logic:** `print()` displays the plot in the RStudio viewer; `ggsave()` exports a high-resolution PNG suitable for printing or report embedding.

### Step 8 ŌĆö Complete Production-Ready Code

```r
library(ggplot2)

final_plot <- ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = class), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "black",
              linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~drv, nrow = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, by = 1),
                     name = "Engine Displacement (L)") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(10, 50, by = 5),
                     name = "Highway MPG") +
  labs(title    = "Fuel Economy vs. Engine Size",
       subtitle = "Faceted by Drive Train",
       color    = "Vehicle Class") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

print(final_plot)
ggsave("mpg_plot.png", plot = final_plot, width = 8, height = 4, dpi = 300)
```

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix ŌĆö `geom_bar()` vs `geom_col()` vs `geom_histogram()`

| Property | `geom_bar()` | `geom_col()` | `geom_histogram()` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Default `stat`** | `count` (tabulates rows) | `identity` (uses raw y) | `bin` (creates bins) |
| **Requires y aesthetic?** | No (y is auto-counted) | **Yes** (explicit y) | No (y is auto-counted) |
| **X variable type** | Categorical (factor/character) | Categorical | Continuous (numeric) |
| **Bin argument** | N/A | N/A | `bins = 30` or `binwidth = 5` |
| **Typical use** | Frequency of categories | Comparing known values | Distribution of numeric data |
| **KTU Code Snippet** | `geom_bar(aes(x = cyl))` | `geom_col(aes(x = cyl, y = mean_mpg))` | `geom_histogram(aes(x = mpg), bins = 20)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question says *"draw a bar chart of counts"*, use `geom_bar()`. If it says *"draw a bar chart of values"*, use `geom_col()`. Mixing them is a **frequent 2-mark deduction**.

## 3.3 Derivation: How `aes()` Inheritance Works

When you place `aes()` in `ggplot()`, **every subsequent layer inherits** those mappings. You can **override** or **add** mappings inside each `geom_*`:

$$
\text{Layer Mapping} = \text{Global Mapping} \cup \text{Local Override}
$$

```r
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +   # Global
  geom_point() +                                          # Inherits all
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black")             # Overrides color
```

**Logic Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Aes}_{\text{point}} &= \{x = displ,\; y = hwy,\; color = class\} \\
\text{Aes}_{\text{smooth}} &= \{x = displ,\; y = hwy,\; color = black\} \quad \text{(override)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ggplot2 Layered Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["LAYER 1 DATA Input tibble or data frame"] --> B["LAYER 2 AESTHETIC MAPPING aes x y color fill shape size"]
    B --> C["LAYER 3 GEOMETRIC OBJECTS geom point line bar histogram boxplot smooth"]
    C --> D["LAYER 4 STATISTICAL TRANSFORM stat identity count bin smooth summary"]
    D --> E["LAYER 5 SCALES scale x continuous scale color brewer scale log10"]
    E --> F["LAYER 6 COORDINATE SYSTEM coord cartesian coord flip coord polar"]
    F --> G["LAYER 7 FACETING facet wrap facet grid"]
    G --> H["LAYER 8 THEME theme minimal theme bw theme classic"]
    H --> I["FINAL RENDERED PLOT Output to screen or ggsave"]
    J["ADD LAYERS via plus operator"] -.-> C
    J -.-> D
    J -.-> E
    J -.-> F
    J -.-> G
    J -.-> H
```

## 4.2 Geom Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START What do you want to plot"] --> B["One numeric variable"]
    A --> C["Two numeric variables"]
    A --> D["One categorical variable"]
    A --> E["Numeric vs categorical"]
    A --> F["Distribution shape"]
    B --> B1["geom_histogram or geom_density"]
    C --> C1["geom_point or geom_line"]
    D --> D1["geom_bar counts"]
    E --> E1["geom_col values or geom_boxplot"]
    F --> F1["geom_violin or geom_boxplot"]
    B1 --> G["Add stat layer if needed"]
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G
    G --> H["Add facet wrap or facet grid"]
    H --> I["Apply theme minimal or theme bw"]
    I --> J["Render and ggsave"]
```

## 4.3 Aesthetic Mapping Inheritance Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GLOBAL["GLOBAL aes in ggplot call"]
    G1["x displ"]
    G2["y hwy"]
    G3["color class"]
    end
    subgraph LAYER1["LAYER 1 geom point"]
    P1["Inherits x y color"]
    P2["Adds size alpha"]
    end
    subgraph LAYER2["LAYER 2 geom smooth"]
    S1["Inherits x y"]
    S2["Overrides color black"]
    end
    GLOBAL --> LAYER1
    GLOBAL --> LAYER2
    LAYER1 --> OUT["FINAL PLOT"]
    LAYER2 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Grammar of Graphics Component Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DATA["DATA LAYER"]
    D1["mpg"]
    D2["diamonds"]
    D3["iris"]
    end
    subgraph AESTHETIC["AESTHETIC LAYER"]
    A1["x position"]
    A2["y position"]
    A3["color hue"]
    A4["shape category"]
    A5["size magnitude"]
    end
    subgraph GEOM["GEOMETRY LAYER"]
    G1["geom_point"]
    G2["geom_bar"]
    G3["geom_line"]
    G4["geom_boxplot"]
    G5["geom_histogram"]
    end
    subgraph THEME["NON DATA LAYER"]
    T1["theme minimal"]
    T2["labs title"]
    T3["coord flip"]
    T4["facet wrap"]
    end
    DATA --> AESTHETIC
    AESTHETIC --> GEOM
    GEOM --> THEME
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Define the Grammar of Graphics. List any four components of the grammar as implemented in ggplot2.**

**Model Answer:**
The Grammar of Graphics, proposed by Leland Wilkinson, is a conceptual framework that decomposes any statistical graphic into a set of **semantic components** layered together. ggplot2 implements this grammar in R. The key components are:
1. **Data** ŌĆö the input dataset.
2. **Aesthetic Mappings (`aes()`)** ŌĆö assignment of variables to visual properties (x, y, color, size, shape).
3. **Geometric Objects (`geom_*`)** ŌĆö the visual marks representing data (points, bars, lines).
4. **Statistical Transformations (`stat_*`)** ŌĆö summaries like counts, bins, smooths.
5. **Scales** ŌĆö control of how aesthetic values map to perceptual values.
6. **Coordinate System** ŌĆö Cartesian, polar, or map projection.
7. **Faceting** ŌĆö small-multiple sub-plots.
8. **Theme** ŌĆö non-data visual styling.

*(Any four fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Differentiate between `geom_bar()` and `geom_col()` with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `geom_bar()` | `geom_col()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Default stat** | `count` ŌĆö auto-tabulates rows | `identity` ŌĆö uses raw y values |
| **Y aesthetic** | Not required | **Required** |
| **Use case** | Frequency of categories | Plotting pre-computed values |
| **Example** | `geom_bar(aes(x = cyl))` shows count of cars per cylinder | `geom_col(aes(x = cyl, y = mean_mpg))` shows average MPG per cylinder |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of ggplot2 with a neat block diagram. List any **five aesthetic mappings** used in `aes()`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the `mtcars` dataset in R, write a complete R program using `ggplot2` to draw a **scatter plot of `wt` vs `mpg`**, color the points by `cyl` (number of cylinders), add a linear regression smooth line, and apply `theme_minimal()`. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Layered Architecture:**

ggplot2 follows a **declarative layered grammar**. A plot is built by adding independent components using the `+` operator on top of the base `ggplot()` call.

| # | Layer | Function | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data | `ggplot(data, ...)` | Source tibble/data.frame |
| 2 | Aesthetic | `aes(x, y, color, fill, shape, size, linetype, alpha, group)` | Variable ŌåÆ visual channel |
| 3 | Geom | `geom_point()`, `geom_line()`, `geom_bar()` | Marks on canvas |
| 4 | Stat | `stat_smooth()`, `stat_count()`, `stat_bin()` | Summary computations |
| 5 | Scale | `scale_x_continuous()`, `scale_color_brewer()` | Axis/legend mapping |
| 6 | Coord | `coord_cartesian()`, `coord_flip()` | Coordinate system |
| 7 | Facet | `facet_wrap(~var)`, `facet_grid(rows ~ cols)` | Small multiples |
| 8 | Theme | `theme_minimal()`, `theme_bw()` | Non-data styling |

**Five Aesthetic Mappings:**
1. `x` ŌĆö horizontal position
2. `y` ŌĆö vertical position
3. `color` ŌĆö outline color (discrete/continuous)
4. `fill` ŌĆö interior color (bars, polygons)
5. `shape` ŌĆö point marker style
6. `size` ŌĆö point/line thickness
7. `alpha` ŌĆö transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = opaque)
8. `linetype` ŌĆö solid/dashed/dotted
9. `group` ŌĆö grouping for line connections

*(Any five fetch full marks.)*

**(b) Complete R Program:**

```r
library(ggplot2)

# Build the scatter plot
scatter_plot <- ggplot(data = mtcars,
                       mapping = aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Weight (1000 lbs)") +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Miles per Gallon") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1", name = "Cylinders") +
  labs(
    title    = "Vehicle Weight vs Fuel Efficiency",
    subtitle = "Color grouped by number of cylinders"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# Render and export
print(scatter_plot)
ggsave("mtcars_scatter.png", plot = scatter_plot,
       width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

1. **`factor(cyl)`** ŌĆö converts the numeric cylinder count into a discrete factor so `color` gets a categorical palette instead of a continuous gradient.
2. **`geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8)`** ŌĆö adds scatter points, sized for visibility and slightly transparent to reveal overlap.
3. **`geom_smooth(method = "lm")`** ŌĆö fits a linear regression; `se = TRUE` shows the 95 % confidence ribbon (default).
4. **`scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")`** ŌĆö applies a color-blind-friendly qualitative palette from ColorBrewer.
5. **`theme_minimal()`** ŌĆö removes the gray background, retaining minimal gridlines for clean publication output.
6. **`ggsave()`** ŌĆö exports the plot to a high-resolution PNG file (300 DPI suitable for print).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Differentiate between `facet_wrap()` and `facet_grid()` in ggplot2. Provide a code snippet demonstrating the use of `facet_wrap()` on the `iris` dataset to create a scatter plot of `Sepal.Length` vs `Sepal.Width` faceted by `Species`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between **aesthetic mapping** and **aesthetic setting** with appropriate code examples. Why is placing `color = "blue"` inside `aes()` considered a mistake? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) `facet_wrap()` vs `facet_grid()`:**

| Property | `facet_wrap()` | `facet_grid()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | `~variable` (1-D) | `rows ~ cols` (2-D) |
| **Layout** | Wraps panels into a rectangular grid automatically | Explicit row/column matrix |
| **Use case** | One faceting variable | Two faceting variables |
| **Argument** | `nrow`, `ncol` | Direct row and column variables |

**Code for `facet_wrap()` on iris:**

```r
library(ggplot2)

iris_plot <- ggplot(data = iris,
                    mapping = aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width,
                                  color = Species)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~Species, nrow = 1) +
  labs(title = "Sepal Dimensions across Iris Species",
       x = "Sepal Length (cm)",
       y = "Sepal Width (cm)",
       color = "Species") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)

print(iris_plot)
ggsave("iris_facet.png", plot = iris_plot, width = 10, height = 4, dpi = 300)
```

**Explanation:**
- `facet_wrap(~Species, nrow = 1)` produces 3 horizontal panels, one for each species.
- The aesthetic `color = Species` is inherited from the global `ggplot()` call, so each panel uses a consistent color for the species.
- `geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)` adds a linear fit per panel without the confidence ribbon.

**(b) Aesthetic Mapping vs. Aesthetic Setting:**

| Aspect | Mapping (inside `aes()`) | Setting (outside `aes()`) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of value** | A **variable** in the data | A **fixed constant** |
| **Effect on legend** | Creates a legend entry | No legend entry |
| **Use case** | When property depends on data | When property is uniform for all marks |
| **Example** | `aes(color = species)` | `geom_point(color = "red")` |

**Why `aes(color = "blue")` is a mistake:**

```r
# CORRECT ŌĆö fixed value
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = class)) +     # Variable ŌåÆ legend created
  geom_line(color = "blue")            # Constant ŌåÆ no legend

# INCORRECT ŌĆö wrong layer
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = "blue"))      # Treats "blue" as a variable!
```

When `"blue"` is placed **inside `aes()`**, ggplot2 interprets it as a **categorical variable with one constant value**, creating a misleading legend entry like *"color: blue"*. It also bypasses the standard color palette and renders inconsistently across themes. The correct approach is to place fixed colors **outside** `aes()` so they bypass the scale system entirely.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to load `library(ggplot2)`** ŌĆö full code does not run ŌåÆ 0 marks for output.
> 2. **Using `geom_bar()` with a `y` aesthetic** ŌĆö produces a `stat_count()` error ŌåÆ -2 marks.
> 3. **Placing fixed colors inside `aes()`** ŌĆö incorrect grammar ŌåÆ -1 to -2 marks.
> 4. **Missing `+` at line ends** ŌĆö R parses `Error: unexpected symbol` ŌåÆ entire plot fails.
> 5. **Forgetting `factor()` for discrete color** ŌĆö continuous gradient instead of distinct colors ŌåÆ -1 mark.
> 6. **Not specifying `bins` in `geom_histogram()`** ŌĆö warning message, default 30 may be unsuitable ŌåÆ -1 mark.
> 7. **Confusing `facet_wrap(~var)` with `facet_grid(~var)`** ŌĆö same one-D form, but the latter expects a formula `rows ~ cols` and crashes if only one variable is given ŌåÆ -1 mark.
> 8. **Omitting `labs()` for title/axis names** ŌĆö plot is correct but loses presentation marks ŌåÆ up to -2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ggplot2** implements the **Grammar of Graphics** as a layered, declarative system in R.
- Every plot begins with `ggplot(data, aes(...))` and is built using the **`+` operator**.
- **`aes()` inside the function** ŌåÆ **variable-driven**; **outside the function** ŌåÆ **constant**.
- **Geoms** are the marks: `geom_point`, `geom_line`, `geom_bar`, `geom_col`, `geom_histogram`, `geom_boxplot`, `geom_smooth`, `geom_violin`.
- **Stats** are summary transforms: `stat_count`, `stat_bin`, `stat_smooth`, `stat_summary`.
- **Scales** control mappings: `scale_x_continuous`, `scale_color_brewer`, `scale_fill_manual`.
- **Faceting** creates small multiples: `facet_wrap(~var)` for 1-D, `facet_grid(rows ~ cols)` for 2-D.
- **Themes** control non-data ink: `theme_minimal`, `theme_bw`, `theme_classic`, `theme_gray` (default).
- **Always load the library** at the top of the script: `library(ggplot2)` or `library(tidyverse)`.
- **Use `factor()`** to convert numeric columns to discrete categories before color/shape mapping.
- **Export with `ggsave("filename.png", plot, width, height, dpi)`** for publication-quality output (Ōēź 300 dpi).
- **The 7-component mnemonic is "GLASS-FT"**: **G**eoms ŌåÆ **L**abels ŌåÆ **A**esthetics ŌåÆ **S**tats ŌåÆ **S**cales ŌåÆ **F**acets ŌåÆ **T**hemes.
- **Quick map of stat defaults**: `geom_bar` = `count`, `geom_col` = `identity`, `geom_histogram` = `bin`, `geom_smooth` = `smooth`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning with R: Basic Concepts, Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning, Simple Regression, and Classification Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT746 - PROGRAMMING IN R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning with R: Basic Concepts, Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning, Simple Regression, and Classification Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Machine Learning (ML)?

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computer systems to **learn patterns from data and improve their performance on a given task without being explicitly programmed**. Instead of writing rule-based code, the system *induces* a function from examples (inputŌĆōoutput pairs) and generalizes that function to predict outcomes for new, unseen inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Arthur Samuel (1959)** coined ML as the *"field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."* **Tom Mitchell (1997)** gave the formal definition: *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

#### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Student Who Learns from Examples"
Imagine a child learning to identify fruits. The parent shows the child 100 pictures labeled *"Apple"* and *"Orange"*. The child notices patterns ŌĆö apples are round and red, oranges are round and orange. Later, when the child sees a new fruit, they can classify it correctly **without the parent writing down rules**. That is ML: the *model* is the child's brain, the *training data* is the labeled pictures, and *generalization* is the ability to classify new fruits.

---

### 1.2 The Two Principal Paradigms of Learning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly requires students to **distinguish between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning** with real-world examples. This is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

| Paradigm | Definition | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Supervised Learning** | Learning a mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ from **labeled** training data $(x_i, y_i)$. | A teacher supervising a student with an answer key. |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | Discovering hidden structure in **unlabeled** data $X$ only. | A tourist exploring a new city without a guide. |
| **Reinforcement Learning** *(overview only)* | An *agent* learns by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards/penalties. | A baby learning to walk via trial and error. |

#### 1.2.1 Supervised Learning ŌĆö Deeper Look
- The dataset is $\{ (x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \dots, (x^{(n)}, y^{(n)}) \}$.
- **Goal:** Learn $h_{\theta}(x)$ such that $h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) \approx y^{(i)}$.
- **Sub-tasks:**
  - **Classification** ŌĆö discrete output $y \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, K\}$ (e.g., spam vs. ham).
  - **Regression** ŌĆö continuous output $y \in \mathbb{R}$ (e.g., house price).

#### 1.2.2 Unsupervised Learning ŌĆö Deeper Look
- Dataset is $\{ x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(n)} \}$ with **no labels**.
- **Goal:** Discover structure ŌĆö groupings, low-dimensional representations, anomalies.
- **Sub-tasks:**
  - **Clustering** (K-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN)
  - **Dimensionality Reduction** (PCA, t-SNE)
  - **Association Rule Mining** (Apriori)

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** If you have a *target column* ($y$) in your dataset, it is **supervised**. If your dataset has *features only* (no $y$), it is **unsupervised**.

---

### 1.3 Regression vs. Classification ŌĆö The Output Decides

| Aspect | Regression | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Output type | Continuous numerical value | Discrete class label |
| Example | Predicting temperature, salary, sales | Spam detection, disease diagnosis, image recognition |
| Typical algorithms | Linear Regression, Ridge, Lasso, SVR | Logistic Regression, KNN, Decision Tree, SVM, Naive Bayes |
| Evaluation | RMSE, MAE, $R^2$ | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC |

#### Analogy ŌĆö *"The Weather Forecaster vs. The Doctor"*
- A **weather forecaster** predicts *"tomorrow's temperature will be 32.5 ┬░C"* ŌåÆ **Regression**.
- A **doctor** predicts *"this patient has diabetes (Yes/No)"* ŌåÆ **Classification**.

---

### 1.4 Why R for Machine Learning?

> [!NOTE]
> R is a **statistical programming language** born for data analysis. Packages like `caret`, `tidymodels`, `mlr3`, `e1071`, `rpart`, and `randomForest` make R a first-class citizen for ML prototyping, statistical validation, and publication-quality visualization (via `ggplot2`).

Key R packages for this module:
- **`stats::lm()`** ŌĆö Linear regression
- **`stats::glm()`** ŌĆö Logistic regression
- **`class::knn()`** ŌĆö K-Nearest Neighbors
- **`rpart::rpart()`** ŌĆö Decision Trees
- **`e1071::svm()`** ŌĆö Support Vector Machines
- **`caret::train()`** ŌĆö Unified ML workflow
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Machine Learning Pipeline (Supervised)

A KTU-favourite flow diagram description. The ML workflow consists of **6 stages**:

1. **Problem Definition** ŌĆö Is it classification or regression? What is the business goal?
2. **Data Collection** ŌĆö Gather raw data (CSV, databases, APIs).
3. **Data Pre-processing** ŌĆö Handle missing values, encode categorical variables, scale features, split into train/test sets.
4. **Model Selection** ŌĆö Choose an algorithm (linear, tree-based, kernel-based, etc.).
5. **Training & Validation** ŌĆö Fit the model on training data; tune hyperparameters using **k-fold cross-validation**.
6. **Evaluation & Deployment** ŌĆö Test on unseen data, compute metrics, deploy to production.

---

### 2.2 Simple Linear Regression ŌĆö Theory

**Simple Linear Regression (SLR)** models the relationship between a single independent variable $x$ and a continuous dependent variable $y$ using a straight line:

$$
y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i
$$

Where:
- $\beta_0$ = **intercept** (value of $y$ when $x = 0$)
- $\beta_1$ = **slope** (change in $y$ per unit change in $x$)
- $\varepsilon_i$ = **error term** (residual), assumed $\sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$

#### 2.2.1 The Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Criterion
The model parameters $\beta_0, \beta_1$ are chosen to **minimize the sum of squared residuals (RSS)**:

$$
\text{RSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i) \right)^2
$$

The **closed-form solutions** (derived in SECTION 3) are:

$$
\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

$$
\hat{\beta_0} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta_1}\,\bar{x}
$$

#### 2.2.2 Why "Squared" Residuals?
- Squaring **penalizes large errors more heavily** than small ones.
- It makes the function **differentiable**, allowing calculus-based optimization.
- It gives equal weight to over- and under-predictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions of Linear Regression (LINE):**
> - **L**inearity between $x$ and $y$
> - **I**ndependence of errors
> - **N**ormality of residuals
> - **E**qual variance (**Homoscedasticity**)

---

### 2.3 Logistic Regression ŌĆö Theory (Classification)

When $y$ is binary ($0$ or $1$), a linear model is inappropriate because it can output values outside $[0, 1]$. **Logistic Regression** wraps the linear combination with the **sigmoid (logistic) function**:

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \text{where } z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x
$$

$$
P(y=1 \mid x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x)}}
$$

The decision rule: predict $\hat{y} = 1$ if $P(y=1 \mid x) \geq 0.5$, else $\hat{y} = 0$.

The **log-odds (logit)** transformation makes the relationship linear:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{1-P}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x
$$

Parameters are estimated by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** ŌĆö see SECTION 3.

---

### 2.4 K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)

A **lazy, non-parametric** algorithm:
- For a new point $x_{\text{new}}$, find the $K$ training points closest in feature space.
- **Regression:** output = average of the $K$ neighbors' $y$ values.
- **Classification:** output = majority vote of the $K$ neighbors' labels.

Distance metric: usually **Euclidean**:

$$
d(x^{(i)}, x^{(j)}) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{p}\left(x_k^{(i)} - x_k^{(j)}\right)^2}
$$

---

### 2.5 Decision Tree (CART)

A tree-structured model that recursively splits the feature space:
- **Regression Tree:** minimizes RSS within each leaf.
- **Classification Tree:** minimizes impurity (Gini or Entropy).

$$
\text{Gini} = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2
$$

$$
\text{Entropy} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k
$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula** you may need for KTU 2024 Module 4 derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Regression | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon$ | SLR hypothesis |
| 2 | Slope estimate | $\hat{\beta_1} = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | OLS estimator |
| 3 | Intercept estimate | $\hat{\beta_0} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta_1}\bar{x}$ | OLS estimator |
| 4 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error |
| 5 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Root MSE |
| 6 | MAE | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Mean Absolute Error |
| 7 | $R^2$ (Coefficient of Determination) | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\text{SS}_{\text{res}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{tot}}}$ | Variance explained |
| 8 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Logistic function |
| 9 | Logistic Regression | $P(y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x)$ | Probability output |
| 10 | Logit | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1-P}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Linear log-odds |
| 11 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \left[ y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i) \right]$ | Logistic loss |
| 12 | Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_k^{(i)} - x_k^{(j)})^2}$ | KNN metric |
| 13 | Gini Impurity | $\text{Gini} = 1 - \sum p_k^2$ | Decision tree |
| 14 | Entropy | $H = -\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$ | Decision tree |
| 15 | Information Gain | $\text{IG} = H(\text{parent}) - \sum \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Tree split criterion |
| 16 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classification metric |
| 17 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of predicted +ves, how many correct |
| 18 | Recall | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual +ves, how many caught |
| 19 | F1 Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 20 | Confusion Matrix | $\begin{bmatrix} TN & FP \\ FN & TP \end{bmatrix}$ | Classification evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT `|`) for absolute value inside markdown table cells to avoid breaking syntax.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | ML Task | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Civil Engineering | Regression | Predicting concrete compressive strength |
| Mechanical | Regression | Predicting remaining useful life of bearings |
| Electrical | Classification | Fault detection in power lines |
| Biomedical | Classification | Tumor classification from images |
| Finance | Regression | Stock price prediction |
| Marketing | Clustering (unsupervised) | Customer segmentation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of OLS Estimates for Simple Linear Regression

**Goal:** Minimize RSS with respect to $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$.

$$
\text{RSS}(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right)^2
$$

**Step 1:** Take the partial derivative of RSS with respect to $\beta_0$ and set to 0.

$$
\frac{\partial \text{RSS}}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the equation.

$$
\sum y_i = n \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum x_i
$$

Dividing both sides by $n$:

$$
\bar{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{x}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore,

$$
\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} \quad \text{...(Equation A)}
$$

**Step 4:** Take the partial derivative of RSS with respect to $\beta_1$ and set to 0.

$$
\frac{\partial \text{RSS}}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\beta_0$ from Equation A.

$$
\sum x_i \left( y_i - (\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}) - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i - \bar{y} \sum x_i + \beta_1 \bar{x} \sum x_i - \beta_1 \sum x_i^2 = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate $\beta_1$.

$$
\beta_1 \left( \bar{x} \sum x_i - \sum x_i^2 \right) = \bar{y} \sum x_i - \sum x_i y_i
$$

**Step 7:** Use the algebraic identity: $\bar{x}\sum x_i - \sum x_i^2 = - \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$. Applying this and the analogous identity on the right side:

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

This is the **Ordinary Least Squares estimator** for $\beta_1$. Combined with Equation A, we have the full solution.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Linear Regression in R

**Dataset:** 5 observations of study hours ($x$) and exam score ($y$).

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 2 | 55 |
| 3 | 3 | 65 |
| 4 | 4 | 70 |
| 5 | 5 | 80 |

**Step 1:** Compute means.

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{50+55+65+70+80}{5} = 64
$$

**Step 2:** Compute deviations and products.

| $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| -2 | -14 | 28 | 4 |
| -1 | -9 | 9 | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 6 | 6 | 1 |
| 2 | 16 | 32 | 4 |
| **Sum** | | **75** | **10** |

**Step 3:** Compute $\hat{\beta_1}$.

$$
\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{75}{10} = 7.5
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\hat{\beta_0}$.

$$
\hat{\beta_0} = 64 - 7.5 \times 3 = 64 - 22.5 = 41.5
$$

**Step 5:** Final regression equation.

$$
\hat{y} = 41.5 + 7.5 x
$$

**Step 6:** Predict for $x = 6$ hours.

$$
\hat{y} = 41.5 + 7.5 \times 6 = 41.5 + 45 = 86.5
$$

**Step 7:** Equivalent R code.

```r
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
y <- c(50, 55, 65, 70, 80)
model <- lm(y ~ x)
summary(model)
# Coefficients:
# (Intercept) 41.5
# x            7.5
predict(model, data.frame(x = 6))
# 86.5
```

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Logistic Regression (MLE)

**Goal:** Find $\beta_0, \beta_1$ that maximize the likelihood of observed labels.

**Step 1:** Likelihood function. Each $y_i$ is Bernoulli with parameter $p_i = \sigma(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i)$.

$$
L(\beta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i^{y_i} (1 - p_i)^{1 - y_i}
$$

**Step 2:** Log-likelihood (easier to maximize).

$$
\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log p_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - p_i) \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $p_i = \sigma(z_i)$ where $z_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$. After algebraic manipulation (using the property $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$), the gradient with respect to $\beta_j$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - p_i \right) x_{ij}
$$

**Step 4:** Set gradient to zero ŌĆö no closed-form solution exists. Therefore, use **Gradient Descent** or **Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS)**:

$$
\beta_j^{(t+1)} = \beta_j^{(t)} + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - p_i^{(t)} \right) x_{ij}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

**Step 5:** Prediction for new $x$:

$$
\hat{p} = \sigma(\hat{\beta_0} + \hat{\beta_1} x), \quad \hat{y} = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \hat{p} \geq 0.5 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparing Regression Algorithms

| Criterion | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression | KNN | Decision Tree |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Continuous | Probability / Class | Both | Both |
| **Parametric?** | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Training speed** | Fast (closed form) | Medium (iterative) | Instant (lazy) | Fast |
| **Prediction speed** | Very fast | Very fast | Slow (distance to all) | Very fast |
| **Handles non-linearity** | Poor | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | Low | High (visual) |
| **Feature scaling needed?** | Recommended | Recommended | **Yes** (distance-based) | No |
| **Overfitting risk** | Low | Medium | High (small $K$) | High (deep tree) |

---

### 3.5 Confusion Matrix ŌĆö Worked Example

Suppose a binary classifier produced the following on 100 test samples:

|  | Predicted +ve | Predicted -ve |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual +ve** | TP = 40 | FN = 10 |
| **Actual -ve** | FP = 5  | TN = 45 |

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{40 + 45}{100} = 0.85
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{40}{40 + 5} = \frac{40}{45} \approx 0.889
$$

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.889 \times 0.80}{0.889 + 0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.711}{1.689} \approx 0.842
$$

Interpretation: Of all actual positives, 80% were caught. Of all positive predictions, 88.9% were correct.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Machine Learning Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machine Learning] --> B[Supervised Learning]
    A --> C[Unsupervised Learning]
    A --> D[Reinforcement Learning]
    
    B --> B1[Regression]

    B1 --> B1a[Linear Regression]
    B1 --> B1b[Ridge / Lasso]
    B1 --> B1c[Polynomial Regression]

    B2 --> B2a[Logistic Regression]

    C --> C1[Clustering]
    C --> C2[Dimensionality Reduction]

    C1 --> C1a[K-Means]
    C1 --> C1b[Hierarchical]
    C1 --> C1c[DBSCAN]
    
    C2 --> C2a[PCA]
    C2 --> C2b[t-SNE]
    
    D --> D1[Q-Learning]
    D --> D2[Deep Q-Network]
```

---

### 4.2 Supervised Learning Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Data Pre-processing]
    B --> B1[Handle Missing Values]
    B --> B2[Encode Categorical Vars]
    B --> B3[Feature Scaling]
    B1 --> C[Train / Test Split]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Model Training]
    D --> D1[Linear / Logistic / KNN / Tree]
    D1 --> E[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    E --> F[Cross-Validation]
    F --> G[Final Model]
    G --> H[Evaluation Metrics]
    H --> H1[Regression: RMSE, R2]
    H --> H2[Classification: Accuracy, F1]
    H1 --> I[Deployment]
    H2 --> I
```

---

### 4.3 Linear Regression ŌĆö Concept Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature x] --> B[Multiply by Slope beta1]
    B --> C[Add Intercept beta0]
    C --> D[Linear Output: y = beta0 + beta1 x]
    D --> E[Compare with Actual y]
    E --> F[Compute Residual e = y - yhat]
    F --> G[Sum of Squared Residuals - RSS]
    G --> H[Optimization: Minimize RSS]
    H --> I[Optimal beta0, beta1]
    I --> J[Predictions on New Data]
```

---

### 4.4 Logistic Regression ŌĆö Decision Boundary Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Features x1, x2] --> B[Linear Combination z = beta0 + beta1 x1 + beta2 x2]
    B --> C[Sigmoid Function: sigma z = 1 over 1 + e^-z]
    C --> D{Probability greater than or equal 0.5}
    D -->|Yes| E[Predict Class 1]
    D -->|No| F[Predict Class 0]
    
    G[Training Phase] --> H[Maximize Log-Likelihood]
    H --> I[Update betas via Gradient Descent]
    I --> B
```

---

### 4.5 KNN Algorithm ŌĆö Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Unlabeled Point x_new] --> B[Choose K and Distance Metric]
    B --> C[Compute Distance to All Training Points]
    C --> D[Sort Distances - Ascending]
    D --> E[Select Top K Nearest Neighbors]
    E --> F{Task Type}
    F -->|Regression| G[Average y values of K Neighbors]
    F -->|Classification| H[Majority Vote of K Neighbors]
    G --> I[Final Prediction]
    H --> I
```
