> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `data = ` argument:** Students often write `lm(mpg ~ wt + hp)` instead of `lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)`. When variables aren't in the global environment, the model fails to fit. **Always specify `data =`.**
> 2. **Confusing `predict()` defaults:** By default, `predict(model)` returns fitted values for the *training* data, not predictions for new data. To predict for new observations, you **must** pass `newdata` as a data frame with the **same column names** as the training predictors.
> 3. **Misinterpreting p-values in `summary()`:** The `Pr(>|t|)` column tests $H_0: \beta_j = 0$. A high p-value (>0.05) means the predictor is *not* useful given the others in the model ŌĆö it does **not** mean the coefficient is exactly zero.
> 4. **Forgetting to set `par(mfrow)` for plots:** Calling `plot(model)` four times will overwrite plots. Use `par(mfrow = c(2,2))` first to see all four in one grid.
> 5. **Using `predict()` with wrong factor levels:** If a predictor is a factor, `newdata` must contain a level *present in the training data*; otherwise, you'll get an `unused factor level` error.
> 6. **Reporting R┬▓ instead of Adjusted R┬▓** when comparing models with different numbers of predictors. Examiners will deduct marks ŌĆö they want the penalized version.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`lm(formula, data)`** is the core function for fitting OLS linear models in R.
- The **OLS estimator** in matrix form is $\hat{\beta} = (X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}y$.
- **Formula notation** uses `~` (modeled as a function of), `+` (additive), `*` (interaction + main effects), `:` (interaction only), `.` (all remaining variables), `-1` (no intercept), `I()` (as-is arithmetic).
- **`summary(model)`** displays coefficients, standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, $R^2$, Adjusted $R^2$, F-statistic, and residual standard error.
- **`predict(model, newdata)`** generates point predictions; add `interval = "confidence"` for CI of the mean or `interval = "prediction"` for PI of a new observation.
- **PI is always wider than CI** because it includes both parameter uncertainty and irreducible error ($\sqrt{1 + \dots}$ vs $\sqrt{\dots}$).
- **Four diagnostic plots** check: (1) linearity/homoscedasticity, (2) normality, (3) constant variance, (4) influential points.
- **`confint(model)`** returns 95% confidence intervals for all coefficients.
- **`anova(m1, m2)`** performs a partial F-test to compare nested models.
- **`step(model, direction = "backward")`** automates variable selection by minimizing AIC.
- **AIC penalizes model complexity** ($2k$); **BIC penalizes more strongly** ($k \ln n$). Lower values indicate better models.
- **Influential points** are detected via **Cook's distance**; values $> 1$ warrant investigation.
- **Multicollinearity** is detected via the **Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)** from the `car` package: `car::vif(model)` ŌĆö values $> 5$ (or $> 10$) indicate a problem.
- **Adjusted R┬▓ must be used** when comparing models with different numbers of predictors; plain R┬▓ always increases with more variables.
- **Interaction terms** (`y ~ x1 * x2`) model situations where the effect of one predictor *depends on* the value of another.
- **Polynomial regression** is still a "linear model" ŌĆö e.g., `lm(y ~ poly(x, 2))` is linear in coefficients $\beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression - Types, Unusual Observations and Corrective Measures, and Comparison of Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT746 - PROGRAMMING IN R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regression - Types, Unusual Observations and Corrective Measures, and Comparison of Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Regression Analysis?

**Regression Analysis** is a supervised statistical learning method used to model and analyze the relationship between a **dependent variable** (response, $Y$) and one or more **independent variables** (predictors, $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p$). In the KTU 2024 R programming context, it forms the backbone of predictive analytics and is implemented using base R functions like `lm()`, `glm()`, and packages like `caret`, `MASS`, and `car`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> Regression is defined as a parametric supervised learning technique that estimates a functional mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ such that $Y \approx f(X) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is a random error term assumed to follow $N(0, \sigma^2)$.

### 1.2 Intuition: The "Best Fit Line" Analogy

Imagine you are a teacher plotting the **study hours ($X$)** of 30 students against their **exam scores ($Y$)**. The points will not lie on a perfect line ŌĆö they scatter like a cloud. Regression draws the **"single best straight line"** through this cloud so that:
- The line **passes as close as possible** to every point.
- The **total squared vertical distance** from points to the line is minimized (this is the **Ordinary Least Squares / OLS** principle).

If the relationship is curved (e.g., hours vs. fatigue), a straight line is inadequate ŌĆö you then need a **polynomial** or **non-linear** regression.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Regression Types (KTU Module 4 Focus)

| # | Regression Type | Mathematical Form | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple Linear** | $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$ | Single predictor, linear trend |
| 2 | **Multiple Linear** | $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \dots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon$ | Multiple predictors |
| 3 | **Polynomial** | $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 X^2 + \dots + \beta_k X^k + \varepsilon$ | Curvilinear relationships |
| 4 | **Logistic** | $\log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ | Binary classification ($0/1$) |
| 5 | **Ridge** | OLS + $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ penalty | Multicollinearity handling |
| 6 | **Lasso** | OLS + $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ penalty | Variable selection |
| 7 | **Stepwise** | Forward / Backward / Both | Automated feature selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Emphasizes R for Regression?**
> R provides vectorized OLS via the formula interface (`y ~ x1 + x2`), built-in diagnostic plots (`plot(model)` returns 4 standard diagnostics), and packages like `broom` for tidy model output ŌĆö making it the industry-standard teaching tool.

### 1.4 Unusual Observations ŌĆö The Three Culprits

In any regression fit, three types of "anomalous" data points can **distort the model**:

1. **Outliers** ŌĆö Points with large residual $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ (the $Y$ direction).
2. **High-Leverage Points** ŌĆö Points with extreme $X$ values (the $X$ direction).
3. **Influential Observations** ŌĆö Points whose **removal drastically changes** the regression coefficients.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic: "O-L-I"** ŌĆö *Outliers (vertical), Leverage (horizontal), Influence (combined effect)*. A point can be any combination: an outlier without leverage, a leverage point that isn't an outlier, etc.

### 1.5 Model Comparison ŌĆö The Need

When several candidate models exist (e.g., linear vs. polynomial, or different subsets of predictors), we need **objective criteria** to pick the "best" one. The criteria fall into two families:
- **In-sample metrics** ŌĆö $R^2$, Adjusted $R^2$, AIC, BIC, Mallows' $C_p$.
- **Out-of-sample metrics** ŌĆö PRESS, RMSE on test set, $k$-fold Cross-Validation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions of Classical Linear Regression (CLRM)

The OLS estimator is **BLUE** (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) **only if** the **Gauss-Markov assumptions** hold:

1. **Linearity**: $E(Y \vert X) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \dots + \beta_p X_p$.
2. **Independence**: Observations are independent (no autocorrelation). Tested via **Durbin-Watson** statistic.
3. **Homoscedasticity**: Constant variance of errors, $Var(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$. Tested via **Breusch-Pagan** test.
4. **Normality**: $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$. Tested via **Shapiro-Wilk** test, Q-Q plot.
5. **No Multicollinearity**: Predictors not highly correlated. Tested via **VIF** (Variance Inflation Factor).
6. **No measurement error** in $X$ (assumed fixed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Violation Diagnostic ŌĆö KTU Hot Topic**
> - Heteroscedasticity ŌåÆ **Corrected by**: Weighted Least Squares, log-transform $Y$, robust standard errors (`coeftest(..., vcov = vcovHC(model))`).
> - Multicollinearity ŌåÆ **Corrected by**: VIF pruning, Ridge regression, PCA-based regression.
> - Non-normality ŌåÆ **Corrected by**: Box-Cox transformation, non-parametric regression.

### 2.2 OLS Estimation ŌĆö Matrix Formulation

The general linear model in matrix form is:

$$Y = X\beta + \varepsilon$$

where $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (p+1)}$, $\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{(p+1) \times 1}$, and $\varepsilon \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$.

The **OLS estimator** that minimizes the residual sum of squares $RSS = (Y - X\beta)^T (Y - X\beta)$ is obtained by differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$$

The fitted values and residuals are:

$$\hat{Y} = X \hat{\beta} = X (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y = H Y$$

$$e = Y - \hat{Y} = (I - H) Y$$

where $H = X (X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ is the **hat matrix** (also called the projection or influence matrix).

### 2.3 Hat Matrix Diagonal ŌĆö The Leverage

The $i$-th diagonal element of $H$ is the **leverage** of observation $i$:

$$h_{ii} = x_i^T (X^T X)^{-1} x_i$$

- Range: $\frac{1}{n} \le h_{ii} \le 1$.
- Sum of all leverages: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_{ii} = p + 1$ (the trace of $H$).
- **High leverage rule of thumb**: $h_{ii} > \frac{2(p+1)}{n}$ is flagged.

### 2.4 Cook's Distance ŌĆö The Influence Quantifier

**Cook's Distance** $D_i$ measures how much the entire regression coefficients change when observation $i$ is removed:

$$D_i = \frac{e_i^2}{p \cdot \hat{\sigma}^2} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1 - h_{ii})^2}$$

- Equivalent form: $D_i = \frac{(\hat{\beta} - \hat{\beta}_{(i)})^T (X^T X) (\hat{\beta} - \hat{\beta}_{(i)})}{p \hat{\sigma}^2}$.
- **Rule of thumb**: $D_i > \frac{4}{n}$ indicates an influential point.
- **R implementation**: `cooks.distance(model)`.

### 2.5 DFFITS and DFBETAS

**DFFITS** measures the influence of observation $i$ on its **own fitted value**:

$$DFFITS_i = \frac{\hat{y}_i - \hat{y}_{i(i)}}{\hat{\sigma}_{(i)} \sqrt{h_{ii}}} = e_i \cdot \sqrt{\frac{h_{ii}}{1 - h_{ii}}} \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}_{(i)} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}}$$

- Threshold: $\vert DFFITS_i \vert > 2\sqrt{\frac{p+1}{n}}$.

**DFBETAS** measures the influence of observation $i$ on a **specific coefficient** $\beta_j$:

$$DFBETAS_{j(i)} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j - \hat{\beta}_{j(i)}}{\hat{\sigma}_{(i)} \sqrt{C_{jj}}}$$

where $C_{jj}$ is the $j$-th diagonal of $(X^T X)^{-1}$. Threshold: $\vert DFBETAS_{j(i)} \vert > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Master Cheat Table

| # | Concept | Formula | Threshold / Boundary | R Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OLS Estimator | $\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ | Requires $\det(X^T X) \neq 0$ | `lm(y ~ x)` |
| 2 | Hat Matrix | $H = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ | Symmetric, idempotent | `hatvalues(model)` |
| 3 | Leverage | $h_{ii} = x_i^T (X^T X)^{-1} x_i$ | $> 2(p+1)/n$ | `hatvalues(model)` |
| 4 | Studentized Residual | $t_i = \frac{e_i}{\hat{\sigma}_{(i)} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}}$ | $\vert t_i \vert > 2$ | `rstudent(model)` |
| 5 | Cook's Distance | $D_i = \frac{e_i^2}{p \hat{\sigma}^2} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1-h_{ii})^2}$ | $> 4/n$ | `cooks.distance(model)` |
| 6 | DFFITS | $\frac{\hat{y}_i - \hat{y}_{i(i)}}{\hat{\sigma}_{(i)} \sqrt{h_{ii}}}$ | $\vert \cdot \vert > 2\sqrt{(p+1)/n}$ | `dffits(model)` |
| 7 | DFBETAS | $\frac{\hat{\beta}_j - \hat{\beta}_{j(i)}}{\hat{\sigma}_{(i)} \sqrt{C_{jj}}}$ | $\vert \cdot \vert > 2/\sqrt{n}$ | `dfbetas(model)` |
| 8 | $R^2$ | $1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | $\in [0, 1]$ | `summary(model)$r.sq` |
| 9 | Adjusted $R^2$ | $1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p-1}$ | Penalizes extra predictors | `summary(model)$adj.r.sq` |
| 10 | AIC | $2p - 2 \ln(\hat{L})$ | Lower is better | `AIC(model)` |
| 11 | BIC | $p \ln(n) - 2 \ln(\hat{L})$ | Lower is better | `BIC(model)` |
| 12 | Mallows $C_p$ | $\frac{SS_{res,p}}{\hat{\sigma}^2_{full}} - n + 2p$ | $C_p \approx p$ ideal | Manual / `leaps` |
| 13 | PRESS | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2$ | Lower is better | `PRESS(model)` |
| 14 | VIF | $\frac{1}{1 - R^2_{X_j \vert \text{others}}}$ | $> 5$ or $> 10$ | `car::vif(model)` |
| 15 | Box-Cox $\lambda$ | $Y^{(\lambda)} = \frac{Y^\lambda - 1}{\lambda}$ for $\lambda \neq 0$ | $\lambda \in [-2, 2]$ | `MASS::boxcox(model)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE** (used in all tables above): Absolute value $\vert x \vert$ is written using the LaTeX macro `\vert` ŌĆö **never** the raw pipe character `\vert` ŌĆö to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Regression Used |
|---|---|---|
| Civil Engineering | Predicting concrete compressive strength | Multiple linear / polynomial |
| Mechanical | Engine efficiency vs. RPM & load | Ridge (handles collinearity) |
| Electrical | Power consumption vs. temperature & time | Time-series / ARIMAX |
| Biomedical | Disease risk vs. biomarkers | Logistic |
| Economics | GDP vs. fiscal indicators | Ridge / Lasso |
| Data Science | Feature selection in high-dimensional data | Lasso / Stepwise |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the OLS Estimator

We minimize the **Residual Sum of Squares (RSS)**:

$$RSS(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2$$

In matrix form:

$$RSS(\beta) = (Y - X\beta)^T (Y - X\beta)$$

**Step 1** ŌĆö Expand the quadratic:

$$RSS = Y^T Y - 2 \beta^T X^T Y + \beta^T X^T X \beta$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Differentiate w.r.t. $\beta$ (using $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T A \beta) = 2A\beta$ for symmetric $A$):

$$\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial \beta} = -2 X^T Y + 2 X^T X \beta$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Set gradient to zero (first-order optimality):

$$-2 X^T Y + 2 X^T X \hat{\beta} = 0$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Solve for $\hat{\beta}$ (assuming $X^T X$ is invertible, i.e., full column rank):

$$\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5** ŌĆö Second-order check: $\frac{\partial^2 RSS}{\partial \beta^2} = 2 X^T X$. Since this is positive semi-definite, the stationary point is a **global minimum** (assuming $X$ has full rank).

### 3.2 Derivation of Cook's Distance

Cook's Distance is defined as the squared standardized distance between $\hat{\beta}$ and $\hat{\beta}_{(i)}$ (the coefficients with the $i$-th observation deleted):

$$D_i = \frac{(\hat{\beta} - \hat{\beta}_{(i)})^T (X^T X) (\hat{\beta} - \hat{\beta}_{(i)})}{p \, \hat{\sigma}^2}$$

**Step 1** ŌĆö The "leave-one-out" coefficient update formula is:

$$\hat{\beta}_{(i)} = \hat{\beta} - \frac{(X^T X)^{-1} x_i e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Substitute into the numerator:

$$\hat{\beta} - \hat{\beta}_{(i)} = \frac{(X^T X)^{-1} x_i e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Compute the quadratic form:

$$(\hat{\beta} - \hat{\beta}_{(i)})^T (X^T X) (\hat{\beta} - \hat{\beta}_{(i)}) = \frac{e_i^2 \, x_i^T (X^T X)^{-1} (X^T X) (X^T X)^{-1} x_i}{(1 - h_{ii})^2}$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Simplify using $x_i^T (X^T X)^{-1} x_i = h_{ii}$:

$$= \frac{e_i^2 \, h_{ii}}{(1 - h_{ii})^2}$$

**Step 5** ŌĆö Divide by $p \hat{\sigma}^2$:

$$D_i = \frac{e_i^2}{p \hat{\sigma}^2} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1 - h_{ii})^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Adjusted $R^2$ ŌĆö Penalization Derivation

The ordinary $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ **always increases** when predictors are added (even if useless), because $SS_{res}$ can only drop. To penalize, we use **degrees of freedom**.

**Step 1** ŌĆö Define $R^2$ in terms of sums of squares:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res} / (n - p - 1)}{SS_{tot} / (n - 1)}$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Recognize that $SS_{res} / (n - p - 1) = \hat{\sigma}^2$ (the MSE of the regression).

**Step 3** ŌĆö Define adjusted $R^2$ as:

$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \frac{\hat{\sigma}^2}{SS_{tot} / (n - 1)} = 1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)(n - 1)}{n - p - 1} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Key Insight**: Adding a useless predictor increases $n - p - 1$ in the denominator faster than it decreases $SS_{res}$, so $R^2_{adj}$ **decreases** ŌĆö correctly penalizing model complexity.

### 3.4 AIC and BIC ŌĆö Information-Theoretic Foundation

**Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC)** estimates the expected Kullback-Leibler divergence between the fitted model and the true data-generating process:

$$AIC = 2p - 2 \ln(\hat{L})$$

where $\hat{L}$ is the maximized likelihood and $p$ is the number of estimated parameters (NOT including the error variance). For OLS with Gaussian errors:

$$AIC = n \ln\!\left(\frac{RSS}{n}\right) + 2(p + 1)$$

**Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)** adds a stronger complexity penalty:

$$BIC = p \ln(n) - 2 \ln(\hat{L})$$

**Step-by-step comparison logic**:

| Criterion | Penalty per extra parameter | Effect at large $n$ |
|---|---|---|
| $R^2_{adj}$ | Effectively 1 per param (implicit) | Consistent with $R^2_{adj}$ |
| AIC | 2 per param | Tends to pick larger models |
| BIC | $\ln(n)$ per param | For $n \ge 8$, $\ln(n) > 2$, picks smaller models |

### 3.5 PRESS ŌĆö Prediction Sum of Squares

The PRESS statistic computes the **leave-one-out prediction error**:

$$PRESS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_{i(i)})^2$$

**Step 1** ŌĆö Use the relationship between $\hat{y}_i$ and $\hat{y}_{i(i)}$:

$$\hat{y}_i - \hat{y}_{i(i)} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

(Proof: since $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ and $\hat{y}_{i(i)} = x_i^T \hat{\beta}_{(i)} = x_i^T \hat{\beta} - \frac{h_{ii} e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$)

**Step 2** ŌĆö Substitute:

$$PRESS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Key Advantage**: PRESS can be computed from a **single model fit** (using $h_{ii}$), without $n$ refits ŌĆö making it computationally efficient.

### 3.6 VIF ŌĆö Variance Inflation Factor Derivation

The variance of the $j$-th OLS coefficient is:

$$Var(\hat{\beta}_j) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)^2} \cdot VIF_j$$

**Step 1** ŌĆö Define $R^2_j$ as the $R^2$ from regressing $X_j$ on all other predictors.

**Step 2** ŌĆö By definition:

$$VIF_j = \frac{1}{1 - R^2_j} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Interpretation:
- $VIF_j = 1$: $X_j$ is orthogonal to all other predictors (no multicollinearity).
- $VIF_j > 5$: Moderate multicollinearity ŌĆö investigate.
- $VIF_j > 10$: Severe multicollinearity ŌĆö remove or combine predictors, or use Ridge.

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem**: Given $n = 5$ observations and 2 predictors, compute Cook's distance for observation 3.

| $i$ | $x_{i1}$ | $x_{i2}$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 5.2 | -0.2 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 7 | 6.8 | 0.2 |
| 3 | 5 | 5 | 25 | 18.0 | **7.0** |
| 4 | 3 | 4 | 12 | 11.5 | 0.5 |
| 5 | 4 | 3 | 14 | 13.5 | 0.5 |

Assume $p = 2$, $h_{33} = 0.6$, $\hat{\sigma}^2 = 1.5$.

**Step 1** ŌĆö Plug into Cook's distance formula:

$$D_3 = \frac{(7.0)^2}{2 \cdot 1.5} \cdot \frac{0.6}{(1 - 0.6)^2}$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Evaluate:

$$D_3 = \frac{49}{3} \cdot \frac{0.6}{0.16} = 16.333 \cdot 3.75 = 61.25$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Compare with threshold $\frac{4}{n} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$.

**Conclusion**: $D_3 = 61.25 \gg 0.8$ ŌĆö Observation 3 is **highly influential** and must be investigated. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why observation 3 is influential**: It has both a **large residual** ($e_3 = 7$) and **high leverage** ($h_{33} = 0.6 > \frac{2 \cdot 3}{5} = 1.2$ ŌĆö borderline, but combined with large residual, it dominates).

### 3.8 Corrective Measures ŌĆö Decision Matrix

| Problem Detected | Diagnostic Test | First Corrective Measure | Last-Resort Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Outlier (large $e_i$) | Studentized residual $> 2$ | Investigate data entry error | Robust regression (Huber, M-estimators) |
| High leverage | $h_{ii} > 2(p+1)/n$ | Verify $X$ measurement | Remove if truly anomalous |
| Influential point | $D_i > 4/n$ | Refit without, compare | Weighted regression |
| Heteroscedasticity | Breusch-Pagan $p < 0.05$ | Log-transform $Y$ | WLS or robust SEs |
| Multicollinearity | VIF $> 10$ | Drop redundant predictor | Ridge regression / PCA |
| Non-normality | Shapiro-Wilk $p < 0.05$ | Box-Cox transformation | Non-parametric regression |
| Autocorrelation | Durbin-Watson $\approx 0$ or $4$ | Add lagged variables | GLS / ARIMAX |

### 3.9 R Code Implementation Block

```r
# === KTU Module 4: Complete Regression Diagnostics in R ===

# 1. Fit a multiple linear regression
model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + cyl + hp, data = mtcars)

# 2. Standard diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model)   # Residuals vs Fitted, Q-Q, Scale-Location, Leverage

# 3. Compute influence measures
cook_d  <- cooks.distance(model)
lev     <- hatvalues(model)
stu_res <- rstudent(model)
dffits  <- dffits(model)

# 4. Flag influential observations
n <- nrow(mtcars); p <- 3
threshold_cook <- 4 / n
influential <- which(cook_d > threshold_cook)

# 5. Multicollinearity check
library(car)
vif(model)

# 6. Compare nested models using AIC
model1 <- lm(mpg ~ wt,            data = mtcars)
model2 <- lm(mpg ~ wt + cyl,      data = mtcars)
model3 <- lm(mpg ~ wt + cyl + hp, data = mtcars)
AIC(model1, model2, model3)
BIC(model1, model2, model3)

# 7. Stepwise selection (both directions)
library(MASS)
final_model <- stepAIC(model3, direction = "both")

# 8. Ridge regression (for multicollinearity)
library(glmnet)
x <- model.matrix(mpg ~ wt + cyl + hp, mtcars)[, -1]
y <- mtcars$mpg
ridge_mod <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0, lambda = 0.1)

# 9. Box-Cox transformation for non-normality
library(MASS)
bc <- boxcox(model1)
lambda <- bc$x[which.max(bc$y)]
model1_bc <- lm(((mpg^lambda - 1) / lambda) ~ wt, data = mtcars)

# 10. Cross-validation for model comparison
library(boot)
cv_m1 <- cv.glm(mtcars, glm(mpg ~ wt,            data = mtcars), K = 10)$delta[1]
cv_m2 <- cv.glm(mtcars, glm(mpg ~ wt + cyl,      data = mtcars), K = 10)$delta[1]
cv_m3 <- cv.glm(mtcars, glm(mpg ~ wt + cyl + hp, data = mtcars), K = 10)$delta[1]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Tip**: In the R lab exam, you are often asked to (a) fit a model, (b) produce the 4 standard diagnostic plots via `plot(model)`, and (c) list the influential observations using `cooks.distance() > 4/n`. Memorize this 3-step pattern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart ŌĆö Regression Modeling Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Problem] --> A1[Collect and Clean Data]
    A1 --> A2[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    A2 --> A3{Select Regression Type}
    A3 -->|Linear| A4a[Simple or Multiple Linear]
    A3 -->|Curvilinear| A4b[Polynomial]
    A3 -->|Binary Y| A4c[Logistic]
    A3 -->|Collinearity| A4d[Ridge or Lasso]
    A4a --> B1[Fit Initial Model lm or glm]
    A4b --> B1
    A4c --> B1
    A4d --> B1
    B1 --> B2[Verify Assumptions: Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality]
    B2 --> B3{Check Unusual Observations}
    B3 -->|Outliers| C1a[Studentized Residual Greater than 2]
    B3 -->|High Leverage| C1b[h_ii greater than 2p+1 divided by n]
    B3 -->|Influential| C1c[Cook D greater than 4 divided by n]
    C1a --> D1[Apply Corrective Measures]
    C1b --> D1
    C1c --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Re-Fit Model and Re-Diagnose]
    D2 --> E1{Compare Candidate Models}
    E1 --> E2[Compute R2, Adj R2, AIC, BIC, PRESS, RMSE]
    E2 --> E3{Select Best Model}
    E3 -->|Lowest AIC BIC| F1[Final Model]
    E3 -->|Highest Adj R2| F1
    E3 -->|Lowest PRESS| F1
    F1 --> G1[Validate on Test Set or Cross-Validation]
    G1 --> H1[Deploy and Report]
```

### 4.2 Influence Diagnostics Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observation i] --> B{Is it an Outlier?}
    B -->|Yes: Large e_i| C[Studentized Residual > 2]
    B -->|No| D{Is it a Leverage Point?}
    D -->|Yes: Extreme X| E[h_ii > 2p+1 / n]
    D -->|No| F[Typical Observation]
    C --> G{Is it Influential?}
    E --> G
    G -->|Yes: D_i > 4/n| H[Influential Point]
    G -->|No| I[Non-Influential Anomaly]
    H --> J[Investigate and Correct]
    I --> K[May Retain]
    F --> L[Retain in Model]
```

### 4.3 Comparison of Models ŌĆö Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Have Multiple Candidate Models] --> A1{Same dataset?}
    A1 -->|Yes: Nested| B1[Use Likelihood Ratio Test]
    A1 -->|No: Non-nested| B2[Use AIC or BIC]
    B1 --> C1{P-value less than 0.05?}
    C1 -->|Yes| D1[Prefer Larger Model]
    C1 -->|No| D2[Prefer Simpler Model]
    B2 --> E1[Lowest AIC wins]
    B2 --> E2[Lowest BIC wins]
    D1 --> F1[Cross-validate]
    D2 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Final Selected Model]
```

### 4.4 Corrective Measures Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Detected Problem] --> B1[Outlier]
    A --> B2[High Leverage]
    A --> B3[Influential]
    A --> B4[Heteroscedasticity]
    A --> B5[Multicollinearity]
    A --> B6[Non-normality]
    B1 --> C1[Option 1: Remove if data error]
    B1 --> C2[Option 2: Transform Y]
    B1 --> C3[Option 3: Robust regression]
    B2 --> D1[Verify X measurement]
    B2 --> D2[Remove if anomalous]
    B3 --> E1[Cook D test]
    B3 --> E2[Refit without and compare]
    B4 --> F1[Log or sqrt transform]
    B4 --> F2[Weighted least squares]
    B5 --> G1[Drop redundant X]
    B5 --> G2[Ridge or Lasso]
    B6 --> H1[Box-Cox transform]
    B6 --> H2[Non-parametric regression]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Regression Pipeline in R

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Layer
        DATA[Raw CSV Data] --> EDA[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    end
    subgraph Preprocessing_Layer
        EDA --> CLEAN[Missing Value Treatment]
        CLEAN --> TRANS[Transformation if needed]
    end
    subgraph Modeling_Layer
        TRANS --> FIT[Fit lm or glm]
        FIT --> DIAG[Diagnostics via plot]
    end
    subgraph Validation_Layer
        DIAG --> INFL[Influence Measures]
        DIAG --> ASSUMP[Assumption Tests]
    end
    subgraph Comparison_Layer
        INFL --> COMP[AIC, BIC, Adj R2, PRESS]
        ASSUMP --> COMP
        COMP --> SELECT[Best Model]
    end
    subgraph Output_Layer
        SELECT --> PRED[Predictions on Test Set]
        PRED --> REPORT[Final Report]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied**: All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`A1`, `B2`, `C1`, etc.), no reserved keywords used as IDs, and node labels are written in plain uppercase text without markdown bold/italic tags to ensure clean rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**
**Define Cook's Distance. State its threshold rule for identifying influential observations in a linear regression model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks)**: Cook's Distance $D_i$ is an influence measure that quantifies the squared standardized change in the estimated regression coefficients when the $i$-th observation is deleted. It is defined as:

$$D_i = \frac{e_i^2}{p \hat{\sigma}^2} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1 - h_{ii})^2}$$

where $e_i$ is the residual, $h_{ii}$ is the leverage, $p$ is the number of predictors, and $\hat{\sigma}^2$ is the MSE.

**Threshold (1 mark)**: An observation is flagged as influential if $D_i > \frac{4}{n}$, where $n$ is the sample size. In R, this is computed via `cooks.distance(model)` and the influential indices via `which(cooks.distance(model) > 4/nrow(data))`.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between R-squared and Adjusted R-squared. Why is the latter preferred for model comparison?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | $R^2$ | Adjusted $R^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | $1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p-1}$ |
| Effect of adding predictor | Always increases or stays same | Increases only if the predictor is useful; decreases otherwise |
| Penalization | None | Penalizes extra parameters via degrees of freedom |
| Use | Goodness-of-fit on training data | **Model comparison across different complexities** |

**Why preferred (1 mark)**: Adjusted $R^2$ corrects for the number of predictors $p$ and sample size $n$, ensuring that a model with more useless predictors does not artificially appear "better." Hence it is the correct metric for comparing models with different numbers of predictors.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the OLS estimation procedure in matrix form. Derive the OLS estimator $\hat{\beta}$ and the hat matrix $H$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a multiple regression with $n = 50$ observations and $p = 3$ predictors, the following summary is given: $R^2 = 0.85$, $SS_{tot} = 1200$, $SS_{res} = 180$, $h_{22} = 0.08$, $e_2 = 5.4$, $\hat{\sigma}^2 = 4.2$.

Compute (i) Adjusted $R^2$, (ii) Cook's Distance for observation 2, and (iii) state whether observation 2 is influential. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) OLS in Matrix Form (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Model specification (1 mark)**:

$$Y = X\beta + \varepsilon$$

where $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the response vector, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (p+1)}$ is the design matrix (with a column of 1s for the intercept), $\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{(p+1) \times 1}$ is the parameter vector, and $\varepsilon \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the error vector.

**Step 2 ŌĆö RSS minimization (1 mark)**: The OLS objective is to minimize:

$$RSS = (Y - X\beta)^T (Y - X\beta)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Expansion and differentiation (2 marks)**: Expanding:

$$RSS = Y^T Y - 2 \beta^T X^T Y + \beta^T X^T X \beta$$

Differentiating with respect to $\beta$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial \beta} = -2 X^T Y + 2 X^T X \hat{\beta} = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $\hat{\beta}$ (1 mark)**:

$$X^T X \hat{\beta} = X^T Y \implies \hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Hat matrix (2 marks)**: The fitted values are:

$$\hat{Y} = X \hat{\beta} = X (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y = H Y$$

where $H = X (X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ is the hat matrix. Properties:
- $H$ is symmetric: $H^T = H$.
- $H$ is idempotent: $H^2 = H$.
- $0 \le h_{ii} \le 1$ (diagonal elements are leverages).
- $\sum h_{ii} = p + 1$ (trace equals rank).

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Adjusted $R^2$ (2 marks)**:

$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)(n - 1)}{n - p - 1} = 1 - \frac{(1 - 0.85)(50 - 1)}{50 - 3 - 1}$$

$$= 1 - \frac{0.15 \cdot 49}{46} = 1 - \frac{7.35}{46} = 1 - 0.1598 = 0.8402$$

**(ii) Cook's Distance for observation 2 (3 marks)**:

$$D_2 = \frac{e_2^2}{p \hat{\sigma}^2} \cdot \frac{h_{22}}{(1 - h_{22})^2} = \frac{(5.4)^2}{3 \cdot 4.2} \cdot \frac{0.08}{(1 - 0.08)^2}$$

$$= \frac{29.16}{12.6} \cdot \frac{0.08}{0.8464} = 2.3143 \cdot 0.0945 = 0.2188$$

**(iii) Influence classification (2 marks)**: Threshold is $\frac{4}{n} = \frac{4}{50} = 0.08$.

Since $D_2 = 0.2188 > 0.08$, **observation 2 is influential** and should be investigated. Possible actions: verify data entry, refit the model without observation 2, or apply robust regression.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a)** List and explain any **four types of regression** with their mathematical forms. Discuss when each is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a multiple regression model, the following VIF values were obtained: $VIF_{X_1} = 1.2$, $VIF_{X_2} = 12.5$, $VIF_{X_3} = 3.8$, $VIF_{X_4} = 2.1$.

(i) Identify the predictor causing multicollinearity. (ii) Explain two corrective measures. (iii) Compute the $R^2$ of the auxiliary regression for $X_2$ on the other predictors. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Types of Regression (7 marks ŌĆö 1.5 marks each + 1 mark for summary):**

1. **Simple Linear Regression**:
   - Form: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$
   - When used: One predictor, linear relationship, continuous response.
   - Example: Predicting yield strength from carbon content in steel.

2. **Multiple Linear Regression**:
   - Form: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon$
   - When used: Multiple predictors, linear in parameters, no severe collinearity.
   - Example: Predicting house price from area, bedrooms, and location.

3. **Polynomial Regression**:
   - Form: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 X^2 + \dots + \beta_k X^k + \varepsilon$
   - When used: Curvilinear relationship captured by powers of $X$.
   - Example: Engine efficiency vs. RPM (parabolic).

4. **Ridge Regression** (or **Lasso**):
   - Form: OLS + penalty term.
   - Ridge: $\min \left\{ RSS + \lambda \sum \beta_j^2 \right\}$.
   - Lasso: $\min \left\{ RSS + \lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}$.
   - When used: Multicollinearity (Ridge) or feature selection (Lasso).
   - Lasso can shrink coefficients to exactly zero, performing variable selection.

**Summary (1 mark)**: Choice depends on data type, relationship shape, collinearity, and the goal (prediction vs. interpretation vs. selection).

---

**(b) VIF Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) Multicollinearity Identification (1 mark)**: $X_2$ has $VIF_{X_2} = 12.5 > 10$, indicating **severe multicollinearity**. The other predictors are within acceptable limits ($VIF < 5$).

**(ii) Two Corrective Measures (4 marks ŌĆö 2 each):**

**Measure 1 ŌĆö Drop or combine predictors**: If $X_2$ is highly correlated with another predictor, drop one of them. Alternatively, combine correlated predictors into a single composite (e.g., via PCA or domain-driven index).

**Measure 2 ŌĆö Ridge Regression**: Replace OLS with Ridge, which adds the penalty $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ to the RSS. This shrinks coefficients and stabilizes them in the presence of collinearity, though it does not perform variable selection.

**Alternative Measure ŌĆö Increase sample size** (1 extra mark): More data reduces the standard errors of coefficients and mitigates the variance-inflation effect of collinearity.

**(iii) Compute $R^2$ for the auxiliary regression (2 marks)**: By the VIF definition:

$$VIF_{X_2} = \frac{1}{1 - R^2_{X_2 \vert \text{others}}} \implies R^2_{X_2 \vert \text{others}} = 1 - \frac{1}{VIF_{X_2}}$$

$$R^2_{X_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{12.5} = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92$$

**Interpretation**: 92% of the variation in $X_2$ is explained by the other predictors ŌĆö confirming severe redundancy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam)**
>
> 1. **Formula misapplication in Cook's Distance** ŌĆö Students frequently use $D_i = \frac{e_i^2}{p \hat{\sigma}^2}$ (forgetting the leverage ratio $\frac{h_{ii}}{(1-h_{ii})^2}$). This yields an underestimate. **Always include the leverage component.**
>
> 2. **Confusion between $R^2$ and Adjusted $R^2$ formulas** ŌĆö The numerator is $\frac{SS_{res}}{n-p-1}$, NOT $\frac{SS_{res}}{n-1}$. Using the wrong denominator gives a value of $R^2_{adj}$ that is too large.
>
> 3. **Threshold misremembering** ŌĆö Leverage threshold is $\frac{2(p+1)}{n}$ (not $\frac{2p}{n}$). Cook's threshold is $\frac{4}{n}$. DFFITS threshold is $2\sqrt{\frac{p+1}{n}}$.
>
> 4. **AIC vs. BIC ŌĆö penalty term confusion** ŌĆö AIC uses $2p$ as penalty; BIC uses $p \ln(n)$. For $n < 8$, AIC penalizes more; for $n \ge 8$, BIC penalizes more.
>
> 5. **PRESS formula misuse** ŌĆö Some students write PRESS as $\sum e_i^2$ (which is just RSS). PRESS uses $\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$ ŌĆö a leave-one-out correction.
>
> 6. **In R code**: Forgetting to set `par(mfrow = c(2,2))` before `plot(model)` causes only one plot to be visible ŌĆö **lose 1 mark** in practical exams.
>
> 7. **VIF interpretation**: VIF $> 5$ is "investigate," VIF $> 10$ is "severe." Do not write "VIF $> 1$" ŌĆö that is the minimum, not a threshold.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exam)**

- **Regression Types (4 must-know)**: Simple Linear, Multiple Linear, Polynomial, Logistic. Bonus: Ridge, Lasso, Stepwise.
- **OLS Estimator (memorize)**: $\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$. Hat matrix $H = X(X^T X)^{-1}X^T$.
- **Residual Variance**: $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{RSS}{n - p - 1} = \frac{SS_{res}}{n - p - 1}$.
- **Leverage** $h_{ii} = x_i^T (X^T X)^{-1} x_i$. Threshold: $> \frac{2(p+1)}{n}$.
- **Studentized Residual** $t_i = \frac{e_i}{\hat{\sigma}_{(i)} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}}$. Threshold: $\vert t_i \vert > 2$.
- **Cook's Distance** $D_i = \frac{e_i^2}{p\hat{\sigma}^2} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1-h_{ii})^2}$. Threshold: $> \frac{4}{n}$.
- **DFFITS** threshold: $\vert DFFITS_i \vert > 2\sqrt{\frac{p+1}{n}}$.
- **DFBETAS** threshold: $\vert DFBETAS_{j(i)} \vert > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- **$R^2$** never decreases on adding predictors (limitation).
- **Adjusted $R^2$** = $1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p-1}$. Used for model comparison.
- **AIC** = $2p - 2\ln(\hat{L})$. **BIC** = $p\ln(n) - 2\ln(\hat{L})$. Lower is better.
- **PRESS** = $\sum \left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2$. Leave-one-out error in one fit.
- **Mallows $C_p$** = $\frac{SS_{res,p}}{\hat{\sigma}^2_{full}} - n + 2p$. Ideal: $C_p \approx p$.
- **VIF** = $\frac{1}{1 - R^2_j}$. Threshold: $> 5$ (investigate), $> 10$ (severe).
- **Corrective Measures**:
  - Heteroscedasticity ŌåÆ log-transform, WLS, robust SEs.
  - Multicollinearity ŌåÆ drop predictors, Ridge, PCA.
  - Non-normality ŌåÆ Box-Cox transformation.
  - Influential points ŌåÆ investigate, robust regression (Huber, M-estimators).
- **R Functions (must memorize)**: `lm()`, `glm()`, `summary()`, `plot(model)`, `cooks.distance()`, `hatvalues()`, `rstudent()`, `dffits()`, `dfbetas()`, `AIC()`, `BIC()`, `car::vif()`, `MASS::boxcox()`, `MASS::stepAIC()`, `boot::cv.glm()`, `glmnet()`.
- **Rule of Thumb Summary Table** (memorize thresholds in one place):

| Diagnostic | Symbol | Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Leverage | $h_{ii}$ | $> \frac{2(p+1)}{n}$ |
| Studentized Residual | $t_i$ | $\vert t_i \vert > 2$ |
| Cook's Distance | $D_i$ | $> \frac{4}{n}$ |
| DFFITS | $DFFITS_i$ | $\vert \cdot \vert > 2\sqrt{\frac{p+1}{n}}$ |
| DFBETAS | $DFBETAS_{j(i)}$ | $\vert \cdot \vert > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| VIF | $VIF_j$ | $> 5$ (or $> 10$ severe) |
| Durbin-Watson | $DW$ | $\approx 2$ (no autocorrelation) |
| Breusch-Pagan | $p$-value | $> 0.05$ (homoscedasticity) |
| Shapiro-Wilk | $p$-value | $> 0.05$ (normality) |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies and Applications - Real-World Applications of R in Various Fields Like Finance, Healthcare, and Social Sciences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT746 - PROGRAMMING IN R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies and Applications - Real-World Applications of R in Various Fields Like Finance, Healthcare, and Social Sciences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Case Studies and Applications** in the context of R programming refer to the practical, end-to-end deployment of R's statistical computing, graphical, and machine-learning capabilities to solve domain-specific real-world problems across interdisciplinary fields such as **Finance**, **Healthcare**, and **Social Sciences**. A *case study* in R is a structured analytical narrative that integrates data ingestion, cleaning, transformation, modeling, visualization, and interpretation using R's ecosystem (base R, `tidyverse`, `caret`, `ggplot2`, `shiny`, `quantmod`, `survival`, `tm`, `quanteda`, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to **identify, justify, and demonstrate** the suitability of R packages and analytical paradigms for finance (time-series, VaR, CAPM), healthcare (survival analysis, epidemiological modeling, biostatistics), and social sciences (survey weighting, sentiment mining, structural equation modeling).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of R as a **Swiss Army Knife for Data**:

- A **Finance Analyst** uses R like a **stock-market telescope** ŌĆö it crunches years of price ticks in seconds to spot trends, volatility clusters, and tail-risks that the naked eye cannot see.
- A **Healthcare Researcher** uses R like a **diagnostic scanner** ŌĆö it parses patient records, clinical trial outcomes, and genomic sequences to find hidden disease patterns.
- A **Social Scientist** uses R like a **public-opinion microphone** ŌĆö it amplifies survey whispers and tweet streams into measurable, statistically valid conclusions about human behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of R's Real-World Adoption:**
> 1. **Reproducibility** ŌĆö R Markdown / Quarto allow every analysis to be re-run verbatim.
> 2. **Open-source CRAN ecosystem** ŌĆö Over **20,000** community-vetted packages (as of 2024).
> 3. **Vectorized statistical engine** ŌĆö Operations on entire datasets without explicit loops.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in R Case Studies

| Domain | Key Metric | Typical Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
|--------|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| Finance | Confidence Level for VaR | $\alpha$ | **95%** or **99%** |
| Finance | Risk-free rate (approx.) | $r_f$ | **~4ŌĆō6%** (US T-bill, varies) |
| Healthcare | Significance level | $\alpha$ | **0.05** (standard) |
| Healthcare | Hazard Ratio | $HR$ | **HR = 1** ŌåÆ no effect |
| Social Sciences | Cronbach's Alpha | $\alpha$ | **Ōēź 0.7** (acceptable reliability) |
| Social Sciences | Effect size (Cohen's d) | $d$ | **0.2** small, **0.5** medium, **0.8** large |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Theoretical Framework

A real-world R case study follows a **six-stage analytical pipeline**:

1. **Problem Definition** ŌĆö Frame the business/research question (e.g., *"What is the 1-day 99% Value-at-Risk of our equity portfolio?"*).
2. **Data Acquisition** ŌĆö Pull from APIs, CSV, databases (e.g., `quantmod::getSymbols()`, `DBI`, `httr`).
3. **Data Wrangling** ŌĆö Clean and reshape using `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `data.table`.
4. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** ŌĆö Visualize via `ggplot2`, `plotly`, `corrplot`.
5. **Modeling & Inference** ŌĆö Fit statistical/ML models (`lm`, `glm`, `coxph`, `arima`, `randomForest`).
6. **Reporting & Deployment** ŌĆö `rmarkdown`, `shiny`, `plumber` for dashboards and APIs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this structure matters for KTU:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes *Outcome-Based Education (OBE)*. Examiners award marks not just for code, but for demonstrating the **analytical thought process** ŌĆö what package, why that model, and how to interpret output.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Domain | Formula / Equation | Purpose in R Case Study |
|---|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Finance ŌĆö Return | $R_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ or $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right)$ | Daily log-returns; base for volatility |
| 2 | Finance ŌĆö Mean Return | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} R_t$ | Expected return of an asset |
| 3 | Finance ŌĆö Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(R_t - \bar{R})^2$ | Risk measure |
| 4 | Finance ŌĆö Sharpe Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{\bar{R_p} - r_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Risk-adjusted performance |
| 5 | Finance ŌĆö CAPM | $E(R_i) = r_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - r_f)$ | Expected return on asset $i$ |
| 6 | Finance ŌĆö Historical VaR | $VaR_{\alpha} = -\,\text{Quantile}_{\alpha}(\text{Loss distribution})$ | Worst loss at confidence $\alpha$ |
| 7 | Finance ŌĆö Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = w^{\top} \Sigma w$ | Risk of weighted portfolio |
| 8 | Healthcare ŌĆö Kaplan-Meier Survival | $S(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t}\left(1 - \dfrac{d_i}{n_i}\right)$ | Survival probability estimate |
| 9 | Healthcare ŌĆö Hazard Ratio | $HR = \dfrac{h_1(t)}{h_0(t)}$ | Relative risk in Cox model |
| 10 | Healthcare ŌĆö Sensitivity | $\text{Sens} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True positive rate |
| 11 | Healthcare ŌĆö Specificity | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate |
| 12 | Social ŌĆö Chi-square | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | Goodness-of-fit test |
| 13 | Social ŌĆö Cronbach's $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)$ | Internal consistency of survey scale |
| 14 | Social ŌĆö TF-IDF | $\text{tfidf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ | Term importance in text mining |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Conventions:**
> - All financial returns are **dimensionless ratios** (often expressed in %).
> - Survival probabilities $S(t)$ are bounded in $[0, 1]$.
> - $\alpha$ in statistics = significance level (typically **0.05**); in finance (VaR) = confidence level (**0.95** or **0.99**) ŌĆö do **not** confuse them in KTU answers.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Finance**: Hedge funds like **Two Sigma** and **Renaissance** use R alongside Python for back-testing strategies. R's `quantmod` and `PerformanceAnalytics` packages are industry standards.
- **Healthcare**: The **Bioconductor** project (an R-based repository) hosts **2,000+** packages for genomics; FDA accepts R-based submissions for drug trials.
- **Social Sciences**: Used by **Pew Research**, **OECD**, and academia worldwide for reproducible survey analysis.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Case Study A ŌĆö Finance: Portfolio Value-at-Risk (VaR) using R

**Scenario:** An analyst holds a Ōé╣10,00,000 portfolio of two stocks: **Reliance (RELIANCE.NS)** and **TCS (TCS.NS)** with weights $w = (0.6, 0.4)$. Estimate the 1-day **99% Historical VaR** using the last 252 trading days.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Data Ingestion (`quantmod`)**
```r
library(quantmod)
getSymbols(c("RELIANCE.NS", "TCS.NS"), src = "yahoo",
           from = Sys.Date() - 365, auto.assign = TRUE)
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Daily Log-Returns**

$$
R_t = \ln\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right)
$$

```r
ret_RELIANCE <- dailyReturn(RELIANCE.NS, type = "log")
ret_TCS      <- dailyReturn(TCS.NS,      type = "log")
returns      <- merge(ret_RELIANCE, ret_TCS)
colnames(returns) <- c("RELIANCE", "TCS")
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Portfolio Returns**

$$
R_p = w^{\top} R = 0.6 \cdot R_{\text{REL}} + 0.4 \cdot R_{\text{TCS}}
$$

```r
w       <- c(0.6, 0.4)
port_ret <- returns %*% w   # matrix multiplication
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö Estimate 99% Historical VaR**

$$
\text{VaR}_{99\%} = -Q_{0.01}(R_p)
$$

```r
VaR_99 <- -quantile(port_ret, probs = 0.01, na.rm = TRUE)
```

**Step 5 ŌĆö Convert to Monetary Terms**

$$
\text{VaR}_{\text{Ōé╣}} = \text{VaR}_{99\%} \times \text{Portfolio Value}
$$

$$
\text{VaR}_{\text{Ōé╣}} = \text{VaR}_{99\%} \times 10{,}00{,}000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If VaR = 0.025 (i.e., 2.5%), then with 99% confidence, the portfolio will **not** lose more than **Ōé╣25,000** in one day.

---

### 3.2 Case Study B ŌĆö Healthcare: Kaplan-Meier Survival Analysis

**Scenario:** A clinical trial follows **200 patients** post-surgery. Estimate the survival function $S(t)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Survival Object**
```r
library(survival)
surv_obj <- Surv(time = patient_data$surv_time,
                 event = patient_data$status)  # 1 = death, 0 = censored
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Fit Kaplan-Meier**
```r
km_fit <- survfit(surv_obj ~ 1, data = patient_data)
summary(km_fit)
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö KM Estimator Derivation**

At each distinct death time $t_i$:

$$
S(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t}\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)
$$

where:
- $d_i$ = number of deaths at time $t_i$
- $n_i$ = number at risk just before $t_i$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

| Time $t_i$ | At Risk $n_i$ | Deaths $d_i$ | $1 - d_i/n_i$ | $S(t)$ |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|----------------|--------|
| 5 | 200 | 10 | 0.9500 | 0.9500 |
| 10 | 180 | 12 | 0.9333 | $0.9500 \times 0.9333 = 0.8867$ |
| 20 | 150 | 8 | 0.9467 | $0.8867 \times 0.9467 = 0.8395$ |
| 40 | 100 | 15 | 0.8500 | $0.8395 \times 0.8500 = 0.7136$ |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Plot**
```r
plot(km_fit, xlab = "Days", ylab = "Survival Probability S(t)",
     main = "Kaplan-Meier Survival Curve")
```

**Step 5 ŌĆö Cox Proportional Hazards Model**

$$
h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \cdot \exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p)
$$

```r
cox_fit <- coxph(surv_obj ~ age + treatment + stage, data = patient_data)
summary(cox_fit)
```

Interpretation: A coefficient $\beta = 0.7$ for `treatment` gives $HR = e^{0.7} \approx 2.013$ ŌåÆ treatment roughly **doubles** the hazard (interpret with clinical context).

---

### 3.3 Case Study C ŌĆö Social Sciences: Sentiment Analysis of Twitter Data

**Scenario:** Analyze **5,000 tweets** containing `#KeralaElections2025` to classify sentiment as positive, neutral, or negative.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Data Cleaning**
```r
library(tm); library(textstem)
corpus <- VCorpus(VectorSource(tweets$text))
corpus <- tm_map(corpus, content_transformer(tolower))
corpus <- tm_map(corpus, removePunctuation)
corpus <- tm_map(corpus, removeNumbers)
corpus <- tm_map(corpus, removeWords, stopwords("english"))
corpus <- tm_map(corpus, stemDocument)
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Document-Term Matrix & TF-IDF**

$$
\text{tfidf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)
$$

```r
dtm <- DocumentTermMatrix(corpus,
                          control = list(weighting = weightTfIdf))
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Lexicon-based Sentiment Scoring** (e.g., `tidytext` Bing lexicon)
```r
library(tidytext)
bing <- get_sentiments("bing")
tokens <- tweets %>% unnest_tokens(word, text)
sent <- tokens %>% inner_join(bing, by = "word") %>%
        count(sentiment) %>% mutate(prop = n/sum(n))
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö Visualize**
```r
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(sent, aes(sentiment, prop, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col() + theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Sentiment Distribution: #KeralaElections2025")
```

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Finance | Healthcare | Social Sciences |
|-----------|---------|------------|-----------------|
| Data Type | Numeric, time-series | Numeric + censored | Text + categorical + numeric |
| Key R Packages | `quantmod`, `PerformanceAnalytics`, `TTR` | `survival`, `survminer`, `bioconductor-*` | `tm`, `quanteda`, `tidytext`, `survey` |
| Primary Model | ARIMA, GARCH, CAPM | Cox PH, Logistic Regression | Chi-square, Regression, SEM |

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*List any three R packages commonly used in financial data analysis and state their purpose.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **`quantmod`** ŌĆö A package for quantitative financial modeling. It provides functions to download financial data (`getSymbols()`), compute returns (`dailyReturn()`), and visualize with OHLC/candlestick charts. *(1 mark)*
2. **`PerformanceAnalytics`** ŌĆö Used for performance and risk analysis of financial portfolios. Includes functions for Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, drawdown analysis, and VaR. *(1 mark)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*What is the Kaplan-Meier estimator in survival analysis? Write its mathematical form.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Kaplan-Meier (KM) estimator is a **non-parametric** statistic used to estimate the survival function $S(t)$ from lifetime data. It is particularly useful when data are **right-censored** (i.e., some subjects have not yet experienced the event by the end of the study). *(1 mark)*

**Mathematical form:**

$$
\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t}\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)
$$

where $t_i$ are the distinct event times, $d_i$ is the number of events (e.g., deaths) at $t_i$, and $n_i$ is the number of subjects at risk just before $t_i$. *(2 marks)*

In R, it is implemented in the `survival` package via `survfit()` and visualized with `ggsurvplot()` from the `survminer` package.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Finance Focus**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Explain how R is used in **portfolio risk management**. Discuss the **Historical Value-at-Risk (VaR)** approach with relevant R code. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the **Sharpe Ratio** and **CAPM**. With a suitable R code snippet, show how to compute and interpret the Sharpe Ratio for a portfolio of two stocks. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) R in Portfolio Risk Management & Historical VaR (7 marks):**

Portfolio risk management involves identifying, measuring, and mitigating financial risks (market, credit, liquidity). R provides an integrated environment for these tasks through packages like `quantmod`, `PerformanceAnalytics`, and `rugarch`. *(1 mark)*

**Historical VaR** estimates the maximum expected loss of a portfolio over a given time horizon at a specified confidence level, using past returns. It is the simplest non-parametric VaR method. *(1 mark)*

**Formula:**

$$
\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = -Q_{1-\alpha}(R_p)
$$

where $Q_{1-\alpha}$ is the $(1-\alpha)$-quantile of the historical return distribution. *(1 mark)*

**R Code (3 marks):**
```r
library(quantmod); library(PerformanceAnalytics)
getSymbols(c("AAPL", "MSFT"), src = "yahoo",
           from = "2022-01-01", to = Sys.Date())
ret <- merge(dailyReturn(AAPL), dailyReturn(MSFT))
w   <- c(0.5, 0.5)
port_ret <- Return.portfolio(ret, weights = w)
VaR_99 <- VaR(port_ret, p = 0.99, method = "historical")
print(VaR_99)
```
The output is a negative number; e.g., **VaR = -0.022** means there is a 1% chance the portfolio loses more than **2.2%** in one day. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Sharpe Ratio & CAPM (7 marks):**

**Sharpe Ratio** measures the excess return per unit of total risk (volatility):

$$
SR = \frac{\bar{R_p} - r_f}{\sigma_p}
$$

A higher Sharpe Ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. *(1 mark)*

**CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model)** describes the relationship between systematic risk and expected return:

$$
E(R_i) = r_f + \beta_i \left[E(R_m) - r_f\right]
$$

where $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$. *(1 mark)*

**R Code (5 marks):**
```r
# Assume we already have ret (AAPL, MSFT) and port_ret
rf <- 0.0001  # daily risk-free rate ~ 2.5% annual
sharpe <- SharpeRatio(port_ret, Rf = rf, FUN = "StdDev")
sharpe_annual <- sharpe * sqrt(252)
print(sharpe_annual)

# CAPM
beta_aapl <- CAPM.beta(AAPL_returns, MSFT_returns)
cat("Beta of AAPL w.r.t MSFT:", round(beta_aapl, 3), "\n")
expected_return <- rf + beta_aapl * (mean(MSFT_returns) - rf)
cat("Expected return (CAPM):", round(expected_return * 252 * 100, 2), "%\n")
```

**Interpretation example:** If $SR_{\text{annual}} = 1.5$, the portfolio delivers 1.5 units of excess return per unit of risk ŌĆö considered **good**. If $\beta = 1.2$, the stock is **20% more volatile** than the market. *(0 marks for interpretation, awarded under code explanation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Healthcare & Social Sciences Focus**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* With a suitable case study, explain how R is used in **clinical trial survival analysis**. Write R code to fit a **Kaplan-Meier** and a **Cox Proportional Hazards** model. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the application of R in **social science research** for analyzing **survey data** and **text/sentiment analysis**. Provide illustrative R snippets. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) R in Clinical Trial Survival Analysis (7 marks):**

Survival analysis studies the time until an event of interest occurs (death, relapse, recovery). Clinical trials commonly deal with **right-censored** data where some patients are lost to follow-up. *(1 mark)*

**Case Study:** A phase-III oncology trial on **200 lung-cancer patients** comparing a new drug vs. standard care, with covariates: age, treatment, tumor stage. *(1 mark)*

**Kaplan-Meier (non-parametric)** ŌĆö estimates $S(t)$:

$$
\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t}\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)
$$

**Cox PH (semi-parametric)** ŌĆö models hazard:

$$
h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \cdot \exp(\beta^{\top} X)
$$

*(1 mark for formulas)*

**R Code (4 marks):**
```r
library(survival); library(survminer)
data(lung)  # built-in dataset

# Kaplan-Meier by treatment
km <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung)
ggsurvplot(km, data = lung, pval = TRUE, risk.table = TRUE,
           title = "KM Curve by Sex")

# Cox Proportional Hazards
cox <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog, data = lung)
summary(cox)
ggforest(cox, data = lung)
```

**Interpretation:** A hazard ratio $HR = 1.03$ for `age` means each additional year of age increases the hazard of death by **3%**, holding other variables constant. *(in code commentary, 0 separate marks)*

---

**(b) R in Social Science ŌĆö Survey & Text Analysis (7 marks):**

**Survey Data Analysis:** Social scientists collect questionnaire data on attitudes, demographics, and behaviors. R's `survey` package handles **complex survey designs** (stratified, clustered, weighted). *(1 mark)*

```r
library(survey)
design <- svydesign(ids = ~district, strata = ~state,
                    weights = ~wt, data = kerala_survey)
svymean(~ literacy_rate, design)
svychisq(~vote_choice ~ age_group, design)
```
*(2 marks)*

**Text/Sentiment Analysis:** With the explosion of social media, R's `tidytext`, `tm`, and `quanteda` are used to mine tweets, reviews, and news. *(1 mark)*

**TF-IDF formula:**

$$
\text{tfidf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)
$$

*(1 mark)*

**R Code (2 marks):**
```r
library(tidytext); library(dplyr)
bing <- get_sentiments("bing")
tweets %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text) %>%
  inner_join(bing, by = "word") %>%
  count(sentiment) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(sentiment, prop, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col() + theme_minimal()
```

**Outcome:** Researchers quantify public mood, identify misinformation patterns, and correlate sentiment with real-world events (elections, pandemics, market movements). *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ in Finance vs. Statistics** ŌĆö In VaR, $\alpha = 99\%$ is the *confidence*; in a t-test, $\alpha = 0.05$ is the *significance level*. Mixing these loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to set `weights` in `svydesign()`** ŌĆö A common mistake in survey analysis. If your sample is weighted, omitting weights gives **biased estimates**, and the examiner will deduct 1 mark for "methodological error".
> 3. **Not handling `NA`s in `coxph()`** ŌĆö Always check `summary(df)` and use `na.omit()` or imputation. A Cox model run on raw data with NAs throws an error and **0 marks** for output.
> 4. **Confusing `quantile(prob=0.01)` for VaR** ŌĆö Remember the negative sign! VaR is conventionally reported as a *positive loss number*.
> 5. **Writing Python code in R exam** ŌĆö Even if you know Python, **KTU strictly evaluates R syntax**. A `pandas` snippet will be marked **zero**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Finance in R** ŌåÆ packages: `quantmod`, `PerformanceAnalytics`, `TTR`, `rugarch`. Core tasks: returns, VaR, Sharpe, CAPM, GARCH.
- **Healthcare in R** ŌåÆ packages: `survival`, `survminer`, `Bioconductor`. Core tasks: Kaplan-Meier, Cox PH, log-rank test, ROC curves.
- **Social Sciences in R** ŌåÆ packages: `survey`, `tidytext`, `tm`, `quanteda`, `psych`. Core tasks: weighted survey analysis, sentiment mining, chi-square, reliability tests.
- **R Markdown / Quarto** enables **reproducible research** ŌĆö a key advantage in regulated industries.
- The **six-stage pipeline** (Problem ŌåÆ Data ŌåÆ Wrangle ŌåÆ EDA ŌåÆ Model ŌåÆ Report) is the **universal framework** for any R case study.
- **Always** interpret statistical output in domain context ŌĆö VaR in Ōé╣, $HR$ in clinical terms, $\chi^2$ with degrees of freedom.
- **Formulae to memorize cold for KTU:**
  - Sharpe: $SR = (\bar{R_p} - r_f)/\sigma_p$
  - CAPM: $E(R_i) = r_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - r_f)$
  - KM: $\hat{S}(t) = \prod(1 - d_i/n_i)$
  - Cox PH: $h(t\mid X) = h_0(t)\exp(\beta^{\top}X)$
  - TF-IDF: $\text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log(N/\text{df}(t))$
- **Datasets built into R** for practice: `EuStockMarkets`, `lung`, `anes`, `airquality` ŌĆö use them in your lab records to score easy marks.
- **Shiny apps** for deploying case studies as interactive dashboards ŌĆö bonus point territory in KTU lab evaluations.
